Aluno
Travel English -- 48 Aulas

Danielle Moreira

Business English para People & Culture, comunicação executiva e a liderança da divisão do Canadá

B2 (Intermediário-Superior) Trabalho híbrido Gerente de People & Culture — Maple Bear 60 min · Online
Progresso Geral0%
Velocidade:
Lesson 01 -- Pre-class

Diagnostic Session -- Who Is Danielle in English?

Executive self-positioning in People & Culture: say who you are, what your role involves and what you lead — in the senior register the Canadian team expects. Key words: organizational culture, employee journey, people strategy, framework, stakeholder, alignment, to roll out, headquarters, franchisor, bilingual education, hybrid model, senior leadership, to make headway, to take a stance, to carry out. Structures: present simple (permanent role) vs present continuous (in progress) vs present perfect continuous (for/since).
0%
▼

Stage 1.1: Vocabulary Cards

Vocabulary

Listen to each term and read the example. These are the words you already use every day — now in the register the Canadian team uses.

Organizational culture -- the shared values and behaviors that decide how work really happens
"Organizational culture is what people do when nobody is measuring them."
Employee journey -- every stage a person goes through, from the first interview to the last day
"The employee journey starts long before the first day of work."
People strategy -- the long term plan for how a company attracts, grows and keeps its people
"Our people strategy has three pillars, and culture is the first one."
Framework -- a structured model you use to organize and explain your approach
"I don't bring answers, I bring a framework."
Stakeholder -- anyone with a real interest in the outcome of your work
"Every stakeholder wants to know what changes for them."
Alignment -- the point where leadership, strategy and culture pull in the same direction
"Alignment is when leadership says one thing and the team lives the same thing."
To roll out -- to launch something across a whole organization, step by step
"We're rolling out the new employee journey across every region."
Headquarters -- the main office, where the senior decisions are made
"Headquarters owns the framework, but each market owns its own culture."
Franchisor -- the company that licenses its brand and its model to local operators
"Maple Bear is a franchisor: we license the model, we don't run the schools."
Bilingual education -- teaching a full curriculum in two languages
"We work in bilingual education, from kindergarten to high school."
Hybrid model -- a way of working that mixes days at the office and days at home
"We're still learning how to build culture inside a hybrid model."
Senior leadership -- the executives who set the direction and own the decisions
"Senior leadership sets the direction, but the middle managers set the tone."
To make headway -- to make real progress on something difficult
"We're making headway on the hybrid model."
To take a stance -- to state a clear position and defend it
"Take a stance, but leave room for their input."
To carry out -- to conduct something you planned: research, an assessment, a review
"Before I propose anything, I want to carry out a cultural assessment."

Stage 1.2: Matching

Practice

Match each term with the correct definition.

Organizational culture
Employee journey
People strategy
Framework
Stakeholder
Alignment
To roll out
Headquarters
Franchisor
Bilingual education
Hybrid model
Senior leadership
To make headway
To take a stance
To carry out

Stage 1.3: Grammar in Context

GRAMMAR

Read the text and answer the questions.

Danielle leads People and Culture at Maple Bear, a franchisor in bilingual education. Her role involves the employee journey — every stage, from the first interview to the last day — and the people strategy behind it. She works closely with senior leadership, and she reports to the Chief People Officer at headquarters. "I have been leading the culture transformation since 2023," she says, "and I have been working in People and Culture for eight years." She has carried out four cultural assessments, and she has built a framework that every stakeholder can read. Right now the picture is different: at the moment, her team is rolling out the new employee journey across every region, and this year she is preparing to take on Canada. They are making headway on the hybrid model, although alignment between leadership and the schools is still the hardest part. In August she will meet the Canadian academic team — and, before she proposes anything, she wants to carry out a cultural assessment and take a stance of her own.

1. Why do we say "I have been leading the culture transformation since 2023" and not "I am leading it since 2023"?
A Because it started in the past and is still going now — present perfect continuous with since.
B Because the action is over and has no link to the present.
C Because it is a permanent part of her role.
2. "At the moment, her team is rolling out the new employee journey" uses the present continuous because:
A It is a permanent responsibility of the role.
B It is in progress right now, temporary — not the permanent state of things.
C It is a finished result, already counted.
3. Which sentence about her permanent role is correct?
A "She is leading People and Culture since eight years."
B "She leads People and Culture, and her role involves the employee journey."
C "She has been leading People and Culture in 2023."

Stage 1.4: Grammar Tip -- Present Simple vs Present Continuous vs Present Perfect (Continuous)

GRAMMAR

The three forms that carry your professional story — the ones the Canadian team will hear in your first two minutes.

FormUseExample
Present Simple
I / we + verb (she + s)		Permanent role and what it involves.I lead People and Culture.
Present Continuous
am / is / are + -ing		In progress right now, temporary (at the moment, this year).We are rolling out the framework.
Present Perfect Continuous
have been + -ing		Started in the past and is still going — with for / since.I have been leading it since 2023.
Present Perfect
have + past participle		A finished, countable result, with no time said.I have carried out four assessments.
Negativehave / has + not + past participle. In speech, contracted: haven't.We haven't rolled it out in Canada yet.
QuestionHow long + have + subject + been + -ing?How long have you been leading this?
for vs sincefor + a period (for eight years) · since + a starting point (since 2023)

Watch out (the classic senior slip): duration up to now never takes the present simple or the present continuous. "I am leading this transformation since 2023" is wrong — the correct form is "I have been leading this transformation since 2023" (in speech, I've been leading). Nothing makes you sound junior faster than this one.

Collocation (the other slip): we do not say "make a research" or "make an assessment" — we say carry out research / carry out an assessment. And it is not "responsible by", it is responsible for.

Stage 1.5: Fill in the Blank

Practice

Complete each sentence with the correct form. Tap Listen to hear the full sentence.

"I People and Culture at Maple Bear."
"At the moment, we the new employee journey."
"I the culture transformation since 2023."
"I four cultural assessments."
"I have been working in People and Culture eight years."
"I have been leading the culture transformation 2023."

Stage 2: Put the First Call in Order

Order

Put the steps of your first conversation with the Canadian leadership in the correct order.

?Take a stance: say what you bring and what you don't bring.
?Say what your role involves: the employee journey and the people strategy.
?Propose the first step: carry out a cultural assessment with each leader.
?Introduce yourself: who you are and what you lead.
?Explain how long you've been leading the culture transformation.

Stage 3: Pronunciation

Speaking

Listen to each sentence, then record yourself saying it. These are the five sentences that open any conversation of yours in Canada.

I lead People and Culture at Maple Bear, a franchisor in bilingual education.
My role involves the employee journey and the people strategy behind it.
I've been leading the culture transformation since 2023.
At the moment, we're rolling out the framework across every region.
Before I propose anything, I want to carry out a cultural assessment.

Stage 4: Situational Quiz

Quiz

Choose the best answer for each real moment of your first week in Canada.

A Canadian director asks: "So, what do you do?" You answer:
A "I work with the people in the company."
B "I lead People and Culture at Maple Bear, and my role involves the employee journey and the people strategy behind it."
C "I am responsible by the culture things in the company."
He asks: "How long have you been doing this?" The best answer is:
A "I am leading the culture transformation since 2023."
B "I've been leading the culture transformation since 2023."
C "I lead the culture transformation for three years."
You want to describe what your team is doing right now, this quarter. Which is correct?
A "At the moment, we're rolling out the new employee journey across every region."
B "At the moment, we roll out the new employee journey across every region."
C "At the moment, we have rolled out the new employee journey since every region."
You want to say you will run a cultural diagnosis before proposing anything. The natural collocation is:
A "I want to make a research with the leaders."
B "I want to carry out a cultural assessment with the leaders."
C "I want to do a culture check in the leaders."
An academic director challenges your framework in front of the team. The most senior move is:
A Apologize, say your English isn't good enough, and move on.
B "That's a fair challenge. To some extent, I agree — but let me take a stance: the framework is a starting point, not an answer."
C Agree with everything to avoid conflict in the first meeting.

Stage 5: Free Production

Reflection

Record yourself answering the question below. There is no right or wrong answer — speak for two or three minutes, with no script. This recording is your baseline for the programme.

You've just joined a video call with two Canadian directors you have never met. Introduce yourself: your role (present simple), what you're rolling out right now (present continuous), how long you've been leading the culture transformation (present perfect continuous, for/since), and what you want to carry out in your first thirty days in Canada. Take your time, and don't read from a script.

Survival Card -- Lesson 1

1I lead People and Culture at Maple Bear, a franchisor in bilingual education.
2My role involves the employee journey and the people strategy behind it.
3I've been leading the culture transformation since 2023.
4At the moment, we're rolling out the framework across every region.
5Before I propose anything, I want to carry out a cultural assessment.
Lesson 02 -- Pre-class

The Language of Culture -- Diagnosing and Describing Culture

Diagnosing and describing organizational culture in English: read the numbers, name the problem and say the hard truth without losing the room. Key words: cultural diagnosis, core values, psychological safety, onboarding experience, culture fit, belonging, disengagement, attrition, turnover rate, toxic culture, exit interview, pulse survey, to foster a culture of, to look into, the elephant in the room. Structures: third conditional (if + had + past participle / would have + past participle) and modal perfects (should have / could have / might have), plus executive hedging (It would appear that... / To some extent...).
0%
▼

Stage 1.1: Vocabulary Cards

Vocabulary

Listen to each term and read the example. This is the vocabulary a cultural diagnosis is written in — what separates an opinion about culture from a professional reading of it.

Cultural diagnosis -- a structured reading of what a culture really is, based on evidence
"Before I propose anything, I would like to run a cultural diagnosis."
Core values -- the few principles a company will not trade away, even under pressure
"Core values are not what is on the wall. They are what survives a hard decision."
Psychological safety -- the belief that you can speak up or disagree without being punished
"Psychological safety is built in the meeting where someone disagrees with you."
Onboarding experience -- the first weeks of a new hire, and how those weeks actually feel
"The onboarding experience was excellent for one week, and abandoned by the second."
Culture fit -- how well a person's way of working matches the way the team already works
"We hire for culture fit, and then we punish the first person who thinks differently."
Belonging -- the feeling that you can be yourself at work and still be part of the team
"Belonging is high here. Safety is not. Those are two different problems."
Disengagement -- when people still do the job but stop caring about the outcome
"Disengagement is quiet. It is the last thing you see and the first thing that happens."
Attrition -- the slow loss of people over time, one by one
"Attrition never arrives as a crisis. It arrives as a resignation, and then another one."
Turnover rate -- the percentage of people who leave the company in a year
"Our turnover rate climbed from nine percent to twenty six percent in three years."
Toxic culture -- a culture where fear, blame or disrespect have become normal
"It is not a toxic culture. It is a specific problem that nobody names."
Exit interview -- the conversation with someone who is leaving, where the truth comes out
"They filed the exit interviews. Nobody ever read them."
Pulse survey -- a short, frequent survey that measures how people feel right now
"If they had run a pulse survey that year, they would have seen it coming."
To foster a culture of -- to create the conditions for a behavior to grow
"You foster a culture of speaking up by protecting the first person who does it."
To look into -- to investigate something properly before you judge it
"Before we judge the team, we should look into what happened in that meeting."
The elephant in the room -- the obvious problem everyone can see and nobody names
"Let me name the elephant in the room: nobody here disagrees with a director."

Stage 1.2: Matching

Practice

Match each term with the correct definition.

Cultural diagnosis
Core values
Psychological safety
Onboarding experience
Culture fit
Belonging
Disengagement
Attrition
Turnover rate
Toxic culture
Exit interview
Pulse survey
To foster a culture of
To look into
The elephant in the room

Stage 1.3: Grammar in Context

GRAMMAR

Read the text and answer the questions.

Northline Academy was a network of bilingual schools with an excellent reputation and a serious problem. In three years, its turnover rate climbed from nine percent to twenty six percent. The company had written a beautiful set of core values, printed them and hung them in every corridor — and it had never once run a cultural diagnosis to find out whether anyone lived by them. The onboarding experience was excellent for one week and abandoned by the second. Managers stopped disagreeing with directors in meetings. The people who stayed delivered the curriculum and quietly stopped bringing new ideas: that is disengagement, and it is far more expensive than the attrition it produces. When the board finally asked what had gone wrong, the answer was uncomfortable. If leadership had run a pulse survey in that first year, they would have seen psychological safety collapsing. If someone had looked into the exit interviews instead of filing them, they would have found the elephant in the room by the second one. They should have addressed it during onboarding, and they could have fostered a culture of speaking up at almost no cost. The two coordinators who resigned might not have left if the culture had felt safe. The culture did not fail because it was a toxic culture. It failed because nobody was willing to name what everybody could already see.

1. Why does "If leadership had run a pulse survey, they would have seen it" use had run and not would have run?
A Because the if half always takes had + past participle — would belongs only in the result half.
B Because the survey was run, only too late.
C Because the action can still happen in the future.
2. "The two coordinators might not have left" — why might and not would?
A Because it is a direct criticism of leadership.
B Because it speculates carefully: it was likely, but nobody can state it as a certainty.
C Because the fact happened and is confirmed.
3. According to the text, what came FIRST at Northline Academy?
A The attrition — people resigned, and only then did they stop caring.
B The disengagement — people stopped bringing ideas long before anyone resigned.
C The toxic culture — the text says fear and blame were normal from day one.

Stage 1.4: Grammar Tip -- Third Conditional + Modal Perfects

GRAMMAR

The grammar of the diagnosis: every cultural diagnosis talks about a past that did NOT happen — "if they had done X, Y would not have happened".

FormUseExample
Condition (if)
if + had + past participle		The past that did NOT happen.If they had run a pulse survey...
Result
would have + past participle		The consequence that, for that reason, did not happen either....they would have seen it coming.
should have + past participleCriticism: it was an obligation and it was not done. Judges.We should have addressed it earlier.
could have + past participleAn option that was there and was not taken. Softer.They could have fostered a culture of speaking up.
might have + past participleCareful speculation, with no accusation. Speculates.The team might have felt unsafe.
Negativehad not + past participle / would not have + past participleIf they hadn't ignored it, she wouldn't have resigned.
QuestionWhat + would + subject + have + past participle + if...?What would you have done if you had known?
Executive hedgingIt would appear that... · To some extent... — the same truth, said in a way the room is willing to hear.

Watch out (the classic slip): would NEVER goes in the if half. "If we would have known" is wrong — the correct form is "If we had known, we would have acted". In speech, everything contracts: If we'd known, we'd have acted.

In writing: it is always should have, never "should of" — even though, at native speed, the two sound exactly the same.

Collocation: in English we do not say "do a diagnosis" — we run / carry out / conduct a cultural diagnosis. And it is discuss the results, never "discuss about the results".

Stage 1.5: Fill in the Blank

Practice

Complete each sentence with the correct form. Tap Listen to hear the full sentence.

"If leadership a pulse survey, they would have seen it coming."
"If someone had read the exit interviews, they the problem."
"We the disengagement in the first quarter."
"The coordinators if psychological safety had been higher."
"They a culture of speaking up."
"If the onboarding experience after week one, she would have stayed."

Stage 2: Put the Diagnosis in Order

Order

Put the steps of a cultural diagnosis in the order you will run it in Canada.

?Name the elephant in the room, with the number that proves it.
?Look into the evidence first: the pulse survey and the exit interviews.
?Ask the team what it would take to foster a culture of speaking up.
?Read the two numbers that contradict each other: belonging and psychological safety.
?Say what the company would have known if someone had read those interviews.

Stage 3: Pronunciation

Speaking

Listen to each sentence, then record yourself saying it. These are the five sentences of your first cultural diagnosis in Canada.

Before I propose anything, I would like to run a cultural diagnosis.
It would appear that the issue is psychological safety, not belonging.
If someone had looked into the exit interviews, we would have known a year ago.
To some extent, the team might have felt that disagreeing carried a cost.
Let me name the elephant in the room, and you can tell me if I am wrong.

Stage 4: Situational Quiz

Quiz

Choose the best answer for each real moment of your first diagnosis session with the academic team.

The pulse survey shows belonging at 78% and psychological safety at 41%. Nathan asks what it means. You say:
A "Your culture is toxic. People are afraid of each other."
B "It would appear that the issue is safety, not belonging: people are proud to be here, and they still do not feel safe enough to disagree."
C "The numbers are fine. Forty one percent is normal in academia."
Nine exit interviews were filed and never read. Which sentence is grammatically correct?
A "If someone would have read them, we would have known."
B "If someone had read them, we would have known."
C "If someone had read them, we would know it a year ago."
You want to criticize a decision the directors made, without turning them into the accused. The most senior form is:
A "You should have seen this coming. It was obvious."
B "To some extent, the team might have felt that disagreeing carried a cost — that is a system problem, not a people problem."
C "Nobody here has the courage to say what they think."
You want to say you will investigate the numbers properly before judging anyone. The natural collocation is:
A "Before I take a stance, I want to look into the exit interviews."
B "Before I take a stance, I want to look the exit interviews."
C "Before I take a stance, I want to do a research in the exit interviews."
A director says: "Nobody ever complained to me, so the survey must be wrong." You answer:
A "Then the survey is wrong. Let's move on."
B "That is exactly the problem with this leadership team."
C "To some extent, that is the finding: people might not have felt they could bring it to you. Could you tell me what happens here when someone disagrees with a director?"

Stage 5: Free Production

Reflection

Record yourself answering the question below. Speak for two or three minutes, with no script.

Think of a real cultural challenge you saw or led at Maple Bear Brazil. Walk me through it: what happened, what you did, and — with what you know now — what you would have done differently if you had known then what you know today. Use at least two third conditionals, one modal perfect (should have / could have / might have), and five words from this lesson.

Survival Card -- Lesson 2

1Before I propose anything, I would like to run a cultural diagnosis.
2It would appear that the issue is psychological safety, not belonging.
3If someone had looked into the exit interviews, we would have known a year ago.
4To some extent, the team might have felt that disagreeing carried a cost.
5Let me name the elephant in the room, and you can tell me if I am wrong.
Lesson 03 -- Pre-class

Speaking Like a Senior -- Executive Communication in 1:1s, OKRs and Strategic Alignment

Run a 1:1, read an OKR dashboard and lead a strategic alignment meeting in English: open with the number, put a proposal forward, disagree with a senior leader and close with one owner and one date. Key words: OKR, key performance indicator, cascading goals, deliverable, milestone, accountability, cross-functional, bandwidth, buy-in, to put forward, to push back, to move the needle, to cut to the chase, to bring about, to circle back to. Structure: mixed conditionals (a past cause with a cost today / a present condition with a past result) + executive discourse markers (Building on that... / My take on this is... / What I would like to propose is...).
0%
▼

Stage 1.1: Vocabulary Cards

Vocabulary

Listen to each term and read the example. This is the vocabulary your Canadian peers already use every day — what separates the person who reports the meeting from the person who runs it.

OKR -- one goal you can say out loud, plus the numbers that prove you reached it
"Our first OKR for Canada is one objective and three numbers. Nothing else."
Key performance indicator -- a number you watch all year to see whether the business is healthy
"Engagement is the key performance indicator I will be judged on in Canada."
Cascading goals -- goals that flow down: the company sets one, and every team writes its own version
"Without cascading goals, nine academic teams will write nine different strategies."
Deliverable -- the concrete thing you promised to hand over, on a date
"The onboarding journey is not an idea. It is a deliverable, and it has a date."
Milestone -- a checkpoint with a date on the way to the goal
"Give me one milestone, one owner and one date. That is the whole meeting."
Accountability -- owning the result in public, whether it worked or not
"A team that agrees with everything I say is not accountability. It is silence with good manners."
Cross-functional -- involving people from different teams who do not report to each other
"The group is cross-functional, which is exactly why nobody owns the date."
Bandwidth -- the time and attention a person or a team actually has left
"They do not lack commitment. They lack bandwidth, and those are different problems."
Buy-in -- the moment people stop obeying a plan and start wanting it
"Their buy-in is not a nice-to-have. It is the deliverable."
To put forward -- to formally offer an idea so that a group can decide on it
"What I would like to put forward is one owner and one date."
To push back -- to disagree with a plan, professionally, and say so out loud
"Please push back if you think the target is wrong. I would rather hear it now."
To move the needle -- to produce a change big enough to show up in the numbers
"Tell me which single milestone would actually move the needle this quarter."
To cut to the chase -- to give the point first and the story afterwards
"Let me cut to the chase: two of the four objectives are off track."
To bring about -- to cause a change, not merely to want one
"This is the one change that would bring about real accountability."
To circle back to -- to return, on purpose, to a point you left open
"Before we close, I would like to circle back to the onboarding deliverable."

Stage 1.2: Matching

Practice

Match each term with the correct definition.

OKR
Key performance indicator
Cascading goals
Deliverable
Milestone
Accountability
Cross-functional
Bandwidth
Buy-in
To put forward
To push back
To move the needle
To cut to the chase
To bring about
To circle back to

Stage 1.3: Grammar in Context

GRAMMAR

Read the text and answer the questions.

It is the last week of the quarter at Maple Bear Canada, and the OKR dashboard is not kind. Two of the four objectives are off track. The onboarding deliverable slipped by six weeks; the cross-functional group that was supposed to own it has met exactly twice; and the engagement key performance indicator is still marked as not measured, because there is nothing live to measure. If someone had owned that deliverable in the first quarter, the team would not be having this conversation today. If the goals had cascaded across the nine academic teams last year, every one of them would have its own version of the objective right now, instead of nine different interpretations of it.

The directors blame bandwidth. Claire, the VP, does not. Her reading is colder and more useful: nobody owns the date, so nobody carries the accountability for it, and a milestone without an owner is a wish with a calendar. What she wants on Monday is not a status report. She wants the one milestone that would actually move the needle, and a proposal to reach it. And she wants Danielle to push back on her if the target itself is wrong — because half the leadership team agrees with everything she says, and that is not accountability. That is silence with good manners. If Claire were not the kind of leader who asks to be contradicted, she would have lost this team a long time ago.

So Danielle opens with the number, not the story. She cuts to the chase: two of four, six weeks late, no owner. Then she puts forward one owner, one milestone and one date — September fifteenth. The academic directors will resist, and she wants them to resist in the room rather than after it, because their buy-in is not a nice-to-have: it is the deliverable itself. That single change, she argues, is what would bring about real accountability. Before she closes, she circles back to the engagement number, and names the week it will finally be measured.

1. Why does "If someone had owned that deliverable, we would not be having this conversation today" use would not be having and not would not have had?
A Because the cause is in the past, but the result is now — and a present result takes would + verb, never would have.
B Because both halves of the sentence are in the past.
C Because the conversation can still happen in the future.
2. "If Claire were not the kind of leader who asks to be contradicted, she would have lost this team a long time ago." Which direction is this?
A A past cause with a present result.
B A present condition (this is how she is, permanently) with a past result — hence were + would have lost.
C A real condition about the future.
3. According to the text, what is the real reason the onboarding deliverable is late?
A The cross-functional group does not have the bandwidth to do the work.
B Nobody owns the date, so nobody carries the accountability for it.
C The engagement KPI was measured too late in the quarter.

Stage 1.4: Grammar Tip -- Mixed Conditionals

GRAMMAR

The grammar of executive communication: the two halves of the sentence sit in different tenses, because the decision belongs to one time and the cost belongs to another.

FormUseExample
Direction 1 — condition
if + had + past participle		The decision that was NOT taken, back in the past. The past cause.If we had shipped it in Q1...
Direction 1 — result
would + base verb		The cost you feel today. The present result....we would be measuring engagement right now.
Direction 2 — condition
if + past simple / were		A permanent limit of the present. The present cause.If I were not leading two countries...
Direction 2 — result
would have + past participle		The consequence that is already closed, in the past. The past result....I would have delivered the diagnostic in July.
Negativehad not + past participle / would not be + -ingIf nobody had owned it, we wouldn't be here today.
QuestionWhere + would + subject + be + -ing + if...?Where would we be today if we had shipped it in Q1?
Discourse markersBuilding on that... (add to what was just said) · My take on this is... (give your own reading) · What I would like to propose is... (open a proposal) · To circle back to... (return to an open point)

Watch out (the mixed-conditional slip): if the result is happening now, the result half is would be — never "would have been". "If we had launched in Q1, we would have been hitting our KPI now" is wrong; the correct form is "we would be hitting our KPI now".

Watch out (the classic slip): would NEVER goes into the if half. "If I would be less busy" is wrong — the correct form is "If I were less busy". And in formal register it is always if I were, never "if I was".

Collocation: in English it is align on priorities (never "align in"), reach a milestone (never "make a milestone"), and explain something to someone (never "explain me the OKR").

Stage 1.5: Fill in the Blank

Practice

Complete each sentence with the correct form. Tap Listen to hear the full sentence.

"If we had shipped the deliverable in the first quarter, we engagement right now."
"If the goals last year, every academic team would have its own OKR today."
"If I leading two countries, I would have delivered the diagnostic in July."
"If the group had more bandwidth, they the milestone in June."
"If someone that deliverable, we would not be having this conversation today."
"What I would like to is one owner, one milestone and one date."

Stage 2: Put the 1:1 in Order

Order

Put the five moves of an executive 1:1 in the order you will run them in Canada.

?Put forward one owner, one milestone and one date.
?Cut to the chase: open with the number, not with the story.
?Circle back to the engagement KPI and close with the date it will be measured.
?Say what the delay is costing you today: if it had shipped in Q1, you would be measuring engagement now.
?Invite them to push back on the date, in the room, before anyone leaves it.

Stage 3: Pronunciation

Speaking

Listen to each sentence, then record yourself saying it. These are the five sentences of your first 1:1 with each Canadian leader.

Let me cut to the chase: two of the four objectives are off track.
If we had shipped that deliverable in Q1, we would be measuring engagement right now.
What I would like to put forward is one owner, one milestone and one date.
Building on that, my take on this is different, and here is the number behind it.
Please push back if you think the target is wrong. I would rather hear it now.

Stage 4: Situational Quiz

Quiz

Choose the best answer for each real moment of your first 1:1 and of the strategic alignment meeting.

Nathan opens the 1:1 with: "Quick sync. Where are we on the onboarding milestone?" You say:
A "Well, it is a long story. In March the group was formed, and then we had some issues with the schedule, and after that..."
B "Let me cut to the chase: it slipped six weeks, nobody owns the date, and I have a proposal."
C "Sorry, we are a bit late with that one. I will send you an update by email."
You want to say that a decision from Q1 is still costing the company today. Which sentence is correct?
A "If we had shipped it in Q1, we would have been measuring engagement now."
B "If we had shipped it in Q1, we would be measuring engagement right now."
C "If we would have shipped it in Q1, we would be measuring engagement right now."
Claire, the VP, says September is unrealistic and asks for December. You disagree. The most senior answer is:
A "You are wrong. December makes no sense at all."
B "Okay, December is fine. I will adjust the plan."
C "Building on that, my take on this is different: if we moved it to December, we would lose the buy-in we already have — and here is the number behind that."
The cross-functional group says it has no bandwidth. What is the diagnosis a senior leader gives?
A "Then bandwidth is not the problem. Nobody owns the date, so nobody is accountable for it."
B "Then we should remove the milestone from the OKR."
C "Then the group is not committed enough to this project."
You are closing the alignment meeting and want to return to a point you deliberately left open. You say:
A "Before we close, I want to explain you the engagement KPI one more time."
B "Before we close, I would like to circle back to the engagement KPI and name the week it gets measured."
C "Before we close, let's align in the priorities of the engagement KPI."

Stage 5: Free Production

Reflection

Record yourself answering the question below. Speak for two or three minutes, with no script.

You are opening the strategic alignment meeting with the Canadian leadership on August 1st. Three items: where the culture initiative stands, the onboarding milestone, and your proposal for the diagnostic with the academic team. Open with the number, put forward one owner and one date, and say what the delay is costing you today. Use at least two mixed conditionals and five words from this lesson.

Survival Card -- Lesson 3

1Let me cut to the chase: two of the four objectives are off track.
2If we had shipped that deliverable in Q1, we would be measuring engagement right now.
3What I would like to put forward is one owner, one milestone and one date.
4Building on that, my take on this is different, and here is the number behind it.
5Please push back if you think the target is wrong. I would rather hear it now.
Lesson 04 -- Pre-class

Influencing Without Authority -- Presenting Your People Strategy to Canadian Leadership

Presenting your people strategy to five Canadian leaders who do NOT report to you: open with the data, answer the cost objection with the cost of doing nothing, name the trade-off before the room names it, and leave the meeting with an owner for every pillar. Key words: talent retention, engagement survey, performance cycle, succession planning, employer brand, people analytics, workforce planning, change management, psychological contract, diversity and inclusion, influence without authority, employee value proposition, to make a compelling case, to phase out, a double-edged sword. Structure: inversion for emphasis (Never have I seen... / Not only did we..., but we also... / Hardly had we... when...) + executive presentation language (What the data shows us is... / I would like to draw your attention to... / The rationale behind this approach is... / What this means in practice is...).
0%
▼

Stage 1.1: Vocabulary Cards

Vocabulary

Listen to each term and read the example. This is the vocabulary a people strategy is written in — and the only one in which a Canadian board is willing to fund it.

Talent retention -- keeping the people you cannot afford to lose, and knowing what it costs when you do not
"Talent retention is not a value. It is a number, and this year the number is four."
Engagement survey -- the instrument that measures how invested people are in the work
"The engagement survey had been running for four years, and nobody had ever read it region by region."
Performance cycle -- the yearly loop of goals, feedback and review a company runs on its people
"That region has not completed a performance cycle since the pandemic. Nobody noticed."
Succession planning -- deciding today who is ready to take a critical role tomorrow, before anyone resigns
"Succession planning is the question nobody asks until the answer is urgent."
Employer brand -- what the market says about you as a place to work, when you are not in the room
"Our employer brand is doing our recruiting for us. Unfortunately, it is doing it badly."
People analytics -- using workforce data to make a people decision defensible in a room full of finance people
"Retention as a value was ignored for four years. Retention as people analytics took eleven minutes."
Workforce planning -- deciding how many people, with which skills, you will need, and by when
"Workforce planning is the difference between hiring in September and hiring in a panic in November."
Change management -- the discipline of taking people through a change they did not ask for
"Nobody asked for this change. That is not an obstacle to change management. That is change management."
Psychological contract -- the unwritten expectations between a company and a person, the promises nobody signed
"We never broke her contract. We broke the psychological contract, and she resigned six weeks later."
Diversity and inclusion -- who is in the room, and whether they can actually speak once they are there
"Diversity is who you hire. Inclusion is whether they are still speaking in year two."
Influence without authority -- getting a decision made by people who do not work for you and never will
"Influence without authority is not a soft skill. In that room it is the only skill."
Employee value proposition -- the deal you offer: what a person actually gets in exchange for joining you and staying
"If our employee value proposition is only the salary, we will lose to whoever pays more."
To make a compelling case -- to argue something so well that saying no becomes the harder option
"Make a compelling case, and then stop talking. The silence after a strong argument is not awkward."
To phase out -- to retire an old practice gradually, on purpose, instead of stopping it overnight
"We phase out the old performance cycle over two quarters. We do not switch it off in one."
A double-edged sword -- a real advantage that carries a real cost, at the same time
"Flexible work is a double-edged sword: it widens the talent pool and it weakens the informal culture."

Stage 1.2: Matching

Practice

Match each term with the correct definition.

Talent retention
Engagement survey
Performance cycle
Succession planning
Employer brand
People analytics
Workforce planning
Change management
Psychological contract
Diversity and inclusion
Influence without authority
Employee value proposition
To make a compelling case
To phase out
A double-edged sword

Stage 1.3: Grammar in Context

GRAMMAR

Read the text and answer the questions.

When Marina Ortiz joined the Halden Group as its first Director of People and Culture, she was given a title, almost no budget, and no authority whatsoever. The five regional directors did not report to her, they had built the company, and they had never once been asked to adopt a people strategy written by somebody who had been in the building for six weeks. Her predecessor had presented a beautiful framework to the board, had watched it approved, and had watched nothing happen afterwards. The plan was never rejected. It was simply never carried by anyone. Influence without authority was not a soft skill in that room. It was the only skill available to her.

So Marina spent a quarter inside the numbers before she proposed anything. The engagement survey had been running for four years, and nobody had ever read it region by region. When she did, the story stopped being about culture and started being about money: one region was losing its senior teachers at three times the rate of the others, every departure was costing eleven months of hiring, and that region had not completed a performance cycle since the pandemic. Talent retention, presented as a value, had been ignored for four years. Talent retention, presented as people analytics, took eleven minutes.

The board adopted her strategy in a single meeting. Never had she seen a plan survive because it was right; plans survive because somebody in the room decides to carry them. So she made a compelling case, and then she named the trade-off before anyone else could. Not only did the flexible model widen the talent pool, she told them, but it also weakened the informal culture that had held the company together for twenty years. It was a double-edged sword, and she intended to manage it rather than deny it. Hardly had she finished when the regional director who had quietly killed her predecessor's framework asked to own the first pilot.

1. Why is it "Never had she seen a plan survive" and not "Never she had seen a plan survive"?
A Because, once the emphasis word (Never) moves to the front, the sentence borrows the word order of a question: the auxiliary comes BEFORE the subject.
B Because it is a rhetorical question in disguise.
C Because the subject is feminine and therefore follows the verb.
2. "Not only did the flexible model widen the talent pool..." — why widen and not widened?
A Because the sentence is in the present.
B Because did already carries the past — the main verb goes back to its base form, exactly as in "Did you widen it?".
C Because after "not only" the verb is never conjugated.
3. According to the text, why did the previous framework fail?
A The board rejected it because it was badly written.
B The board approved it, and then nobody in the room ever carried it.
C The regional directors were never shown the framework at all.
4. What made the board adopt Marina's strategy, according to the text?
A Her authority over the five regional directors.
B The fact that her strategy was more correct than her predecessor's.
C She argued it with data, named the trade-off first, and somebody in the room decided to carry it.

Stage 1.4: Grammar Tip -- Inversion for Emphasis

GRAMMAR

The grammar of weight: the emphasis word moves to the FRONT of the sentence and the auxiliary steps ahead of the subject, exactly as it does in a question — but this is not a question, it is authority.

FormUseExample
Never / Rarely / At no point
+ auxiliary + subject		Conviction. You put your whole career behind a single statement.Never have I seen a strategy succeed without an owner.
Not only + did + subject + base verb
..., but ... also ...		Scale. Two achievements in one breath, without turning into a list.Not only did we redesign the cycle, but we also rebuilt the onboarding.
Hardly / Scarcely + had
+ subject + past participle + when		Speed. The result arrived before the doubt did.Hardly had we launched the pilot when the scores rose.
Only when / Only after
+ clause + auxiliary + subject		The only condition. This way, and no other way.Only when they owned it did the plan move.
NegativeThe inversion IS the negative — never add “not”At no point was succession planning discussed. (never “was not”)
QuestionInversion is a device for STATEMENTS. In a question, keep the normal word order.Have you ever seen a plan survive without an owner?
Presentation languageWhat the data shows us is... · I would like to draw your attention to... · The rationale behind this approach is... · What this means in practice is...

Watch out (slip number one): after the emphasis word, the subject cannot come before the auxiliary. “Never I have seen” is wrong — the correct form is “Never have I seen”. The sentence borrows the word order of a question, and only the word order: no question mark.

Watch out (slip number two): if the original sentence has no auxiliary, English borrows one: We redesigned it → “Not only did we redesign it”. And the main verb goes back to its base form (redesign, never redesigned).

Dosage: inversion is the seasoning, not the dish. Two or three of them in a ten-minute presentation sound like authority. Six sound like theater — and the room stops listening to the argument in order to watch the performance.

Collocation: it is make a compelling case (never “do a case”), draw your attention to (never “call your attention for”), and phase out a practice (never “phase off”).

Stage 1.5: Fill in the Blank

Practice

Complete each sentence with the correct form. Tap Listen to hear the full sentence.

"Never a people strategy succeed without an owner in the room."
"Not only the performance cycle, but we also rebuilt the onboarding."
"Hardly the pilot when the engagement scores began to rise."
"Only when the directors owned the plan to move."
"What the data that one senior departure costs us eleven months of hiring."
"I would like to our succession coverage: two of nine critical roles."

Stage 2: Put the Board Presentation in Order

Order

Put the five moves of the presentation in the order in which you will run it in front of the Canadian leadership.

?Name the trade-off yourself, before the room does: it is a double-edged sword, and here is how I manage it.
?Open with the data, never with the belief: what the data shows us is that one senior departure costs eleven months of hiring.
?Close with the ask: one owner per pillar, one metric, and a review in ninety days.
?Draw their attention to the hole: succession coverage, two of nine critical roles.
?Answer the cost objection with the cost of doing nothing, in people analytics, and let them read both numbers.

Stage 3: Pronunciation

Speaking

Listen to each sentence, then record yourself saying it. These are the five sentences of your August first presentation, in the order you will say them.

What the data shows us is that one senior departure costs us eleven months of hiring.
I would like to draw your attention to our succession coverage: two of nine critical roles.
Never have I seen a people strategy succeed without an owner in the room.
Flexible work is a double-edged sword, and I would rather manage it than deny it.
What this means in practice is one owner per pillar, one metric, and a review in ninety days.

Stage 4: Situational Quiz

Quiz

Choose the best answer for each real moment of your presentation to the Canadian board.

You have one slide and a board that only wants to talk about cost. How do you open?
A "People are our greatest asset, and I believe culture is what makes this company different."
B "What the data shows us is that one senior departure costs us eleven months of hiring, and we have had four this year."
C "I would like to start by thanking everyone for the opportunity to be here today."
The CFO says retention is a problem for a good year, not for a year of budget cuts. The most senior answer is:
A "I would not argue with the budget. Not only did our last two departures cost us eleven months of hiring, but they also took the succession plan with them — that is the cost of doing nothing."
B "I understand. Perhaps we can revisit the people strategy next year, then."
C "But retention is about our values, and our values cannot have a budget line."
Which sentence uses inversion correctly?
A "Not only we redesigned the performance cycle, but we also rebuilt the onboarding."
B "Never I have seen a people strategy succeed without an owner."
C "Hardly had we launched the pilot when the engagement scores began to rise."
Two directors will say the flexible model is destroying the culture they built. What do you do?
A Avoid the subject. If nobody raises it, you do not have to defend it.
B Name it first: "Flexible work is a double-edged sword — it widened our talent pool and it weakened the informal culture. I intend to manage that, not deny it."
C Tell them the data proves they are wrong about the culture.
Nobody in the room reports to you. What is the only way your strategy actually gets carried?
A Getting the board to approve it formally, in writing, before anyone leaves the room.
B Escalating to headquarters in Brazil whenever a director resists.
C Making a compelling case and then asking one of them to own the first pilot — approval is not adoption.

Stage 5: Free Production

Reflection

Record yourself answering the question below. Speak for two or three minutes, with no script.

It is August first. You are presenting your People & Culture strategy for Maple Bear Canada to five leaders, and not one of them reports to you. Open with what the data shows, draw their attention to the gap, answer the cost objection with the cost of doing nothing, name one trade-off before they do, and close by asking each of them to own something. Use at least two inversions and six words from this lesson.

Survival Card -- Lesson 4

1What the data shows us is that one senior departure costs us eleven months of hiring.
2I would like to draw your attention to our succession coverage: two of nine critical roles.
3Never have I seen a people strategy succeed without an owner in the room.
4Flexible work is a double-edged sword, and I would rather manage it than deny it.
5What this means in practice is one owner per pillar, one metric, and a review in ninety days.
Lesson 05 -- Pre-class

Brazil vs. Canada -- Articulating Cultural Differences with Academic and Executive Teams

Explaining the difference between Brazilian and Canadian culture to a room of PhDs and directors — with no stereotype, no sweeping generalization, and no retreat from the observation itself: name the dimension, back it with evidence, and put the right cause under the spotlight. Key words: cultural intelligence, high-context culture, low-context culture, power distance, individualism and collectivism, indirect communication, assertiveness, cultural humility, work-life integration, inclusive leadership, to bridge cultural gaps, to read between the lines, to account for, to set out, to take into consideration. Structure: cleft sentences (It was the power distance that... / What this team needs is...) + hedging for comparative cultural analysis (One could argue that... / More often than not... / It would appear that... / For the most part...).
0%
▼

Stage 1.1: Vocabulary Cards

Vocabulary

Listen to each term and read the example. This is the vocabulary the Canadian academics already use to describe you — from August on, it is the vocabulary you use to describe the room.

Cultural intelligence -- the ability to read a culture you were not raised in, and adjust inside it without losing yourself
"Cultural intelligence is not knowing many cultures. It is functioning well inside one you do not know yet."
High-context culture -- a culture where the meaning lives around the words: in the tone, the pause, the relationship
"In a high-context culture, we will look into it can mean no, and everyone in the room knows it."
Low-context culture -- a culture where the meaning lives in the words themselves: say it, write it down, and it is settled
"Canada leans low-context: if it was not said out loud, do not assume the room heard it."
Power distance -- how far the boss feels from the room, and how safe it is to contradict one in public
"It was the power distance, not the language, that kept them silent in that meeting."
Individualism and collectivism -- whether a person is measured on their own result, or on what the group delivered together
"Our reward system rewards the individual. Our culture rewards the group. That gap is the problem."
Indirect communication -- saying the difficult thing by making it easy to infer, never by naming it
"Indirect communication is not dishonesty. It is a different address for the same message."
Assertiveness -- stating your position clearly and holding it, without attacking the person who disagrees
"Assertiveness travels badly. What reads as clear in Toronto can read as harsh in Sao Paulo."
Cultural humility -- assuming your way is one way, not the way, and staying curious once you are in the room
"I am not arriving with a Brazilian model to install. That is cultural humility, and it is also strategy."
Work-life integration -- a life where the work and the rest of it share the same day, on purpose, not in two sealed halves
"Two days at the office and two at home is work-life integration. Managing it is a skill, not a perk."
Inclusive leadership -- leading so that the quietest person in the room still ends up speaking
"Everyone was invited and three people spoke. That was not inclusive leadership. That was a guest list."
To bridge cultural gaps -- to build the crossing between two ways of working, instead of picking one of them
"My job is not to choose a culture. It is to bridge cultural gaps between two of them."
To read between the lines -- to hear what was meant, when it was never actually said
"In Brazil you learn to read between the lines. In Canada you learn to say the line."
To account for -- to include a factor in your reasoning, deliberately, because it changes the result
"Any diagnosis that does not account for power distance will read the silence as agreement."
To set out -- to state expectations clearly, at the start, so that nobody has to guess later
"I would rather set out the expectations in week one than defend them in month six."
To take into consideration -- to weigh a factor before deciding, not after
"We should take into consideration that this team has never been asked for its opinion before."

Stage 1.2: Matching

Practice

Match each term with the correct definition.

Cultural intelligence
High-context culture
Low-context culture
Power distance
Individualism and collectivism
Indirect communication
Assertiveness
Cultural humility
Work-life integration
Inclusive leadership
To bridge cultural gaps
To read between the lines
To account for
To set out
To take into consideration

Stage 1.3: Grammar in Context

GRAMMAR

Read the text and answer the questions.

The first session went beautifully, and that was the problem. Nadia Ferraz had flown in to run a cultural assessment with the academic faculty of a bilingual school group, and for ninety minutes not one professor objected to anything she proposed. She wrote in her notes that the room was aligned. Six weeks later, the plan was quietly dead. It was not the plan that failed, her director told her afterwards. It was the way she read the room that failed, and the two are not the same thing.

She had walked into a low-context culture carrying the instincts of a high-context one. In the country she came from, you read between the lines: the pause tells you the answer, and a polite silence in a meeting is rarely a yes. Here, silence meant something else entirely. Two of those professors had run their faculties for twenty years, and they did not contradict a visitor in public. They contradicted her on Friday, in an email, in three careful paragraphs, copied to her director. Any diagnosis that fails to account for that kind of power distance will read a quiet room as consent.

What she learned that quarter was not a technique. It was a posture. One could argue that her mistake had been a sentence in the first draft of her brief, a sentence with no hedge in it at all: they avoid conflict here. More often than not, she would write later, difficult feedback in that faculty was delivered indirectly — and it would appear that it was the hierarchy, rather than any dislike of conflict, that explained it. For the most part, her second brief said the same things as the first. The difference was that this one could be argued with, and so it was read. What that faculty had needed all along was not another framework. It was cultural humility, and one question nobody had ever asked them: what do you think the problem is?

1. Why is it “It was the power distance that produced the silence” and not “...what produced the silence”?
A Because in an it-cleft the link is always that (or who, with people). The “what” is a direct transfer from your first language.
B Because “what” only exists in questions.
C Because the subject is abstract and takes no relative pronoun.
2. “What she learned that quarter was not a technique.” — why do we NOT say “What she learned that quarter it was not a technique”?
A Because “it” can never appear in a sentence that opens with “What”.
B Because the wh-cleft already has its verb (What ... was). Adding “it was” says the verb twice — it is your first language leaking into the English.
C Because the subject of the sentence is feminine.
3. According to the text, why was the first session a problem because it went beautifully?
A Because the professors had not read the brief before the meeting.
B Because nobody objected in the room, and she recorded that silence as alignment — when the disagreement was simply going to arrive later, in writing.
C Because the faculty agreed with the plan and then changed their minds six weeks later.
4. What made the second brief succeed where the first one failed?
A It contained different findings, based on a second round of data.
B It was shorter, and it avoided any comparison between the two cultures.
C It said the same things, but as tendencies with hedging rather than as rules — so the faculty could argue with it, and therefore read it.

Stage 1.4: Grammar Tip -- Cleft Sentences & Hedging

GRAMMAR

The grammar of the spotlight: the sentence splits in two to push ONE piece of it into the light — and hedging is what keeps that light from turning into a stereotype.

FormUseExample
It-cleft
It was + X + that / who		The knife. Picks ONE cause and rules the others out.It was the power distance that produced the silence, not the language.
Wh-cleft
What + clause + is / was + X		The stage. Holds the room until the last word, then lands it.What this faculty needs is to be asked before it is told.
Always It wasEven with a plural subject. Never “It were”.It was the academic directors who pushed back.
Hedging: a tendencyTurns a rule into a tendency.More often than not, feedback here is delivered indirectly.
Hedging: reading the evidenceAn interpretation, not a verdict.It would appear that the silence has more to do with hierarchy.
Hedging: open and protectOpens the claim, then shields it.One could argue that... · For the most part... · That said, I would be careful not to overgeneralize.
Collocationtake into consideration (never “take in consideration”) · account for a factor (never “account the factor”) · set out the expectations (never “set the expectations out of”) · bridge cultural gaps (never “make a bridge in the culture”)

Watch out (slip number 1): in the it-cleft the link is that, never “what”. “It was the power distance what made them silent” is your first language arriving intact. With people, use who.

Watch out (slip number 2): in the wh-cleft the verb is already there. “What surprised me most it was the directness” says the verb twice. The correct form is “What surprised me most was the directness”.

Dosage: hedging is not insecurity — it is PRECISION. “Brazilians avoid conflict” is a stereotype, and a PhD will take it apart in ten seconds. “More often than not, difficult feedback in our Brazilian teams is delivered indirectly” is an observation, and he will argue with you instead of dismissing you.

The combination you want: hedge + cleft in the SAME sentence. “It would appear that it was the power distance, rather than the language, that kept them quiet.” It is the most senior sentence in this lesson.

Stage 1.5: Fill in the Blank

Practice

Complete each sentence with the correct form. Tap Listen to hear the full sentence.

"It was the produced the silence, not agreement."
"What surprised me most the directness of the feedback."
"It was the academic directors pushed back, three days later, in writing."
"More often , difficult feedback in our Brazilian teams is delivered indirectly."
"It would the silence has more to do with hierarchy than with language."
"Any diagnosis that does not power distance will read the silence as agreement."

Stage 2: Put the Cultural Briefing in Order

Order

Put the five moves in the order in which you will run the first session with the Canadian academic team.

?Put the real cause under the spotlight: it was the power distance that produced the silence, not agreement.
?Close by handing the room the question nobody has asked them: what do you think the problem is?
?Set out the expectations on day one: what this session is, what it is not, and what happens with the findings.
?Protect the claim before anyone attacks it: that said, there is more variation inside each country than between them.
?State the observation as a tendency, never as a rule: more often than not, difficult feedback here is delivered indirectly.

Stage 3: Pronunciation

Speaking

Listen to each sentence, then record yourself saying it. These are the five sentences of your first session with the Canadian academic team, in the order you will say them.

More often than not, difficult feedback in our Brazilian teams is delivered indirectly.
It was the power distance that produced the silence, not agreement.
Any diagnosis that does not account for hierarchy will read the silence as agreement.
What this faculty needs is not a program. It is to be asked before it is told.
That said, I would be careful not to overgeneralize. My job is to bridge cultural gaps, not to rank them.

Stage 4: Situational Quiz

Quiz

Choose the best answer for each real moment of your session with the Canadian academics.

A Canadian professor asks: “Are you not at risk of overgeneralizing about two entire countries?” The most senior answer is:
A “You are right. I will remove that part of the brief.”
B “I would be, if I were describing people. I am describing tendencies: more often than not, difficult feedback in our Brazilian teams is delivered indirectly.”
C “No. The data is very clear about how Brazilians behave.”
Nobody objected in the diagnostic session. What do you write in your report?
A “It would appear that it was the power distance in the room, rather than agreement, that produced the silence.”
B “The faculty is fully aligned with the proposed plan.”
C “The faculty was not interested enough to comment.”
Which sentence uses a cleft correctly?
A “It was the hierarchy what kept them quiet.”
B “What surprised me most it was the directness of the feedback.”
C “It was the academic directors who pushed back, and they did it in writing.”
You are asked to summarize your approach to the Canadian division in one sentence. You say:
A “I will bring the Brazilian culture program and adapt it to the local reality.”
B “What I am bringing is not a model. It is a diagnosis, and my job is to bridge cultural gaps rather than rank them.”
C “I will observe first and decide later whether we need a strategy at all.”
Everyone was invited to the culture session, and three people spoke. What is the correct reading?
A The session was inclusive: nobody was excluded from the invitation.
B That was a guest list, not inclusive leadership — and any diagnosis that does not account for who felt safe to speak is measuring the wrong thing.
C The other participants had nothing to add, which is normal in academic teams.

Stage 5: Free Production

Reflection

Record yourself answering the question below. Speak for two or three minutes, with no script.

A Canadian director asks you on a video call: how is leading People & Culture in Canada going to be different from what you did in Brazil? Answer as the senior HR leader you are about to become on August 1st. Name two dimensions, hedge both of them, put the real cause under the spotlight with at least two cleft sentences, and use six words from this lesson. End with what you are NOT going to do.

Survival Card -- Lesson 5

1More often than not, difficult feedback in our Brazilian teams is delivered indirectly.
2It was the power distance that produced the silence, not agreement.
3Any diagnosis that does not account for hierarchy will read the silence as agreement.
4What this faculty needs is not a program. It is to be asked before it is told.
5That said, I would be careful not to overgeneralize. My job is to bridge cultural gaps, not to rank them.
Lesson 06 -- Pre-class

Diagnosing Culture -- Running a Cultural Assessment with the Canadian Team

Running the cultural assessment with the Canadian team — and defending the instrument in front of a room that was surveyed once and never told the outcome: choose the tool, take the baseline before you change anything, and read the findings back. Key words: cultural assessment, diagnostic tool, baseline, response rate, focus group, trust index, root cause, gap analysis, findings, benchmark, to run a diagnostic, to gather insights, to surface an issue, to come up with, to open a can of worms. Structure: causative passive (We had the questionnaire designed by... / I am having the focus groups facilitated... / Our goal is to have the baseline completed by...) + hedging on data (The data would suggest that... / To some extent, the findings indicate...).
0%
▼

Stage 1.1: Vocabulary Cards

Vocabulary

Listen to each term and read the example. This is the vocabulary of the deck the Canadian leadership reads — and from September on, it is the deck you write.

Cultural assessment -- a structured study of how an organization actually works, rather than how it says it works
"A cultural assessment does not ask people to describe the culture. It puts the culture in a position where it has to show itself."
Diagnostic tool -- the instrument you choose to see with: a questionnaire, an interview protocol, a focus group
"The diagnostic tool is never neutral. Choose the wrong one and you will collect politeness and call it data."
Baseline -- the measurement you take before you change anything, so that later you can prove what moved
"Have the baseline taken in September. Without it, every improvement in March is just your opinion."
Response rate -- the share of people who actually answered, and the first thing a skeptical room will attack
"Thirty-one percent is not a technical failure. The response rate is the first finding."
Focus group -- a small room, a facilitator, and the sentences a questionnaire can never capture
"A survey tells you how many. A focus group tells you why, and it tells you in ninety minutes."
Trust index -- a single number that tracks whether people believe what leadership tells them
"Whatever the trust index comes back as, it will be lower than the leadership team believes."
Root cause -- the thing underneath the symptom, and the only thing worth fixing
"Attrition is not a root cause. It is what a root cause looks like nine months later."
Gap analysis -- the distance between where the culture is today and where the strategy needs it to be
"The gap analysis is the only slide the board will remember, because it is the only one with a distance on it."
Findings -- what the data actually says, before anyone decides what to do about it
"Nobody in that faculty was ever told what the findings were. That is why they will not answer you now."
Benchmark -- an external number you compare yourself to, so that good stops being an opinion
"Sixty-two means nothing on its own. Against the education benchmark of seventy-one, it means a great deal."
To run a diagnostic -- to put an organization through a structured examination before prescribing anything
"We are not here to run a diagnostic on you. We are here to run one with you."
To gather insights -- to collect what people mean, not only what they answered
"The survey gathers numbers. The focus groups are where we gather insights."
To surface an issue -- to bring a problem out into the open, where the room can finally work on it
"A good focus group does not collect opinions. It surfaces the issue that everybody already knew about."
To come up with -- to produce an idea or a framework, usually under pressure
"Give me three weeks and I will come up with an instrument this faculty will actually answer."
To open a can of worms -- to raise one question that releases a dozen you did not plan for
"I know I am opening a can of worms. I would rather open it on a Thursday than read it in an exit interview in March."

Stage 1.2: Matching

Practice

Match each term with the correct definition.

Cultural assessment
Diagnostic tool
Baseline
Response rate
Focus group
Trust index
Root cause
Gap analysis
Findings
Benchmark
To run a diagnostic
To gather insights
To surface an issue
To come up with
To open a can of worms

Stage 1.3: Grammar in Context

GRAMMAR

Read the text and answer the questions.

Every People and Culture leader who arrives in a new division wants to run a diagnostic. Very few of them stop to ask what a diagnostic does to the people being diagnosed. The diagnostic tool is never neutral. The moment a questionnaire lands in an inbox, the organization starts answering a question nobody wrote: is it safe to be honest here?

This is why the response rate is the first finding, and not a footnote. A team that has had its opinion collected before and was never told the outcome will answer at thirty percent — and the thirty percent who do answer are not a random sample. They are the angriest and the most loyal, and the middle of the organization is missing from the data. The data would suggest that a low response rate is not a technical failure at all. It is a trust index with no survey attached to it.

So the sequence matters more than the instrument. Have the baseline taken before you change anything, so that in March you can prove what moved instead of arguing about it. Have the expectations set out in writing before the first question is asked: who reads the findings, when, and what happens to them. One director in Toronto had the questionnaire designed by an external specialist and then had it reviewed by two people inside her own building, precisely so that the faculty could not dismiss it as a document written somewhere else. To some extent, the findings indicate that this single decision did more for her response rate than the questions ever did.

And when the results arrive, resist the symptom. Attrition is not a root cause; it is what a root cause looks like nine months later. A good focus group will not collect opinions — it will surface issues that have been quiet for a decade, and some of them will not be culture problems at all. You will open a can of worms. The gap analysis you present to the board will be clean; the room you leave behind will not. That is not a reason to skip the assessment. It is a reason to come up with a plan for the second week, and not only for the first.

1. Why do we say “We had the questionnaire designed by a specialist” and not “We had designed the questionnaire by a specialist”?
A Because in the causative passive the past participle comes after the object (have + object + past participle). “We had designed” is past perfect, and it means WE did the designing.
B Because “design” cannot be used in the passive voice in English.
C Because the subject is plural, which forces inversion.
2. “I had Marc review the questions.” — why do we NOT say “I had Marc to review the questions”?
A Because “review” is a noun in that position.
B Because after have + person the verb takes the base form, with no “to”. That “to” is a direct translation from your first language.
C Because Marc is a proper name, and proper names are never followed by an infinitive.
3. According to the text, why is a low response rate a finding rather than a technical failure?
A Because the questionnaire was too long and people gave up halfway through it.
B Because a team that was asked once and never told the outcome answers at thirty percent — so the rate is a trust index with no survey attached to it.
C Because thirty percent is statistically sufficient for any cultural assessment.
4. What does the Toronto director's decision (an external specialist, then an in-house review) actually buy her?
A A cheaper instrument, because the review work was not paid for.
B A shorter questionnaire, because two reviewers cut half of the questions.
C Credibility — the faculty could not dismiss the survey as a document written somewhere else, and the text says it did more for the response rate than the questions did.

Stage 1.4: Grammar Tip -- Causative Passive & Hedging Data

GRAMMAR

The grammar of the person who has it done: the subject does not perform the action, but answers for it — which is exactly where a People & Culture leader stands.

FormUseExample
Causative Passive
have + object + past participle		You arranged it; somebody else performed it. The action is theirs, the decision is yours.We had the questionnaire designed by an external specialist.
Causative Active
have + person + base verb		You name the person you called on. No “to”. Never “to review”.I had Marc review the questions before they went out.
Deadline pattern
to have + X + completed by		The delivery commitment, without promising who does what.Our goal is to have the baseline completed by the end of September.
get instead of haveSame structure, less formal, and it hints at effort: “I finally got the 2024 data re-coded.” Use have with the board; get with your team.
Plain passive vs causative“The survey was designed by a consultant” names the person who typed it — and erases you. “We had the survey designed” names the person who DECIDED. In a diagnostic room, nobody asks who typed it.
Hedging on dataA reading of the data, not a verdict. An interpretation, which the room can still argue with.The data would suggest that... · To some extent, the findings indicate...
Collocationrun a diagnostic · gather insights · surface an issue · come up with a framework (never “come up a framework”) · open a can of worms

Watch out (the number one slip): the ORDER. In English the past participle comes AFTER the object: “had the questionnaire designed”. “We had designed the questionnaire” is past perfect — it says you designed it yourself, which is not what happened.

Watch out (the other slip): no “to” after have + person. “I had Marc to review” is a word-for-word translation arriving whole. The correct form is “I had Marc review”.

The combination you want: a causative and a hedge in the SAME sentence. “The data would suggest that we should have the baseline taken before September, rather than after.” It is the most senior line in this lesson.

Stage 1.5: Fill in the Blank

Practice

Complete each sentence with the correct form. Tap Listen to hear the full sentence.

"We had the questionnaire by an external specialist."
"I am the focus groups facilitated in-house."
"Our goal is to have the baseline before we change anything."
"I had Marc the questions before they went out."
"The data would the response rate is itself the first finding."
"Attrition is not a . It is what a root cause looks like nine months later."

Stage 2: Put the Assessment in Order

Order

Put the five moves in the order you will run the cultural assessment with the Canadian team.

?Go deeper: have the focus groups facilitated in-house, and gather the insights the survey could not capture.
?Read the findings back to the faculty first, before the board sees them, exactly as you promised in week one.
?Set out in writing who reads the findings and what happens to them, before a single question goes out.
?Run the gap analysis and look for the root cause underneath the symptom, not the attrition number itself.
?Have the baseline taken before you change anything, so that in March you can prove what actually moved.

Stage 3: Pronunciation

Speaking

Listen to each sentence, then record yourself saying it. These are the five sentences of your diagnostic kickoff with the Canadian team.

We had the questionnaire designed by an external specialist, and I had it reviewed in-house.
Our goal is to have the baseline completed before we change anything.
The data would suggest that the response rate is itself the first finding.
Attrition is not a root cause. It is what a root cause looks like nine months later.
I know I am opening a can of worms, and I would rather surface it now than read it in an exit interview.

Stage 4: Situational Quiz

Quiz

Choose the best answer for each real moment of your cultural assessment in Canada.

A Canadian director asks: “Who wrote this questionnaire? If it came from Brazil, we will see it in the first line.” The most senior answer is:
A “It was written by our team in Brazil, but we adapted it for you.”
B “We had the questionnaire designed by an external specialist here, and I had it reviewed by two people in this building before it went out.”
C “I do not think it matters who wrote it, as long as the questions are good.”
Only thirty-one percent of the faculty answered the survey. What do you say to the leadership team?
A “The data would suggest that the response rate is itself the first finding: a team that was asked once and never told the outcome answers at thirty.”
B “The survey failed. We should send it again next month with a reminder.”
C “Thirty-one percent is enough. I will present the results as if the whole faculty had answered.”
Which sentence uses the causative correctly?
A “We had designed the survey by an external specialist.”
B “I had the consultant to present the findings to the faculty.”
C “I am having the focus groups facilitated in-house, and I had Marc review the questions first.”
The board says: “Attrition is up nine points. That is the culture problem.” You answer:
A “Agreed. I will build a retention program and present it next month.”
B “Attrition is not a root cause. It is what a root cause looks like nine months later, and what I want to bring you is the thing underneath it.”
C “The number is high, but it is probably a coincidence of this quarter.”
Marc warns you that the focus groups will bring up a decade of quiet resentment. What is the senior reading?
A Cancel the focus groups and keep the assessment to the anonymous survey only.
B “I know I am opening a can of worms. I would rather surface it now, on a Thursday, than read it in an exit interview in March — so I have a plan for the second week, not only the first.”
C Run them anyway and leave the resentment out of the findings, because it is not strictly a culture issue.

Stage 5: Free Production

Reflection

Record yourself answering the question below. Speak for two or three minutes, with no script.

You are opening the diagnostic kickoff with the Canadian academic team.
