In two minutes: explain what cultural assessment you plan to have carried out, who arranged each part of it, when the baseline is taken, what you expect to find, and what happens to the findings. Use at least two causative structures, one hedge on a number, and six words from this lesson. End by naming the resistance you already know is in the room.

Survival Card -- Lesson 6

1We had the questionnaire designed by an external specialist, and I had it reviewed in-house.
2Our goal is to have the baseline completed before we change anything.
3The data would suggest that the response rate is itself the first finding.
4Attrition is not a root cause. It is what a root cause looks like nine months later.
5I know I am opening a can of worms, and I would rather surface it now than read it in an exit interview.
Lesson 07 -- Pre-class

Navigating Difficult Conversations -- Feedback, Accountability, and Conflict in a Canadian Context

Leading the conversation nobody wants to have: name a behavioral pattern with evidence, work through the defensiveness without wounding the person, close with a corrective action and a date on it — and then REPORT the conversation upward, with precision. Key words: constructive feedback, conflict resolution, blind spot, defensiveness, resistance to change, non-violent communication, growth mindset, behavioral pattern, performance gap, corrective action, to take ownership of, to address head-on, to follow through on, to sugarcoat, to beat around the bush. Structure: reporting verbs and their five complementation patterns (he acknowledged THAT... / he denied MISSING... / he apologized FOR reacting... / I encouraged him TO take... / he agreed TO follow through...).
0%
▼

Stage 1.1: Vocabulary Cards

Vocabulary

Listen to each term and read the example. This is the vocabulary of a difficult conversation that ends in an agreement, not in resentment.

Constructive feedback -- feedback that names the behavior and its impact, and leaves the person able to act on it
"Constructive feedback is not softer feedback. It is feedback the other person can actually do something with."
Conflict resolution -- the work of turning a disagreement into a decision both sides can live with
"Conflict resolution is not making the conflict disappear. It is making it produce something."
Blind spot -- the thing everyone on the team can see about you, except you
"His blind spot is that he hears a question about a deadline as an accusation about his character."
Defensiveness -- the reflex of protecting yourself instead of hearing the point
"Defensiveness is not disagreement. It is the door closing before the sentence has finished."
Resistance to change -- the pushback a team produces when a change arrives without them
"Resistance to change is rarely about the change. More often than not, it is about not having been asked."
Non-violent communication -- naming the observation, the impact and the request, and never the character of the person
"Non-violent communication separates the behavior from the person. That separation is the whole method."
Growth mindset -- treating an ability as something you build, not something you were issued at birth
"A growth mindset turns I am not good at this into I am not good at this yet."
Behavioral pattern -- a behavior that has happened often enough to stop being an accident
"One missed deadline is an incident. Three is a behavioral pattern, and it needs a different conversation."
Performance gap -- the distance between what was agreed and what was actually delivered
"Name the performance gap with a number, or the whole conversation will sound like an opinion."
Corrective action -- the specific step, with a date on it, that closes the gap
"A conversation without a corrective action and a date is a conversation you will simply have again."
To take ownership of -- to accept that the outcome was yours, without being asked to
"I am not asking you to agree with me. I am asking you to take ownership of the rollout."
To address head-on -- to raise the difficult thing directly, at the start, instead of circling it
"I would rather address it head-on today than manage the consequences of it for six months."
To follow through on -- to actually do the thing you agreed to do, after the meeting has ended
"He agreed to every action in that meeting. He followed through on none of them."
To sugarcoat -- to make the message so gentle that the other person never hears the problem
"If you sugarcoat this, he will leave the call happy and nothing at all will change."
To beat around the bush -- to talk around the difficult point instead of naming it
"Do not beat around the bush with this team. Name the issue first, then protect the person."

Stage 1.2: Matching

Practice

Match each term with the correct definition.

Constructive feedback
Conflict resolution
Blind spot
Defensiveness
Resistance to change
Non-violent communication
Growth mindset
Behavioral pattern
Performance gap
Corrective action
To take ownership of
To address head-on
To follow through on
To sugarcoat
To beat around the bush

Stage 1.3: Grammar in Context

GRAMMAR

Read the text and answer the questions.

The conversation lasted forty minutes, and Ines Correia lost it in the first ninety seconds. She had rehearsed the opening for two days. She had a performance gap with three dates behind it, and a behavioral pattern that was no longer arguable. And then, at the door of the sentence, she flinched. She spent four minutes on how much the team valued him, and by the time she arrived at the deadlines, he had already worked out where she was going and had built the wall. She had tried not to wound him. What she had actually done was sugarcoat it, and a message that is sugarcoated is a message that never lands.

Her director asked for the debrief on the Thursday, and that is where the second lesson arrived. He acknowledged that the dates had slipped, Ines said. He denied being resistant to the project. He pointed out that nobody had asked him whether the timeline was realistic, and he accused the head office of imposing a plan built without a single question to the people who had to run it. He apologized for raising his voice. He did not apologize for anything he had said. And then, at the end, he agreed to follow through on one revised date, and he promised to bring the plan himself.

Her director listened to all of it and asked one question. And what did you ask him? Nothing, Ines said. There had not been room. That, her director told her, is the blind spot: she had walked in to deliver a verdict and had walked out with a confession she had never requested. Defensiveness is information, he said. It is a man telling you, badly, that something happened to him that you do not know about. Next time, name the pattern in the first thirty seconds, address it head-on, and then be quiet for long enough that he tells you the thing that is not in your spreadsheet. I would encourage you to close with one corrective action and a date — and, above all, I would advise you not to beat around the bush with a man who is already braced for the blow.

1. “He denied being resistant to the project.” — why can you NOT say “He denied to be resistant”?
A Because deny never takes a complement of any kind, only a direct object.
B Because deny takes the -ing form (deny + -ing), never the infinitive. The pattern belongs to the VERB itself, and it has to be learned together with it.
C Because the sentence is in the past, and in the past the infinitive disappears.
2. The number one mistake of this lesson: why is “She suggested me to talk to him” wrong?
A Because suggest does NOT take an object + infinitive. It is “suggested that I talk to him” or “suggested talking to him”. The structure travels straight in from your first language and brings the whole sentence down.
B Because the correct form would be “suggested me talking to him”.
C Because suggest only exists in the passive voice.
3. According to the text, what did Ines actually do wrong in the first ninety seconds?
A She opened with the three dates before she had built any rapport with him.
B She spent four minutes praising him before naming the issue — so he saw the message coming and was already defending himself by the time it arrived.
C She raised her voice, and he raised his voice back.
4. What does the director mean when he says that defensiveness is information?
A That a defensive employee is always hiding something, and the manager should keep pushing until it comes out.
B That defensiveness should simply be ignored, because it is an emotional reaction and not a professional one.
C That the defensiveness is telling her, badly, that something happened to him which is not in her spreadsheet — so the right move is to name the pattern and then be quiet.

Stage 1.4: Grammar Tip -- Reporting Verbs & Their Patterns

GRAMMAR

Every reporting verb carries its own pattern, and the pattern cannot be worked out from the meaning: it is learned together with the verb. This is the grammar of the debrief — the moment you tell your VP what was said in the room.

PatternVerbsExample
1. verb + that + clauseacknowledge, point out, explain, insist, claim, admit, addHe acknowledged that the deadlines had slipped.
2. verb + -ingdeny, admit, suggest, recommend, mentionHe denied missing the deadlines. · She suggested talking to him first.
3. verb + preposition + -ingapologize for, insist on, object to, accuse sb of, blame sb forHe apologized for reacting defensively. · He accused the head office of imposing it.
4. verb + object + to + infinitiveencourage, urge, advise, remind, ask, invite, warnI encouraged him to take ownership of the rollout.
5. verb + to + infinitiveagree, refuse, promise, offer, threaten, decideHe agreed to follow through on the corrective action.
BackshiftWhen you report in the past, the tense steps back ONE step: “the dates have slipped” → He acknowledged that the dates had slipped. But if the fact still holds today, keeping the present is perfectly acceptable: “He insists that the timeline is impossible.”

Watch out (mistake number one): suggest does NOT take an object + infinitive. “He suggested me to talk to her” is the structure of your first language arriving intact. The correct form is “He suggested that I talk to her” or “He suggested talking to her”. The same goes for recommend.

Watch out (mistake number two): after a PREPOSITION you always use the -ing form, never the infinitive. “He apologized to react defensively” → “He apologized for reacting defensively”. Store each preposition glued to its verb: apologize for, insist on, object to, accuse somebody of.

Why this matters to you: the reporting verb you choose IS ALREADY an assessment. “He said the timeline was impossible” is neutral. “He insisted that the timeline was impossible” is a man who will not move. “He acknowledged that the timeline was impossible” is a man conceding a fact against himself. Same event, three readings — and your Canadian VP will read exactly the one you chose.

The three-sentence debrief: what he acknowledged, what he denied, and what he agreed to do. In that order, and nothing else. It is the report your VP wants on Thursday.

Stage 1.5: Fill in the Blank

Practice

Complete each sentence with the correct reporting verb pattern. Tap Listen to hear the full sentence.

"He apologized defensively in front of the whole team."
"She suggested to him before the next steering meeting."
"He denied the deadlines, and the three dates said otherwise."
"He accused the head office the initiative without consulting anyone."
"He agreed on the corrective action by the fifteenth."
"I encouraged him of the rollout rather than explain it."

Stage 2: Put the Difficult Conversation in Order

Order

Put the five moves in the order in which you will run the 1:1 with a Canadian coordinator who is resistant to change.

?Hand him the microphone and go quiet: ask what you do not know, and listen past the defensiveness to the information underneath it.
?Debrief upward in three sentences: what he acknowledged, what he denied, and what he agreed to do.
?Address it head-on in the first thirty seconds: name the issue without sugarcoating it and without beating around the bush.
?Close with one corrective action and a date on it, and ask him to take ownership of that date in front of you.
?Put the evidence on the table: the behavioral pattern, the three dates, and the performance gap between what was agreed and what arrived.

Stage 3: Pronunciation

Speaking

Listen to each sentence, then record yourself saying it. These are the five sentences that carry a difficult 1:1 from start to finish — and the debrief that comes after it.

What I have noticed is a pattern, and I would rather address it head-on than manage it for six months.
He denied missing the deadlines, but he acknowledged that his team had never been consulted.
I am not asking you to agree with me. I am asking you to take ownership of the outcome.
If I sugarcoat this, you will leave the call happy and nothing at all will change.
He apologized for reacting defensively, and he agreed to follow through on the corrective action.

Stage 4: Situational Quiz

Quiz

Choose the best answer for each real moment of your 1:1 with a Canadian coordinator.

You open the 1:1. He has missed three deadlines. The most senior first sentence is:
A “Before anything else, I want you to know how much this team values you and how happy we are with your work.”
B “I want to address something head-on. Three dates on the rollout have slipped, and what I have noticed is a pattern rather than an incident.”
C “There is a small thing I wanted to mention, and it is really not a big deal at all.”
He answers: “So you are building a case against me.” You say:
A “It is not the behavior itself that concerns me. It is the impact, and this conversation ends with one corrective action that you and I agree on together.”
B “If you are going to be defensive, there is no point in continuing this conversation.”
C “No, no. Forget the deadlines. Let us talk about something else.”
Your VP asks for the debrief. Which sentence reports the conversation with the most precision?
A “He said a lot of things, and in the end he was fine with everything.”
B “He suggested me to change the timeline, and he denied to be resistant.”
C “He acknowledged that the dates had slipped, he denied being resistant to the project, and he agreed to follow through on a revised plan by the fifteenth.”
Which sentence uses the reporting verb pattern correctly?
A “He apologized to react defensively in front of the team.”
B “He accused the head office of imposing a plan that nobody had been asked about.”
C “She recommended me to speak with him before the steering meeting.”
He goes quiet and then says: “Nobody ever asked me if that timeline was possible.” And he is right. You say:
A “You are right, and I am not going to argue with that. The pattern still stands, and so does the question I am asking you now: what would a realistic date look like?”
B “That is not relevant. The deadlines were the deadlines.”
C “You are completely right, and I am sorry. Let us forget the three dates.”

Stage 5: Free Production

Reflection

Record yourself answering the question below. Speak for two or three minutes, with no script.

Your Canadian VP calls you on Thursday and asks: how did the conversation with the coordinator go? Give her the debrief, as the senior HR leader you are about to become on August 1st. Three sentences on the conversation itself, with three different reporting verbs (what he acknowledged, what he denied, what he agreed to do). Then say what YOU got wrong in that room, and what the corrective action is, with a date on it. Use six words from this lesson.

Survival Card -- Lesson 7

1What I have noticed is a pattern, and I would rather address it head-on than manage it for six months.
2He denied missing the deadlines, but he acknowledged that his team had never been consulted.
3I am not asking you to agree with me. I am asking you to take ownership of the outcome.
4If I sugarcoat this, you will leave the call happy and nothing at all will change.
5He apologized for reacting defensively, and he agreed to follow through on the corrective action.
Lesson 08 -- Pre-class

Midpoint Review -- Consolidating HR Leadership Language for Canada

The midpoint: the lesson where you stop, take stock and PRESENT what you found — without inflating what worked or burying what fell short. All the language of lessons 1 to 7 comes back here as REVIEW (causative passive, cleft, inversion, third conditional, reporting verbs, hedging), inside a single executive presentation. Key words: takeaway, caveat, rationale, trade-off, traction, quick win, roadblock, track record, preliminary, tangible, to take stock of, to fall short of, to build on, to iron out, the jury is still out. New structure: participle clauses — the grammar of the executive summary (Having reviewed the findings... / ...leaving us nine points below the benchmark / Asked about the timeline... / Given the response rate...).
0%
▼

Stage 1.1: Vocabulary Cards

Vocabulary

Listen to each term and read the example. This is the vocabulary of someone who presents a midpoint review — and survives the questions that come after it.

Takeaway -- the one thing you want the room to still remember tomorrow morning
"If they remember one takeaway from this review, it should be that the culture is not broken -- it is unheard."
Caveat -- the limit you attach to your own claim, before somebody else attaches it for you
"One caveat: these are preliminary numbers, and the November survey may move them."
Rationale -- the reasoning behind a decision, stated out loud so the room can argue with it
"Nobody objected to the plan. They objected to never having been given the rationale for it."
Trade-off -- what you agree to lose in order to gain something you want more
"Every people strategy is a trade-off. A leader who presents one without naming the cost is presenting a wish."
Traction -- the point at which an initiative starts moving without you pushing it
"The mentorship program is finally gaining traction: three teams asked to join without being invited."
Quick win -- a small, fast, visible result that buys you the credit to attempt the slow one
"I need one quick win before December, or nobody will fund the two-year work."
Roadblock -- the obstacle that stops the work, as opposed to the one that merely slows it
"The real roadblock was never the budget. It was that two directors had stopped speaking."
Track record -- the history of what you have actually delivered, which speaks before you do
"In a new country, you have no track record. The first review is where you begin building one."
Preliminary -- true so far, and openly not final yet
"These are preliminary findings. Treating them as conclusions is how a review loses a room."
Tangible -- concrete enough to be counted, shown, or disagreed with
"Give the board one tangible number per slide, or the whole review sounds like a feeling."
To take stock of -- to stop, and honestly assess where you actually are
"Eight lessons in, it is worth taking stock of what has moved and what has not."
To fall short of -- to miss a target you had publicly committed to
"We fell short of the response rate we projected, and I would rather report that than bury it."
To build on -- to use what already worked as the foundation of the next step
"The focus groups worked. What I propose is to build on them rather than start again."
To iron out -- to resolve the small remaining problems in something that basically works
"The framework is sound. We have six weeks to iron out the details before the launch."
The jury is still out -- it is genuinely too early to say, and pretending otherwise costs you credibility
"On whether the new onboarding is working, the jury is still out. Ask me again in March."

Stage 1.2: Matching

Practice

Match each term with the correct definition.

Takeaway
Caveat
Rationale
Trade-off
Traction
Quick win
Roadblock
Track record
Preliminary
Tangible
To take stock of
To fall short of
To build on
To iron out
The jury is still out

Stage 1.3: Grammar in Context

GRAMMAR

Read the text and answer the questions.

Mariana Vidal had twenty-two slides and eleven minutes, and she lost the room on slide three. Having spent six months on the most careful cultural work of her career, she opened the midpoint review by explaining her methodology. By the time she arrived at what she had actually found, the Chief Operating Officer had stopped taking notes. The work was excellent. The review was a failure, and the two facts have nothing to do with each other.

The mistake was not nerves, and it was not her English. It was sequence. A senior room does not want the journey; it wants the takeaway, and it wants it first. Asked afterwards what he had needed, the COO was blunt: one tangible number, the rationale behind her recommendation, and the caveat she was clearly holding back. Given eleven minutes, he said, spend one of them telling me what you found and ten defending it.

She rebuilt the deck in a single evening. The new version opened with a sentence, not a slide: the culture here is not broken, it is unheard, and the response rate is the proof. Then the number, then the trade-off. Based on the preliminary findings, she proposed two quick wins and one long piece of work, naming what each would cost. She said out loud that the survey had fallen short of the target she had promised in June, leaving her with a smaller sample than she had wanted — and that she would rather report that than bury it. On the new onboarding, she said, the jury is still out: ask me again in March.

The COO approved the plan in four minutes. What convinced him was not the confidence. It was the caveat. A leader who names the limit of her own data is a leader whose numbers can be trusted the next time she brings some — and that, more than any single result, is how a track record begins.

1. "Having spent six months on the work, she opened by explaining her methodology." — what is the perfect participle (having + past participle) saying here?
A That both actions happened at the same time: she spent six months while she was opening the presentation.
B That the participle action came FIRST: the six months of work, then the opening. It is how you pack the whole story into one subordinate clause and go straight to the point.
C That she is still spending the six months, because having marks the present continuous.
2. "Asked afterwards what he had needed, the COO was blunt." — why Asked and not Asking?
A Because the subject (the COO) RECEIVES the action: somebody asked HIM. A passive participle = past participle. "Asking" would mean HE was the one asking.
B Because after a comma English always requires a past participle.
C Because "ask" is an irregular verb and does not take an -ing form.
3. According to the text, why exactly did Mariana lose the room on slide three?
A Because her English was not precise enough for a senior Canadian audience.
B Because of sequence: she opened with her methodology instead of her takeaway, and by the time she reached what she had found, the COO had stopped listening.
C Because the cultural work itself was weak, and the room could see it immediately.
4. What actually convinced the COO to approve the plan in four minutes?
A Her confidence, and the fact that she never admitted any weakness in the data.
B The two quick wins, which meant the project would show results before December.
C The caveat — because a leader who names the limit of her own data is a leader whose numbers can be trusted the next time, and that is where a track record begins.

Stage 1.4: Grammar Tip -- Participle Clauses

GRAMMAR

The grammar of the executive summary: it takes TWO sentences and turns the less important one into a short clause, with no subject and no connector — leaving the main clause with all the weight. It is what separates someone who NARRATES from someone who SUMMARIZES.

FormUseExample
1. Perfect participle
Having + past participle		The action that came FIRST. It packs the whole past into five words.Having reviewed the findings, I would propose two quick wins.
= After I reviewed the findings, I would propose...
2. Present participle
verbo + -ing		The RESULT or consequence of the main clause. It almost always comes AFTER the comma.The index came back at fifty-eight, leaving us nine points below the benchmark.
= ...and this left us nine points below.
3. Past participle
the participle alone		A PASSIVE meaning: something was done TO the subject.Asked about the timeline, most of the faculty said the same thing.
= When they were asked about the timeline...
4. Fixed openings
Given / Based on / Drawing on		The executive opening. It anchors the recommendation in the evidence, before you say what it is.Given the response rate, the caveat belongs on slide one.
Based on the preliminary findings, I am proposing...
The golden ruleThe participle and the main clause must share the SAME SUBJECT. If they do not, the sentence collapses (the classic dangling participle) — and in English it does not sound odd: it sounds wrong.

Watch out (mistake no. 1 — the dangling participle): "Having reviewed the findings, the response rate was disappointing" is WRONG: YOU reviewed the findings, not the response rate. The subject of the main clause has to be the one who performs the participle. Correct: "Having reviewed the findings, I found the response rate disappointing." Foolproof test: ask "who did it?" → the answer has to be the subject of the main clause.

Watch out (mistake no. 2): having takes a PAST PARTICIPLE, never an infinitive or a base form. "Having review the data" → "Having reviewed the data". And the passive participle is the participle alone, without being: "Being asked about the timeline" is possible, but the executive register prefers the dry "Asked about the timeline".

Why this matters to you: you have eleven minutes and a COO who stops taking notes on slide three. The participle clause is the tool that compresses "We reviewed the findings, and after that I prepared a proposal" into "Having reviewed the findings, I would propose..." — and gives your seconds back to the only part the room wants to hear: what you found, and what you are going to do.

Review of lessons 1-7 (today it all comes back): causative passive (We had the survey designed by...), cleft (What the team needs is...), inversion (Never have I seen...), third conditional + modal perfects (If we had asked earlier, we would have known / we should have addressed it), reporting verbs (He acknowledged that... / She agreed to...) and hedging (It would appear that... / To a certain extent...). This is not new content: these are your tools. Today you use them ALL in a single presentation.

Stage 1.5: Fill in the Blank

Practice

Complete each sentence with the correct participle form. Tap Listen to hear the full sentence.

" the focus groups, I would propose that we build on them rather than start again."
"The trust index came back at fifty-eight, us nine points below the benchmark."
" about the timeline, most of the faculty said exactly the same thing."
" the response rate, the caveat belongs on the first slide, not the last."
" the preliminary findings, I am proposing two quick wins and one long piece of work."
"Three teams asked to join without being invited, that the program is finally gaining traction."

Stage 2: Put the Midpoint Review in Order

Order

Put the five moves in the order you will run the midpoint review with the Canadian leadership. Remember Mariana's mistake: the room wants the end first.

?Name the caveat yourself, before anyone else does: what you fell short of, and what the jury is still out on.
?Close by asking for the one decision you actually came for, with a date attached to it.
?Open with the takeaway, in one sentence, before a single slide: the culture is not broken, it is unheard.
?Propose what comes next: two quick wins to build on, one long piece of work, and the trade-off each one costs.
?Put one tangible number on the table, and give the rationale behind it before anybody has to ask.

Stage 3: Pronunciation

Speaking

Listen to each sentence, then record yourself saying it. These are the five sentences that hold your midpoint review together, from start to finish.

Having taken stock of the first six months, I would propose two quick wins and one long piece of work.
We fell short of the response rate we projected, and I would rather report that than bury it.
Given the preliminary numbers, one caveat belongs on the first slide, not the last.
The focus groups worked, so what I propose is to build on them rather than start again.
On whether the new onboarding is working, the jury is still out. Ask me again in March.

Stage 4: Situational Quiz

Quiz

Choose the best answer for each real moment of your midpoint review with the Canadian leadership.

You have eleven minutes with the COO. Your first sentence is:
A “Let me start by walking you through the methodology we used and the four phases of the assessment.”
B “The takeaway first: the culture here is not broken, it is unheard — and the response rate is the evidence. Everything else I say today defends that sentence.”
C “Thank you all so much for your time today. I know how busy everyone is at this time of year.”
Which sentence uses the participle clause correctly?
A “Having reviewed the findings, the response rate was disappointing.”
B “Having reviewed the findings, I would propose that we build on the focus groups rather than start again.”
C “Having review the findings, I am proposing two quick wins.”
You promised a seventy percent response rate in June. It came back at thirty-eight. The COO has not asked about it yet. You:
A “One caveat, and I would rather give it to you than have you find it: we fell short of the rate I projected in June. It leaves me a smaller sample, and it is itself a finding about how heard this team feels.”
B Say nothing about it. He has not asked, the work is strong, and the number would only distract from the recommendation.
C “The response rate was low, but honestly that happens with every survey, so I would not read anything into it.”
He asks: “Is the new onboarding working?” You genuinely do not know yet. The most senior answer is:
A “Yes, definitely. Everyone I have spoken to seems very positive about it.”
B “I have no idea. It is impossible to measure something like that.”
C “On that one, the jury is still out. The first cohort finishes in February, so I will have a tangible number for you in March — and not before.”
He pushes back: “Two quick wins? I want the whole framework by Q2.” You say:
A “We can do that, and I would want you to see the trade-off: pulling the framework into Q2 means the baseline gets taken after the change rather than before, which costs us the ability to prove that any of it worked.”
B “That is not possible. Q2 is far too early for something of this size.”
C “Of course, no problem at all. I will have the whole framework ready by Q2.”

Stage 5: Free Production

Reflection

Record yourself answering the question below. Speak for two or three minutes, with no script.

You have eleven minutes with the Canadian leadership team, and this is the midpoint review. Give it. Open with your takeaway in ONE sentence, before any detail. Then: one tangible number and the rationale behind it; the caveat you are holding (what you fell short of, and what the jury is still out on); and what you propose next -- two quick wins to build on, one long piece of work, and the trade-off each one costs. Open at least two sentences with a participle clause (Having reviewed... / Given... / Based on...), and use six words from this lesson.

Survival Card -- Lesson 8

1Having taken stock of the first six months, I would propose two quick wins and one long piece of work.
2We fell short of the response rate we projected, and I would rather report that than bury it.
3Given the preliminary numbers, one caveat belongs on the first slide, not the last.
4The focus groups worked, so what I propose is to build on them rather than start again.
5On whether the new onboarding is working, the jury is still out. Ask me again in March.
Lesson 09 -- Pre-class

Diplomatic Pushback -- Disagreeing With People Who Know More Than You

Disagreeing with an academic council of senior researchers without capitulating and without fighting: concede out loud what is true, hold what is yours, and turn the objection into a concrete ask. Key words: counterpoint, reservation, premise, assumption, merit, anecdotal, rigor, peer-reviewed, common ground, to concede, to take issue with, to play devil's advocate, to stand your ground, to defer to, to hold water. Structure: concessive clauses (While / Although the sample is small, the pattern is consistent · Granted... That said... · Much as I would like to... · Even if we accept that... still) — his point goes in the subordinate clause, yours goes in the main clause, and the room walks out remembering the main clause.
0%
▼

Stage 1.1: Vocabulary Cards

Vocabulary

Listen to each term and read the example. This is the vocabulary of someone who disagrees with a specialist and is still taken seriously.

Counterpoint -- an argument you place against the one on the table, without rejecting the person who made it
"My counterpoint is not that he is wrong about the sample. It is that the sample is not what the number is measuring."
Reservation -- a specific doubt you name out loud, rather than a general feeling of discomfort
"I have one reservation about the plan, and I would rather say it now than nod and take it to the corridor afterwards."
Premise -- the thing an argument stands on before it starts, and usually never proves
"I take issue with the premise, not with the conclusion. If the premise holds, everything he said after it is correct."
Assumption -- something taken as true without evidence, and invisible to the person holding it
"The assumption in this room is that silence means agreement. The whole assessment exists to test that one sentence."
Merit -- the part of an argument that is genuinely good, whether or not you accept where it ends up
"There is real merit in what he is saying about the sample. There is none at all in what he concludes from it."
Anecdotal -- based on individual stories rather than on systematic evidence, and it can still be true
"He called the focus groups anecdotal, and he is right. Anecdotal is not the same as wrong, and that is the sentence I have to be able to say."
Rigor -- the discipline that makes a finding survive somebody actively trying to break it
"They are not attacking me. They are applying the only rigor they have, and if my finding cannot survive it, I would rather know on Thursday."
Peer-reviewed -- checked by other specialists in the field before anybody is asked to believe it
"It is not peer-reviewed, and it was never meant to be. A diagnostic and a study are two different objects with two different jobs."
Common ground -- the part both sides already agree on, and the only place a disagreement can safely start
"Let me start from the common ground: none of us believes that a faculty which says nothing is a faculty with nothing to say."
To concede -- to give a point away because it is true, not because you are losing
"I will concede the sample before he takes it from me. What I will not concede is what the sample means."
To take issue with -- to disagree with one specific claim, deliberately and out loud, and never with the person
"I take issue with the premise, and not with a single thing you concluded from it."
To play devil's advocate -- to argue a position you do not actually hold, in order to test whether the other one survives
"Let me play devil's advocate against my own finding for a moment, because you are going to do it anyway."
To stand your ground -- to hold your position under pressure, without raising your voice and without moving
"She conceded the sample without hesitating, and then she stood her ground on what it meant."
To defer to -- to accept somebody's authority on a point where they genuinely know more, as a choice and never a surrender
"I defer to this council entirely on the research question. I would not defer to anyone on what a sixty-two percent silence means."
To hold water -- (of an argument) to survive examination, used almost always in the negative
"That is a fair premise to test, and it does not hold water on exactly one point."

Stage 1.2: Matching

Practice

Match each term with the correct definition.

Counterpoint
Reservation
Premise
Assumption
Merit
Anecdotal
Rigor
Peer-reviewed
Common ground
To concede
To take issue with
To play devil's advocate
To stand your ground
To defer to
To hold water

Stage 1.3: Grammar in Context

GRAMMAR

Read the text and answer the questions.

Teresa Vidal lost the room in eleven seconds, and she lost it by agreeing. She had presented a culture assessment to an academic council once before, in Lisbon, and she had come out of it certain that the mistake had been arrogance. So this time she arrived humble. When the chair took off his glasses and said that a response rate of thirty-four percent was, in his field, anecdotal rather than evidence, she said: you are absolutely right, and I will redo the whole thing. Every word of that sentence was polite, and every word of it was true. It also told nine people that the culture work could wait a year, and they believed her, because she had said it herself.

There is a third answer, and it is not a personality — it is a structure. While the sample was small, the pattern inside it was consistent. Granted, four focus groups are not systematic. That said, they were the only occasion on which anybody had asked that faculty a question in four years. Even if we accept that the instrument is not peer-reviewed, a healthy faculty that is asked how it feels does not still decline to answer at a rate of two thirds. And much as she would have liked to defend the thirty-four percent, the senior move was to say, out loud and before anyone else could, that she would rather replace it — with an instrument the council itself helped her build.

Notice what those sentences do, because they all do the same thing twice. Each one concedes the part that is genuinely true, and each one puts that concession in the clause that depends. And then each one puts the finding — her finding, the one piece of ground that is hers — in the clause that stands. It is not decoration. A room walks out remembering the main clause. “While the sample is small, the pattern is consistent” and “While the pattern is consistent, the sample is small” contain exactly the same two facts and hand down opposite verdicts. Teresa never said a false word in Lisbon. She simply put her own weakness in the clause that survives, and the council heard her argue against herself in her own voice.

The chair told her something afterwards that she has repeated ever since. He said that every consultant who had ever stood in that room had arrived certain, and that he had never believed a single one of them; that rigor is not hostility, it is the only professional courtesy a researcher has to offer a stranger; and that what he had actually been trying to find out was not whether her data was perfect, but whether she knew where it was thin. To defer to him on the research question would have cost her nothing at all. To take issue with him on what a two-thirds silence means would have cost her nothing either — and it was the only thing in the building that was hers.

1. “While the sample is small, the pattern is consistent.” — why does the ORDER of the two clauses CHANGE the argument?
A It does not change it: the two clauses hold the same facts, so the meaning is identical.
B Because the concessive clause (While...) is the subordinate one — it is grammatically DEMOTED. The room walks out remembering the main clause. Invert the order and the same two facts hand down OPPOSITE verdicts.
C Because in English the concessive clause must always come at the end of the sentence.
2. The number one mistake of this lesson: why is “Although the sample is small, but the pattern is consistent” wrong?
A Because although and but do the SAME job, and English uses one of them, never both. It is the Portuguese double marker crossing over whole.
B Because “although” can only be used at the end of a sentence, never at the start.
C Because after “although” the verb has to move into the past.
3. And mistake number two: why is “Despite the response rate is low, the finding holds” wrong?
A Because “despite” only exists in British English.
B Because the correct form would be “Despite that the response rate is low”.
C Because despite / in spite of are prepositions: they take a noun or an -ing form, never a clause. The correct options are “Despite the low response rate”, “despite the fact that the response rate is low”, or simply “although”.
4. According to the text, what did Teresa actually do wrong in Lisbon?
A She was arrogant with the chair, and she defended a number that could not be defended.
B She agreed with everything. She conceded the whole assessment instead of only the part that was true — and by saying it herself, she persuaded nine people that the culture work could wait a year.
C She used the wrong vocabulary, and the council could not follow her technical argument.
5. What does the chair mean when he says that rigor is not hostility?
A That researchers enjoy attacking consultants, and the only defense is to never show them weak data.
B That breaking her argument is the only professional courtesy he has to offer a stranger — and that what he is really testing is not whether her data is perfect, but whether she knows where it is thin.
C That he did not actually disagree with her, and was only being polite in front of the council.

Stage 1.4: Grammar Tip -- Concession & Counter-Argument

GRAMMAR

The concessive clause is the structure that does TWO things in a single sentence: it hands the other person the point where he is right, and it holds yours. And it decides, silently, which of the two the room will remember.

StructureHIS point (subordinate clause)YOUR point (main clause)
1. While / Although / Even though + clause, + main clauseWhile the sample is small,the pattern is consistent.
2. Granted, ... That said, ...Granted, the groups are anecdotal.That said, they are the only question anyone asked.
3. Much as I + verb, + main clauseMuch as I would like to defend it,I would rather replace it.
4. Even if + clause, + main clause with stillEven if we accept that it is anecdotal,the silence still requires an explanation.
Pivots (they work with any of them)That said · Having said that · Nonetheless · Even so · And yet — they all say the same thing: I heard you, and I have not moved.

Watch out (mistake number 1): “Although the sample is small, but the pattern is consistent” is WRONG. Although and but do the same job, and English uses ONE of them, never both. The Portuguese double marker does not survive the crossing. Choose: “Although the sample is small, the pattern is consistent” OR “The sample is small, but the pattern is consistent”.

Watch out (mistake number 2): despite and in spite of are PREPOSITIONS — they take a noun or an -ing form, NEVER a clause. “Despite the response rate is low” → “Despite the low response rate” or “Despite the fact that the response rate is low”. If you want the full clause, the easy route is to switch to although.

Why this matters to you: the concessive clause is NOT politeness — it is hierarchy. The subordinate clause is grammatically demoted, and the room walks out remembering the MAIN one. “While the sample is small, the pattern is consistent” and “While the pattern is consistent, the sample is small” hold the SAME TWO FACTS and hand down OPPOSITE verdicts. Under pressure, the instinct is to put the concession last, because it sounds humbler — and that inverts your own argument without you saying one false word. His point comes first. Always.

The three-move answer: CONCEDE (give what is true, before anyone takes it) → COUNTER (hold what is yours, in the main clause) → PROPOSE (turn the objection into a concrete ask, with a date on it). In that order, and nothing else. A researcher who helps you build the instrument does not spend March attacking the instrument.

Stage 1.5: Fill in the Blank

Practice

Complete each sentence with the correct concessive structure. Tap Listen to hear the full sentence.

" the sample is small, the pattern inside it is consistent."
"Granted, the focus groups are anecdotal. , they are the only question anyone has asked this faculty in four years."
" defend the response rate, I would rather replace it."
" the data is anecdotal, the silence still requires an explanation."
", the finding holds."
"I the premise, and not with a single thing you concluded from it."

Stage 2: Put the Diplomatic Pushback in Order

Order

Put the five moves in the order in which you will face the Canadian academic council.

?Hold your finding in the main clause, where the room will remember it: that said, the sixty-two percent who said nothing are the finding.
?Turn the objection into an ask: two members of the council, in March, building the next instrument with you.
?Start from the common ground, and hand them the weakest part of your own work before anybody has to find it.
?Defer to them on the ground where they genuinely know more, and stand your ground on the one that is yours.
?Concede the part of the objection that is genuinely true, out loud and without flinching: granted, the sample is small.

Stage 3: Pronunciation

Speaking

Listen to each sentence, then record yourself saying it. These are the five sentences that carry a diplomatic disagreement from start to finish.

Granted, the sample is small, and I would not ask you to treat it as representative. That said, the sixty-two percent who said nothing are the finding.
While it is not peer-reviewed, it was never designed to be. A diagnostic and a study answer two different questions.
I defer to this council on the research question. I would not defer to anyone on what a sixty-two percent silence means.
Much as I would like to defend the response rate, I would rather replace it, with an instrument this council helps me build.
I take issue with the premise, and not with a single thing you concluded from it.

Stage 4: Situational Quiz

Quiz

Choose the best answer for each real moment of your academic council in Canada.

Dr. Prescott says: “Thirty-eight percent. In my field, that is not a finding. That is a rumor.” The most senior answer is:
A “You are absolutely right. I will redo the whole assessment and come back when I have a proper number.”
B “Granted, and I concede it without any reservation. That said, the sixty-two percent who chose not to answer are not missing data. They are the finding.”
C “With respect, thirty-eight percent is a perfectly normal response rate and the methodology is sound. I stand by the data.”
Which sentence uses the concessive structure CORRECTLY?
A “Although the instrument is not peer-reviewed, but it was never designed to be.”
B “Despite the sample is small, the pattern inside it is consistent.”
C “While the instrument is not peer-reviewed, it was never designed to be: a diagnostic and a study answer two different questions.”
He says: “You have been here six weeks. What could you possibly know about this faculty?” You say:
A “Very little, and that is precisely why I ran an assessment rather than arriving with a plan. I defer to this council on what the faculty is. I would not defer to anyone on what the response rate says about how it feels to be asked.”
B “Six weeks is enough. I have twelve years of experience in organizational culture.”
C “You are right, I am too new. Perhaps the council should lead this work instead.”
You want to disagree with his reasoning without attacking him. Which sentence does that?
A “I think you have misunderstood what this assessment is for.”
B “I take issue with the premise, and not with a single thing you concluded from it. If the premise held, everything you said after it would be correct.”
C “That argument is simply wrong, and I can prove it.”
He asks what exactly the council is being asked to approve. The strongest close is:
A “The culture strategy, in full, as it appears in the deck.”
B “Nothing today. I would rather come back when the data is stronger.”
C “Not the strategy. One thing: a second round of the instrument, designed with two members of this council, in March. Much as I would like to defend the thirty-eight percent, I would rather replace it.”

Stage 5: Free Production

Reflection

Record yourself answering the prompt below. Speak for two or three minutes, with no script.

The Academic Council of Maple Bear Canada has four objections to your assessment: the sample is 38%, the focus groups are anecdotal, the instrument is not peer-reviewed, and you have been in the country for six weeks. Every one of them is fair. Answer all four, as the senior HR leader you are about to become on August 1st. Concede each one out loud, hold your finding in the main clause every single time, and close by turning the objection into an ask with a date on it. Use the four concessive structures (While... / Granted... That said... / Much as I would like to... / Even if we accept that... still) and six words from this lesson.

Survival Card -- Lesson 9

1Granted, the sample is small, and I would not ask you to treat it as representative. That said, the sixty-two percent who said nothing are the finding.
2While it is not peer-reviewed, it was never designed to be. A diagnostic and a study answer two different questions.
3I defer to this council on the research question. I would not defer to anyone on what a sixty-two percent silence means.
4Much as I would like to defend the response rate, I would rather replace it, with an instrument this council helps me build.
5I take issue with the premise, and not with a single thing you concluded from it.
Lesson 10 -- Pre-class

People Analytics -- Making the Numbers Talk

Presenting an HR finding to the Academic Council without overclaiming: separate correlation from causation, name the confounding variable before Dr. Prescott does, keep the margin of error visible, and hand the room one actionable insight. Key words: data storytelling, correlation, causation, confounding variable, statistical significance, sample size, outlier, margin of error, leading indicator, actionable insight, to control for, to skew, to read too much into, to tease out, to connect the dots. Structure: reporting passives (It is estimated that turnover rose... / Turnover is thought to be linked to... / Retention is reported to have improved...) + data comparatives (twice as likely as / a threefold increase / significantly higher than).
0%
▼

Stage 1.1: Vocabulary Cards

Vocabulary

Listen to each term and read the example. This is the language of the People Analytics brief you present to the Canadian Academic Council — the difference between a chart and a decision.

Data storytelling -- turning a table of numbers into a claim a decision-maker can actually act on
"Data storytelling is not decoration. It is the discipline of making a number say the one true thing it can, and no more."
Correlation -- two things moving together, which is not yet a reason for one another
"Engagement rose while turnover fell, but that is a correlation. The mentorship program is not proven by it."
Causation -- one thing actually producing another, which a chart can hint at but never prove
"Causation is the claim a room of researchers will make you earn. A line on a slide is not enough to earn it."
Confounding variable -- a hidden third factor driving both numbers, and the first thing a researcher looks for
"The reorganization is the confounding variable here: it landed in the same quarter, and either event could be moving the numbers."
Statistical significance -- the test of whether a result is real or just the noise of a small sample
"At this sample size I would not claim statistical significance. I would call it directional and ask for a second read."
Sample size -- how many people the number actually rests on, and the fastest way to overclaim
"Before you defend the finding, defend the sample size. Thirty responses cannot carry a company-wide claim."
Outlier -- the single extreme case that quietly drags an average somewhere it should not go
"One team tripled its score. That team is an outlier, and it skews the mean more than it should."
Margin of error -- the honest band around a number that amateurs drop and researchers demand
"Keep the margin of error on the slide. It makes the number look smaller and makes you look like you have done this before."
Leading indicator -- a signal that moves before the outcome does, so you can act while it still matters
"Treat the onboarding window as a leading indicator, rather than waiting for turnover to confirm the problem a quarter too late."
Actionable insight -- a finding a manager can do something with on Monday, not just admire on a slide
"The actionable insight does not depend on the causal claim. It is the one thing a manager can act on this week."
To control for -- to hold a variable steady so it cannot secretly explain your result
"Once we control for tenure, the gap narrows but does not close, and it is that residual gap I am reporting."
To skew -- to pull a distribution to one side, usually through an outlier or a bad sample
"Drop the outlier and the effect shrinks but survives. Left in, it skews the whole story toward the flattering explanation."
To read too much into -- to draw a bigger conclusion than the data can honestly carry
"The fastest way to lose a research audience is to read too much into a chart that shows correlation and call it proof."
To tease out -- to separate a real signal from the noise around it
"After all the honesty, I can still tease out the one signal that survives every rival explanation."
To connect the dots -- to turn separate findings into one coherent story
"My job is to connect the dots into a single actionable insight, not to make each number say more than it can."

Stage 1.2: Matching

Practice

Match each term with the correct definition.

Data storytelling
Correlation
Causation
Confounding variable
Statistical significance
Sample size
Outlier
Margin of error
Leading indicator
Actionable insight
To control for
To skew
To read too much into
To tease out
To connect the dots

Stage 1.3: Grammar in Context

GRAMMAR

Read the text and answer the questions.

The most dangerous sentence in any analytics deck is the confident one. Two lines move together on the screen — engagement climbs, turnover falls — and the temptation is to stand up and announce that the mentorship program did it. In front of a room of researchers, that is the sentence that ends the presentation. Two numbers moving together is a correlation, and a correlation is not yet a cause. It is estimated that turnover among new managers fell nine points last quarter, and it is tempting to read too much into that — but the honest analyst asks what else changed in the same window.

Almost always, the answer is a confounding variable. Here, the reorganization landed in exactly the quarter the mentorship launched, and it is thought to have shifted the workload of half the teams. Either event could be moving the numbers, and no chart can separate them. So you name the variable you could not control for out loud, before anyone else does. Turnover is believed to be linked to onboarding, you say — not caused by it. The distinction is the whole of your credibility.

Then comes the discipline. One team tripled its score after a change of manager; that team is an outlier, and it will skew the average until you set it aside. You check the sample size, and you keep the margin of error on the slide even though it makes the number look smaller. Statistical significance is reported to matter less to executives than it should — which is precisely why keeping it marks you as someone who has done this before.

And still there is something true to say. You tease out the one signal that survives every rival explanation, and you call it what it is: a leading indicator, a thing that moves before the outcome does, not a proof. New joiners, for instance, are twice as likely as tenured staff to leave in the first year, and that gap holds even after you control for the reorganization. You connect the dots into a single actionable insight — watch the onboarding window monthly, rather than waiting for turnover to confirm the problem a quarter too late. The number never speaks for itself. Your job is to make it say the one true thing it can.

1. Why do we say “It is estimated that turnover fell nine points” and not “It is estimated turnover to fall nine points”?
A Because the impersonal report takes that + a full clause with a finite verb (fell), not an infinitive. “It is estimated” is followed by a whole sentence, not by “to fall”.
B Because “estimate” cannot be used in the passive voice in English.
C Because the number is plural, which forces the infinitive.
2. “Turnover is thought to be linked to onboarding.” — why is this stronger, in that room, than “Onboarding causes turnover”?
A Because the passive is always more polite than the active, regardless of meaning.
B Because the reporting passive states a finding without claiming causation as fact — it attributes the link to the evidence, which is exactly what a research audience demands.
C Because “causes” is grammatically incorrect after the word onboarding.
3. According to the text, why keep the margin of error on the slide when it makes the number look smaller?
A Because researchers cannot read a chart without one printed on it.
B Because keeping it marks you as someone who has done this before, rather than someone overclaiming a fragile number.
C Because the margin of error is what proves causation once and for all.
4. What does the writer say is left to present, once you have refused to overclaim?
A Nothing — an honest analyst has no conclusion to offer.
B The raw correlation, presented louder and with more confidence.
C The one signal that survives every rival explanation — a leading indicator you connect into a single actionable insight a manager can act on.

Stage 1.4: Grammar Tip -- Reporting Passives & Data Comparatives

GRAMMAR

The grammar of the analyst who states a finding without owning it as fact: you attribute the claim to the evidence, which is exactly the register a room of researchers trusts.

FormUseExample
Impersonal report
It is + reported/estimated/thought + that + clause		State a finding as the field's, not yours. Note: that + a full clause with a finite verb.It is estimated that turnover rose by twelve percent.
Personal report
subject + is thought/believed to + base verb		Raise the subject to the front. After to comes the base verb: be, reflect, drive.Turnover is thought to be linked to onboarding.
Perfect report
is + reported to have + past participle		The result reaches back into the past — it already happened.Retention is reported to have improved since March.
Why it sounds senior“Onboarding causes turnover” claims causation as fact — and a researcher will destroy it. “Turnover is thought to be linked to onboarding” states the same finding while conceding it is a reading of evidence, not a proof. That concession IS your credibility.
Data comparativesCompare two groups precisely, without overclaiming size.New joiners are twice as likely as tenured staff to leave · a threefold increase · significantly higher than the benchmark
Collocationcontrol for a variable · tease out a signal · read too much into a chart · an outlier skews the average · connect the dots into an insight

Watch out (the number one slip): the impersonal report needs that + a finite clause. “It is estimated turnover to rise” is a word-for-word import; the English is “It is estimated that turnover rose”.

Watch out (the other slip): after “is thought to” comes the base verb, not the -ing form. “is thought to reflecting” → “is thought to reflect”.

The line that wins the room: a reporting passive plus the honest caveat, in one sentence. “The drop is believed to be linked to the reorganization, which I could not fully control for — so I am reporting it as a leading indicator, not a cause.”

Stage 1.5: Fill in the Blank

Practice

Complete each sentence with the correct form. Tap Listen to hear the full sentence.

"It is turnover among new managers rose by twelve percent last year."
"Turnover is closely linked to how onboarding is run."
"Retention is improved since the mentorship program began."
"New joiners are twice tenured staff to leave in the first year."
"The drop is believed to the reorganization, though it cannot be proven."
"I could not fully the reorganization, so I am reporting the residual effect, not the raw one."

Stage 2: Put the Finding in Order

Order

Put the five moves in the order you will present the finding to the Academic Council — honesty first, conclusion last.

?Set the outlier aside and check the sample size, then keep the margin of error on the slide.
?Hand the room one actionable insight a manager can act on this Monday, without depending on the causal claim.
?Show the correlation plainly — two lines moving together — and refuse to call it a cause yet.
?Tease out the one signal that survives, and name it a leading indicator rather than a proof.
?Name the confounding variable yourself — the reorganization you could not control for — before anyone raises it.

Stage 3: Pronunciation

Speaking

Listen to each sentence, then record yourself saying it. These are the five sentences you will actually say in front of the council.

It is estimated that turnover among new managers rose by twelve percent last year.
Engagement is thought to be linked to onboarding, but I would not yet call that causation.
New joiners are twice as likely as tenured staff to leave in the first year.
The reorganization is the confounding variable I could not control for, so I am flagging it myself.
What survives every rival explanation is a leading indicator, and that is the one thing I am asking you to act on.

Stage 4: Situational Quiz

Quiz

Choose the best answer for each real moment in front of the Academic Council.

Dr. Prescott says: “Your two lines move together, so the mentorship program raised engagement. Yes?” The most senior answer is:
A “Yes. The data clearly proves the program caused the improvement.”
B “It is a correlation I cannot yet attribute — the reorganization landed in the same quarter. What I can defend is a leading indicator, not a cause.”
C “I am not sure. Maybe it did, maybe it did not.”
One team tripled its score after a new manager arrived. How do you present it?
A “That team is an outlier and it skews the average on this sample size. Set it aside and the effect shrinks but survives — that is the version I would act on.”
B “It is our strongest result, so I built the whole recommendation around it.”
C “I removed it quietly so nobody would ask about the sample size.”
Which sentence uses the reporting passive correctly?
A “It is estimated turnover to rise by twelve percent this year.”
B “Turnover is thought to reflecting the reorganization.”
C “It is estimated that turnover rose by twelve percent, and it is thought to be linked to onboarding.”
Prescott asks: “Did you control for tenure? New joiners always score differently.” You answer:
A “Tenure does not matter for engagement, so I left it out.”
B “You are right, and I should have said so first. Once we control for tenure the gap narrows but does not close, and it is that residual gap I am reporting.”
C “I did not, but a third of the sample is new, so it probably balances out.”
The council asks: “So what do we actually do with this on Monday?” The strongest close is:
A “Wait for next quarter's turnover figures to confirm everything first.”
B “One thing: treat the onboarding window as a leading indicator and watch it monthly, rather than waiting for turnover to confirm the problem a quarter too late. That does not depend on the causal claim.”
C “Approve the full culture strategy today, on the strength of these two lines.”

Stage 5: Free Production

Reflection

Record yourself answering the question below. Speak for two or three minutes, with no script.

You are presenting one people-analytics finding to the Canadian Academic Council. In two minutes: state the correlation you found, name the confounding variable you could not control for, admit the sample size and margin of error, then tease out the one signal that survives and turn it into an actionable insight. Use at least two reporting passives (it is estimated that... / is thought to be...), one data comparative (twice as likely as / a threefold increase), and six words from this lesson. End with the single thing a manager should do on Monday.

Survival Card -- Lesson 10

1It is estimated that turnover among new managers rose by twelve percent last year.
2Engagement is thought to be linked to onboarding, but I would not yet call that causation.
3New joiners are twice as likely as tenured staff to leave in the first year.
4The reorganization is the confounding variable I could not control for, so I am flagging it myself.
5What survives every rival explanation is a leading indicator, and that is the one thing I am asking you to act on.
Lesson 11 -- Pre-class

The Turnaround Story -- Narrating What Had Already Changed

Telling the board the turnaround you led in Brazil — and proving it was a build, not luck. The tool is the past perfect: it lets you narrate the SEQUENCE, so your action lands against a problem that was already there and your results arrive AFTER the groundwork was already laid. Key words: legacy, groundwork, tipping point, precedent, turnaround, entrenched, status quo, in hindsight, wake-up call, uphill battle, to inherit, to trace back to, to come to a head, to gain momentum, to reverse a trend. Structure: past perfect (had + past participle) and past perfect continuous (had been + -ing) — By the time..., had already... · had been climbing for two years · had traced back to onboarding before anyone named it. The simple past says WHAT happened; the past perfect proves it was a sequence, and the sequence is the proof.
0%
▼

Stage 1.1: Vocabulary Cards

Vocabulary

Listen to each term and read the example. This is the vocabulary of someone who narrates a recovery and makes the board believe she caused it.

Legacy -- what an earlier situation or leader leaves behind for you to deal with, good or bad
"The disengagement was not something I created; it was the legacy I inherited on my first day."
Groundwork -- the unglamorous preparation you do before anything visible can happen
"By the time the numbers moved, the groundwork had already been in place for two quarters, and it was the only reason the turnaround held."
Tipping point -- the moment a slow change suddenly becomes visible and hard to reverse
"The resignation was the tipping point: after it, the culture problem could no longer be treated as a rumor."
Precedent -- an earlier case that sets the pattern for how the next one is judged
"There was no precedent for a culture role in that office, so the first year was about proving the function should exist at all."
Turnaround -- the point at which a declining situation reverses and starts to recover
"The board wants to know whether the turnaround was leadership or luck, and the answer is in the sequence, not in the final number."
Entrenched -- so deeply established that it resists any attempt to change it
"The pattern was entrenched: it had been building for two years before anyone was willing to name it."
Status quo -- the existing state of things, especially the one people quietly prefer not to disturb
"Every turnaround is a fight with the status quo, and the status quo always had a head start."
In hindsight -- seeing clearly, after the fact, what was not obvious at the time
"In hindsight, I should have set the baseline earlier, before the culture had already deteriorated."
Wake-up call -- a shock that finally forces people to face a problem they had been ignoring
"Her exit interview was the wake-up call the leadership team had been avoiding for a year."
Uphill battle -- a struggle that is hard the whole way because everything is working against you
"Changing an entrenched culture is an uphill battle, and the first months move nothing you can measure."
To inherit -- to take over a situation, a team or a problem that somebody else created
"I did not build that crisis. I inherited it, and then I gave it a name nobody had dared to use."
To trace back to -- to follow a problem to its true origin, however far back that is
"The attrition traced back to onboarding, a decision made two years before the resignations started."
To come to a head -- (of a building problem) to reach the point where it can no longer be ignored
"The tension had been growing for months, and it came to a head the week our most senior teacher resigned."
To gain momentum -- to start moving faster and become harder to stop, once it has begun
"Once the new onboarding gained momentum, engagement climbed on its own, and my job became protecting it."
To reverse a trend -- to turn a line that was going the wrong way and make it go the right way
"We did not just slow the decline; we reversed the trend, and the exit interviews changed from warnings to thank-you notes."

Stage 1.2: Matching

Practice

Match each term with the correct definition.

Legacy
Groundwork
Tipping point
Precedent
Turnaround
Entrenched
Status quo
In hindsight
Wake-up call
Uphill battle
To inherit
To trace back to
To come to a head
To gain momentum
To reverse a trend

Stage 1.3: Grammar in Context

GRAMMAR

Read the text and answer the questions.

Renata Alencar lost the room in the first minute, and she lost it by telling the truth in the wrong order. She had led a genuine turnaround in her last company — attrition halved, engagement recovered, a toxic pattern broken — and when the new board asked her to walk them through it, she said: “I joined, I redesigned onboarding, and within a year the numbers turned around.” Every word was true. It also sounded exactly like luck. Three events in a line, no cause between them, and a chair who had funded that story before and watched it die in a second country.

There is a way to tell the same three facts that proves the opposite, and it is not charisma — it is a tense. By the time she was even asked to fix it, the attrition had been climbing for two years, and nobody had traced it back to onboarding yet. That is the legacy she inherited; she did not create the crisis, she named it. Long before the numbers moved, she had rebuilt the onboarding, she had set up a quarterly pulse survey, and she had trained the leads on psychological safety. So when the most senior teacher resigned and the whole thing came to a head, the groundwork had already been in place for two quarters. The wake-up call did not catch her unprepared. It caught her ready.

Notice what the past perfect is actually doing, because it does the same job every time. It puts what was already there — the failing, the building, the preparing — before the simple-past event that everyone can see. “I redesigned onboarding and engagement rose” is two events that might be a coincidence. “I had redesigned onboarding before engagement rose” is a cause and an effect, in that order, and the order is the whole argument. The status quo was entrenched and it had a head start; changing it was an uphill battle; and the only way to prove you won it on purpose is to show the sequence — what had been failing, what you had built, and only then, what finally moved.

In hindsight, Renata said afterwards, the mistake was never the data. It was that she had handed the board a list when she should have handed them a timeline. A turnaround told in the simple past is a series of happy accidents. The same turnaround told with the past perfect underneath it is a method — and a method is the only thing that survives the trip to a country where you have not lived the culture yet, and where the groundwork will have to be laid again, from the very beginning.

1. Why does “I had redesigned onboarding before engagement rose” prove more than “I redesigned onboarding and engagement rose”?
A It does not prove more — the two sentences carry the same information, so they make the same claim.
B Because the past perfect fixes the ORDER: the cause (your action) is completed BEFORE the effect (the result). Two simple-past events side by side could be a coincidence; a past perfect before a simple past is a claim about cause.
C Because the past perfect makes the sentence more polite and less arrogant.
2. The number one mistake of this lesson: why is “By the time the board met, we already lost thirty teachers” wrong?
A Because an event completed BEFORE another past moment needs the past perfect: “we had already lost thirty teachers”. “By the time” is the classic trigger, and the simple past drops the sequence the whole story depends on.
B Because “by the time” can only be followed by the present tense.
C Because “lost” should be “loosed” in this context.
3. And mistake number two: why is “engagement had been dropped for eighteen months” wrong?
A Because past perfect continuous does not exist in American English.
B Because the correct form is “engagement was dropped for eighteen months”.
C Because “had been dropped” is a PASSIVE (somebody dropped it). For an action in progress over time you need the past perfect CONTINUOUS: “engagement had been dropping for eighteen months” — had been + -ing.
4. According to the text, what did Renata actually do wrong with the new board?
A She lied about the numbers, and the board found out the attrition had not really halved.
B She told three true facts as a flat list of simple-past events, with no sequence between them — so a real turnaround sounded like luck. She handed them a list when she should have handed them a timeline.
C She was arrogant and took too much personal credit for the recovery.
5. What does the text mean when it says a method — not a story of happy accidents — is what survives the trip to a second country?
A That the exact same actions, in the same order, will produce the same numbers anywhere.
B That what transfers to Canada is the SEQUENCE — diagnose the legacy, lay the groundwork, wait for the tipping point, reverse the trend — not the luck. In a culture she has not lived in, the groundwork has to be laid again from the beginning.
C That she should simply repeat the Brazil presentation word for word to the Canadian team.

Stage 1.4: Grammar Tip -- Past Perfect & Past Perfect Continuous

GRAMMAR

The past perfect is the “before the before”. It puts the thing that was already true — the failing, the building, the preparing — in front of the simple-past event everyone can see. In a turnaround story, that ordering is not decoration: it is the difference between a sequence you caused and a coincidence you were standing next to.

StructureFormExample (your Brazil turnaround)
1. By the time + past simple, + past perfectBy the time X happened, Y had (already) + past participleBy the time the board reviewed it, attrition had already fallen.
2. Past perfect continuous (duration up to a past point)had been + -ing + for / sinceEngagement had been dropping for eighteen months.
3. Past perfect + before + past simpleS had + past participle ... before + past simpleI had rebuilt onboarding before the numbers moved.
4. already / never / just + past perfecthad already / never / just + past participleThe groundwork had already been in place for two quarters.
The three layers of a turnaroundDeep past (had been failing / had been building) → your build (had rebuilt, had set up) → the visible result (attrition fell, engagement rose). The past perfect owns the first two layers; the simple past owns the third.

Watch out (mistake number 1): after By the time, the earlier event needs the past perfect. “By the time the board met, we already lost thirty teachers” is WRONG → “we had already lost thirty teachers”. A first language may let you drop that earlier layer in speech; English does not, and dropping it here erases the very sequence your argument depends on.

Watch out (mistake number 2): had been + -ing (active, in progress) is not had been + past participle (passive, done to it). “Engagement had been dropped for eighteen months” says somebody dropped it. You mean it had been dropping. And use for + a period (for two years), since + a starting point (since 2023) — never “since two years”.

Why this matters to you: a turnaround told entirely in the simple past — “I arrived, I changed onboarding, retention improved” — is a to-do list, and a to-do list reads as luck. The same story with the past perfect underneath — “attrition had been climbing for two years; long before it moved, I had rebuilt onboarding” — is a method. The board is not testing whether your Brazil numbers are real. It is testing whether you can show the order, because the order is the only proof that it will happen again in Canada.

The three-move story: BACKDROP (what had already been failing, in the past perfect continuous) → BUILD (what you had put in place, in the past perfect, before any result) → REVERSAL (what finally moved, in the simple past). In that order, and the order is the argument. Never lead with the result.

Stage 1.5: Fill in the Blank

Practice

Complete each sentence with the correct past-perfect form. Tap Listen to hear the full sentence.

"By the time the board reviewed the numbers, attrition for two quarters."
"When I inherited the team, engagement for eighteen months."
"The problem onboarding, a decision made two years earlier."
"Long before the results appeared, I onboarding and set up a quarterly pulse survey."
"The tension for months before it came to a head."
"Once the new onboarding , engagement climbed on its own."

Stage 2: Put the Turnaround Story in Order

Order

Put the five moves in the order in which you will narrate the Brazil turnaround to the board.

?Show the groundwork you had already laid, completed before any result appeared: I had rebuilt onboarding and set up a pulse survey months before the numbers moved.
?Close on the reversal and the method, not the luck: the trend reversed, and what I am bringing to Canada is the sequence, not the coincidence.
?Set the backdrop in the past perfect continuous: for two years before I arrived, attrition had been climbing and engagement had been falling.
?Mark the tipping point as a simple-past event against everything that was already ready: when it came to a head, the new system had already been running for a term.
?Name the legacy you inherited and trace the problem to its root: the decline traced back to onboarding, a decision made long before the resignations.

Stage 3: Pronunciation

Speaking

Listen to each sentence, then record yourself saying it. These are the five sentences that carry a turnaround story from the backdrop to the method.

For two years before I arrived, attrition had been climbing, and nobody had yet traced it back to onboarding.
By the time the results appeared, the groundwork had already been in place for two quarters.
The tension had been building for months, and it came to a head the week our most senior teacher resigned.
We did not just slow the decline. We reversed the trend, and the exit interviews became thank-you notes.
In hindsight, the turnaround was a sequence, not an accident, and the sequence is what I am bringing to Canada.
