Stage 4: Situational Quiz

Quiz

Choose the best answer for each real moment of your board review.

The chair says: “The attrition fell — but the whole sector recovered that year. How do you know it was you?” The most senior answer is:
A “It was me. I redesigned onboarding and the numbers turned around within a year.”
B “By the time the market recovered, attrition had already been falling for two quarters. The groundwork had been laid eight months earlier, and the fall traces back to the onboarding redesign, not to the calendar.”
C “I suppose we will never really know whether it was the market or the work.”
Which sentence uses the past perfect CORRECTLY?
A “By the time the board met, we already lost thirty teachers.”
B “When I inherited the team, engagement had been dropped for eighteen months.”
C “When I inherited the team, engagement had been dropping for eighteen months, and nobody had traced it back to onboarding yet.”
He asks: “Walk me through what the situation was before you arrived.” You say:
A “When I inherited the team, engagement had been dropping for eighteen months, the pattern was entrenched, and nobody had traced it back to onboarding. I did not create the crisis; I named it.”
B “The situation was bad, and then I made it good.”
C “Honestly, everything was already improving by the time I got there.”
He asks what you would do differently. The answer that reads as senior is:
A “Nothing. The turnaround worked, so I would repeat it exactly.”
B “In hindsight, I would have set the baseline earlier. By the time I measured it, the culture had already deteriorated for months, and I lost the cleanest before-and-after I could have had.”
C “I would have asked for a bigger budget and more time.”
He says: “Canada is not Brazil. Why should we believe you can do it again?” The strongest answer is:
A “Because I did it once, so I can do it anywhere.”
B “Because Canada and Brazil are basically the same kind of organization.”
C “You should not believe it yet, and I would not ask you to. The Brazil turnaround was a sequence, not an accident — groundwork, tipping point, reversal, in that order. I have not lived the Canadian status quo, and the groundwork will have to be laid again from the beginning. What transfers is the method.”

Stage 5: Free Production

Reflection

Record yourself answering the prompt below. Speak for two or three minutes, with no script.

The Maple Bear board asks you to walk them through the Brazil turnaround and prove it was leadership, not luck — before they hand you Canada on August 1st. Tell it as a sequence, never as a list. Start with the backdrop (what HAD BEEN failing before you arrived, in the past perfect continuous), then the build (what you HAD put in place before any result appeared, in the past perfect), then the tipping point, and only then the reversal. Close by conceding that Canada is unproven and naming what actually transfers. Use the past perfect and past perfect continuous throughout, and at least six words from this lesson (legacy, groundwork, tipping point, to inherit, to trace back to, to come to a head, to reverse a trend).

Survival Card -- Lesson 11

1For two years before I arrived, attrition had been climbing, and nobody had yet traced it back to onboarding.
2By the time the results appeared, the groundwork had already been in place for two quarters.
3The tension had been building for months, and it came to a head the week our most senior teacher resigned.
4We did not just slow the decline. We reversed the trend, and the exit interviews became thank-you notes.
5In hindsight, the turnaround was a sequence, not an accident, and the sequence is what I am bringing to Canada.
Lesson 12 -- Pre-class

The First 90 Days -- Listening Before You Lead

Taking the reins of the Canadian division without arriving as a stranger who changes everything on day one: run a listening tour, report back what the outgoing director and the frontline staff actually said, and earn credibility before spending the mandate. Key words: entry plan, listening tour, ramp-up period, learning curve, credibility, mandate, predecessor, transition period, frontline staff, vantage point, handover, to hit the ground running, to take the reins, to size up, to lean on. Structure: reported speech + reporting verbs (he admitted that he had been wrong / she urged me to slow down / they warned me not to restructure / one coordinator explained that the reorganizations had been reversed).
0%
▼

Stage 1.1: Vocabulary Cards

Vocabulary

Listen to each term and read the example. This is the language of your first ninety days leading the Canadian division — the difference between arriving as a stranger and arriving as a listener.

Entry plan -- a deliberate 90-day plan for how a new leader will learn, decide, and act
"The entry plan she finally presented had three phases -- learn, decide, act -- and she was still, deliberately, in the first."
Listening tour -- a structured round of one-on-one conversations a new leader holds before changing anything
"Instead of restructuring, she spent the first quarter on a listening tour, sitting with the people who run the classrooms."
Ramp-up period -- the early stretch in a new role when you are still building context and cannot work at full speed
"The ramp-up period is not wasted time, even though it feels like it, because you are used to full speed and suddenly you cannot."
Learning curve -- how steep and how long the climb is before a new role feels natural
"That discomfort is the learning curve, not a sign that you are failing."
Credibility -- the trust you earn that makes people act on what you say, before any authority forces them to
"Credibility is not spent with the title. It is earned in the first quarter, one honest conversation at a time."
Mandate -- the clear authority and remit you are given to make a specific change happen
"You have a real mandate here, and the temptation will be to spend it fast to prove you deserve it."
Predecessor -- the person who held your role before you, whose decisions you inherit
"A predecessor who is honest about what went wrong is the most useful person you have in the first quarter."
Transition period -- the window between two leaders when a role is formally handed over
"The mistake almost every senior hire makes is to treat the transition period as an audition."
Frontline staff -- the people who do the core daily work directly with students, furthest from head office
"She sat with the frontline staff, not just the directors -- the people who actually run the classrooms."
Vantage point -- the position from which you see a situation, which shapes what you notice and miss
"It came from a particular vantage point -- the classroom floor -- that a new leader only reaches by asking early."
Handover -- the formal passing of a role, its context, and its unfinished work from one person to the next
"None of this was in the handover document, and none of it could have been."
To hit the ground running -- to be productive immediately in a new role, with no slow start
"Everyone expects a senior hire to hit the ground running, but the smartest ones slow down first to speed up later."
To take the reins -- to formally assume control and responsibility for something
"On Monday she takes the reins of the Canadian division, and the first decision is to make no big decision for ninety days."
To size up -- to assess a person or a situation quickly and shrewdly
"Size up the culture before you try to move it."
To lean on -- to rely on someone for support or expertise
"Lean on Gordon. He made the fast mistakes so you do not have to."

Stage 1.2: Matching

Practice

Match each term with the correct definition.

Entry plan
Listening tour
Ramp-up period
Learning curve
Credibility
Mandate
Predecessor
Transition period
Frontline staff
Vantage point
Handover
To hit the ground running
To take the reins
To size up
To lean on

Stage 1.3: Grammar in Context

GRAMMAR

Read the text and answer the questions.

On her last day in the role, Gordon left Danielle a voice note about the one thing the handover document could not carry. He admitted that he had restructured the academic team in his first month, and he told her that he had been wrong about almost all of it — because he had changed the answer before he understood the question. His advice was the opposite of his instinct: he urged her to decide nothing for ninety days and to spend the ramp-up period on a listening tour instead.

So she did. Over eleven weeks she sat with the frontline staff, not just the directors, and asked what was genuinely broken. One coordinator explained that the last three reorganizations had each been announced as urgent and then quietly reversed, so the team had learned to wait out any new plan. Another admitted that morale was low, but insisted that the cause was not the workload — it was that nobody in head office had ever asked them anything before deciding. A regional director warned her not to mistake their politeness for agreement, and pointed out that a Canadian team will rarely say no to your face.

None of this could have reached her except from one vantage point — the classroom floor. The temptation the whole time was to act, because a mandate feels like something you are supposed to spend, and every week she did not restructure anything felt like wasted authority. But credibility, she was learning, is not spent with the title. It is earned in the first quarter, one honest conversation at a time.

By day eighty-nine she had a smaller, sharper plan than the one she would have written on day one. She had learned to lean on Gordon, whose early mistakes were now her shortcuts, and to size up a culture before trying to move it. Her entry plan had three phases — learn, decide, act — and she was still, deliberately, in the first. When a stakeholder asked why she had not fixed anything yet, she no longer heard a threat. She heard her cue to say, plainly, that she was still listening, on purpose, and that the listening was the work.

1. Gordon “told her that he had been wrong” — why told her and not said her?
A Because tell takes a person object (tell someone), while say does not: you tell her that... but you say that... (or say to her).
B Because “said” cannot be used in the past tense at all.
C Because “told” is only correct when the sentence is a question.
2. The coordinator “explained that the reorganizations had been reversed”. Why had been and not were?
A Because “reorganizations” is plural, which forces the past perfect.
B Because reported speech backshifts one step: an event already past at the moment of speaking moves from were reversed to had been reversed.
C Because the passive voice always requires “had” in English.
3. Why is “He admitted that he had been wrong” stronger, in a debrief, than “He said that he had been wrong”?
A Because “admitted” is more formal and formality is always better.
B Because the reporting verb names how it was said: admit tells you he conceded a fault, which say would leave out. Choosing the verb is choosing the meaning.
C Because “said” is grammatically incorrect before “that”.
4. According to the text, how is credibility earned in a new senior role?
A It arrives automatically with the title and the mandate.
B By changing something visible in the first two weeks to look decisive.
C In the first quarter, one honest conversation at a time — by listening before acting, not by spending the mandate fast.

Stage 1.4: Grammar Tip -- Reported Speech & Reporting Verbs

GRAMMAR

The grammar of the leader who relays what a room told her — precisely, and without turning someone else's words into her own claim. In a listening tour, reporting well IS the work.

FormUseExample
Backshift
verb + that + clause, one tense back		Reporting a past moment, each tense steps back: is→was, was→had been.“Morale is low” → She said that morale was low. · “It was reversed” → He explained it had been reversed.
Say vs telltell takes a person (tell me); say does not (say that).He told me that... · He said that... · NOT “he said me”.
Verb + object + to-infinitiveurge / advise / warn / ask / tell + someone + to + base verb. Negative: not to.He urged me to slow down. · He warned me not to restructure.
Verb + -ing / thatsuggest / recommend take a gerund or a that-clause — never to + verb.She recommended waiting. · She suggested that I wait. · NOT “recommended to wait”.
Choose the reporting verbadmit (concede a fault) · insist (hold firm) · point out (note a fact) · warn (flag a risk) · deny (reject) · explain (give the reason). The verb carries the attitude that say leaves out.
Why it sounds seniorRelaying a listening tour with the right reporting verbs shows you heard people precisely, without turning their words into your own verdict. “A director warned me not to mistake politeness for agreement” is exact and attributed; “they all agree” is your assumption — and the wrong one.

Watch out (the number one slip): say takes no person and tell requires one. “He said me” → “He told me” or “He said that”.

Watch out (the other slip): after recommend / suggest comes the -ing form or a that-clause, never to. “recommended to wait” → “recommended waiting”.

The line that wins the room: the precise reporting verb plus a clean backshift. “One coordinator explained that the reorganizations had all been reversed, so the team learned to wait — and another insisted that the real problem was never being asked.”

Stage 1.5: Fill in the Blank

Practice

Complete each sentence with the correct form. Tap Listen to hear the full sentence.

"Gordon me that he had restructured the academic team too fast."
"He that he had been wrong about almost all of it."
"Ruth urged me a simple entry plan with three phases."
"The regional director warned me mistake their politeness for agreement."
"One coordinator explained that the reorganizations all been quietly reversed."
"She recommended until the listening tour was finished before changing anything."

Stage 2: Put the First 90 Days in Order

Order

Put the five moves in the order a new leader runs the first quarter — listen first, decide last.

?Report back precisely what people said, using reported speech, without inventing a verdict of your own.
?On day ninety, present a smaller, sharper plan built on what you heard, not on what you assumed on day one.
?Build a simple entry plan with three phases — learn, decide, act — and commit to staying in phase one.
?Earn credibility before spending the mandate — resist changing anything visible just to look decisive.
?Run a listening tour with the frontline staff, not just the directors, and ask what you must not touch.

Stage 3: Pronunciation

Speaking

Listen to each sentence, then record yourself saying it. These are sentences you will actually use when you brief the board on your first quarter.

Gordon told me he had restructured the team in his first month, and that he had been wrong about almost all of it.
He urged me to decide nothing for ninety days and to spend the ramp-up period on a listening tour.
One coordinator explained that past reorganizations had all been quietly reversed, so the team waits out new plans.
The regional director warned me not to mistake their politeness for agreement.
Credibility is not spent with the title; it is earned in the first quarter, one honest conversation at a time.

Stage 4: Situational Quiz

Quiz

Choose the best answer for each real moment in your first ninety days leading the Canadian division.

A stakeholder says: “You have been here six weeks and changed nothing. When do you start leading?” The most senior answer is:
A “You are right — I will announce a restructure this week so you can see progress.”
B “I am leading. I am on a listening tour by design, not by hesitation — I told the team I would decide nothing for ninety days, and keeping that is the first decision.”
C “I am not sure yet. Maybe I will change something soon, maybe not.”
The board asks you to relay what the frontline staff told you. Which is the precise report?
A “One coordinator explained that past reorganizations had all been reversed, and another admitted that morale was low but insisted the cause was never being asked.”
B “Basically they all agree the place is a mess and want me to fix it fast.”
C “They said me that everything was fine, more or less.”
Which sentence uses reported speech correctly?
A “Gordon said me that he restructured too fast.”
B “Ruth recommended to wait until the tour was finished.”
C “Gordon told me that he had restructured too fast, and Ruth recommended waiting until the tour was finished.”
The Canadian team is unfailingly polite and agrees with everything. You should:
A Take the agreement at face value and move fast — they clearly support the plan.
B Remember that a director warned you not to mistake politeness for agreement, and read the room from their vantage point — they will rarely say no to your face.
C Assume the silence means resistance and cancel the plan entirely.
You have a huge mandate on day one. The strongest use of it is to:
A Spend it immediately on the biggest visible change, before anyone questions your authority.
B Hold it while you earn credibility one conversation at a time — a mandate spent before you understand the question buys the wrong change.
C Hand it back to Gordon and ask him to keep running the division.

Stage 5: Free Production

Reflection

Record yourself answering the question below. Speak for two or three minutes, with no script.

You are briefing the board at the end of your first ninety days leading the Canadian division. In two minutes: describe your entry plan and your listening tour, then relay what three different people told you — use reported speech with three different reporting verbs (explained that... / admitted that... / warned me not to...) and a clean backshift. Use at least six words from this lesson, and end with the one thing you will do first, now that you have earned the credibility to do it.

Survival Card -- Lesson 12

1Gordon told me he had restructured the team in his first month, and that he had been wrong about almost all of it.
2He urged me to decide nothing for ninety days and to spend the ramp-up period on a listening tour.
3One coordinator explained that past reorganizations had all been quietly reversed, so the team waits out new plans.
4The regional director warned me not to mistake their politeness for agreement.
5Credibility is not spent with the title; it is earned in the first quarter, one honest conversation at a time.
Lesson 13 -- Pre-class

The Unwritten Rules -- Rapport and Etiquette in the Canadian Office

Decoding the etiquette no document gives you: the small talk that has to come before business, the flat, egalitarian way people speak to their VP, the punctuality that is expected not admired, the understatement that hides a real objection. And the grammar to voice your own adaptation without sounding like you are complaining. Key words: rapport, small talk, egalitarian, cordiality, punctuality, formality, understatement, code-switching, etiquette, reciprocity, to break the ice, to read the room, to hit it off, to save face, the unwritten rules. Structure: wish / if only — wish + past simple (I wish the team made...), wish + past perfect (I wish I had asked...), wish + would (I wish people wouldn't...), if only for emphasis. The verb steps back in time because the sentence admits: this is not how things are, this is how I would want them.
0%
▼

Stage 1.1: Vocabulary Cards

Vocabulary

Listen to each term and read the example. This is the vocabulary of the office culture nobody writes into a handbook.

Rapport -- an easy, trusting connection built with someone through repeated warm contact
"In Brazil the rapport came fast; here it is built more slowly, mostly in the small talk before the meeting starts."
Small talk -- light, informal conversation that builds a connection before the real business starts
"The six minutes of small talk are not filler here; they are how the team warms up and how trust is earned."
Egalitarian -- treating everyone as equals, with little visible distance between senior and junior people
"The office is strikingly egalitarian: the VP is on a first-name basis with everyone and lines up for coffee like the rest of us."
Cordiality -- friendly, polite warmth in professional dealings, without being close friends
"The cordiality is real but measured; people are warm to you long before they are actually close to you."
Punctuality -- the habit of starting and finishing exactly on time
"Punctuality is not a virtue you get credit for here; it is the baseline, and arriving late reads as disrespect."
Formality -- the degree of official, reserved behavior a setting expects, in titles, dress and tone
"I arrived with Brazilian meeting formality and here it created the opposite of respect, a distance I did not intend."
Understatement -- deliberately describing something as smaller or milder than it really is
"'It's a bit of a concern' was an understatement; it meant the whole plan was in trouble, and I read it too literally."
Code-switching -- adjusting how you speak or behave to fit a different cultural setting
"I already code-switch between Portuguese and English; now I am learning to code-switch between two office cultures."
Etiquette -- the unwritten code of polite behavior expected in a particular group
"There is a whole etiquette here that no onboarding document mentions, and breaking it quietly costs you."
Reciprocity -- the give-and-take of trust and favors that keeps a relationship balanced
"The reciprocity is real: invest in the small talk first, and people invest back in you."
To break the ice -- to ease the initial awkwardness when people do not yet know each other
"A question about the weekend is how you break the ice here, and the meeting goes better for it."
To read the room -- to sense the unspoken mood and dynamics of a group before you act
"I have to read the room, not just the words, because the real objection is rarely said out loud."
To hit it off -- to form a warm rapport with someone very quickly
"I hit it off with the operations lead over coffee, and that one conversation opened three doors."
To save face -- to protect your own or someone else's dignity in front of others
"I raised the problem privately so the coordinator could save face, and she fixed it the same day."
The unwritten rules -- the implicit norms everyone is expected to follow without being told
"The unwritten rules are where the real culture lives, and they are exactly what no document ever gives you."

Stage 1.2: Matching

Practice

Match each term with the correct definition.

Rapport
Small talk
Egalitarian
Cordiality
Punctuality
Formality
Understatement
Code-switching
Etiquette
Reciprocity
To break the ice
To read the room
To hit it off
To save face
The unwritten rules

Stage 1.3: Grammar in Context

GRAMMAR

Read the text and answer the questions.

On her first Monday in the Toronto office, Danielle walked into the nine o'clock meeting with her agenda open and ready. Nobody else opened one. For six minutes the room talked about the weekend, the snow, someone's dog. She waited, a little impatient, and then did the efficient thing: she started on item one. She could feel the temperature of the room drop. It took her three days and a quiet word from Nathan to understand why. In Brazil, getting straight to business is a way of respecting people's time. Here, skipping the small talk does not read as focused. It reads as cold, and the rapport she needed was being built in exactly the minutes she had tried to skip.

There is a way to talk about a frustration like that which keeps you inside the culture instead of standing outside it, judging — and it is a grammar. She did not say “these meetings are inefficient”. She said, “I wish the meetings got to the point a little faster — but the small talk is where the trust is built, so I'm learning to break the ice first.” Notice the verb. She means now, yet the verb is got, in the past. That backward step is the whole point: it is English admitting this is not how things are; this is how I would want them. And by the time she adds what she is already doing about it, she is no longer a newcomer complaining. She is a leader adapting.

The etiquette kept surprising her. The office was strikingly egalitarian — the VP went by her first name and stood in the coffee line like everyone else — so the formality Danielle had arrived with created distance, not respect. And the disagreement was almost never on the table. When a colleague called her plan “interesting” and “not quite there yet”, that was an understatement: a real objection wearing a polite coat. “If only I had read the room,” she told Nathan afterwards, “I would have caught it.” The regret takes had read, the past perfect, because she is looking back and wanting a different past — and naming a mistake that cleanly, without drowning in it, is one of the most senior things a leader can do.

The rule that took the longest to trust was reciprocity. The cordiality was real but measured; people were warm long before they were close. And there was one thing she wanted other people to change, not just survive: “I wish people wouldn't read my silence as agreement.” When she went quiet, she was weighing something, not consenting. So she began saying “let me think on that out loud” — it let everyone save face and it kept things clear. None of this meant she was becoming someone else. She was code-switching, the same way she already switched between Portuguese and English. And Nathan was right: within a month, she had hit it off with the team, because she had learned the one thing no handbook teaches — that the culture lives in the unwritten rules, and you read them by wishing out loud what you would change, and adapting while you do.

1. Why does “I wish the meetings got to the point faster” use “got” and not “get”, even though she means now?
A Because the meetings got faster in the past and are slow again now.
B Because after “wish”, a present situation you want to be different steps back into the past simple. The backward verb is English admitting this is not reality — it is what she would want.
C Because “wish” can only be followed by an irregular verb.
2. Mistake number one of this lesson: why is “I wish I would have asked about the etiquette” wrong?
A A regret about the past takes the past perfect: “I wish I had asked”. “Would have asked” is the classic error — save “would” for a behavior in others you want changed.
B Because “etiquette” cannot be the object of “ask about”.
C Because a regret must always use the present perfect, “have asked”.
3. Mistake number two: why is “I wish people will stop reading my silence as agreement” wrong?
A Because “silence” is uncountable and cannot be read.
B When you want SOMEONE ELSE to change a behavior, wish takes would, not “will”: “I wish people wouldn't read my silence as agreement.”
C Because the sentence should be in the passive voice.
4. What did the colleague actually mean by calling the plan “interesting” and “not quite there yet”?
A That the plan was genuinely intriguing and almost finished.
B It was an understatement — a real objection said softly. In this culture the disagreement is often in the room, not on the table, so she had to read the room, not the words.
C That the plan had already been approved by the VP.
5. What is the difference between the newcomer who resists a culture and the leader who adapts to it?
A The leader hides every frustration and pretends everything is fine.
B The newcomer says “this is wrong” and stands outside judging. The leader says “I wish it worked differently, and here is what I am doing meanwhile” — same frustration, but inside the culture, working with it.
C The leader simply imposes the Brazilian way until the team adjusts.

Stage 1.4: Grammar Tip -- Wish & If Only

GRAMMAR

“Wish” and “if only” let you name a frustration precisely without complaining. The trick is that the verb after them steps BACKWARD in time — that backward step is the language marking “this is not how things are”. Which tense you step back to depends on what you mean.

You meanFormExample (your Canadian office)
1. A present situation you want to be differentwish + past simpleI wish the team made more small talk.
2. Same, more formal (all persons)wish + wereI wish I were further along with the etiquette.
3. A regret about the pastwish + past perfect (had + p.p.)I wish I had asked about the etiquette on day one.
4. You want someone else to change a behaviorwish + would + base verbI wish people wouldn't read my silence as agreement.
5. Emphatic wish or regretif only + the same tense rules: If only I had read the room.

Watch out (mistake number 1): after “wish”, a present you want changed goes into the past simple. “I wish I know the rules” is WRONG → “I wish I knew the rules”. Portuguese uses a present/subjunctive here, and translating literally keeps the verb in the present. English needs the backward step.

Watch out (mistake number 2): a past regret takes the past perfect, never “would have”. “I wish I would have asked” → “I wish I had asked”. Keep would for a behavior in OTHERS you want changed — and there it is “would”, never “will”: “I wish they would listen”.

Why this matters to you: “This office is wrong” puts you outside the culture, judging it — and no one lets an outsider change anything. “I wish it worked differently, and here is what I am doing meanwhile” puts you inside it, adapting. Same frustration, opposite impression. The backward-stepping verb is what signals you know it is your wish, not their failing.

The three-move reframe: WISH (name the frustration, verb in the past) → OWN (what you are already doing about it) → INVITE (open the door without imposing your norm). Skip the middle move and it is a complaint; skip the last and it is a demand.

Stage 1.5: Fill in the Blank

Practice

Complete each sentence with the correct form after wish / if only. Tap Listen to hear the full sentence.

"I wish the team more small talk before we start."
"I wish I about the etiquette on day one."
"I wish people read my silence as agreement."
"If only I the room, I would have caught the understatement."
"I wish I further along with the unwritten rules, but I am getting there."
"I wish the meetings on time, so I model the punctuality myself."

Stage 2: Put the Reframe in Order

Order

Put the five moves in the order that turns a frustration about the Canadian office into a leader's adaptation.

?Own the adaptation you are already making: so I am learning to break the ice first and treat the small talk as where the rapport is built.
?Close on reciprocity, not resentment: the more I invest in the culture first, the more the team invests back, and I am code-switching into it, not resisting it.
?Name the frustration as a wish, verb stepped back in time: I wish the meetings got to the point a little faster.
?Invite the other person without imposing your norm: and when you do have a real concern, I would genuinely rather you told me plainly.
?Acknowledge the reason the rule exists here: but the small talk is not filler, it is how this team builds trust.

Stage 3: Pronunciation

Speaking

Listen to each sentence, then record yourself saying it. These five carry a frustration into an adaptation without a single complaint.

I wish the meetings got to the point faster, but the small talk is where the rapport is built, so I break the ice first.
I wish I had asked about the etiquette on day one, because I read the formality of this office wrong.
I wish people wouldn't read my silence as agreement, so I now say let me think on that out loud.
If only I had read the room, I would have caught the understatement behind the word interesting.
The reciprocity here is real: I invest in the small talk first, and the team invests back. I am code-switching, not resisting.

Stage 4: Situational Quiz

Quiz

Choose the best answer for each real moment of your first month in the Canadian office.

Your VP asks, in a 1:1, how you are settling in. The most senior answer is:
A “Honestly, everything here is so indirect and slow. It is frustrating.”
B “It's an adjustment. I wish I had asked about the etiquette on day one, but I am learning fast — I break the ice first now, and I read the room for the understatement instead of waiting for a direct no.”
C “Everything is perfect, no problems at all.”
Which sentence uses “wish” CORRECTLY?
A “I wish I know the unwritten rules of this office.”
B “I wish I would have understood the formality here sooner.”
C “I wish I had understood the formality here sooner, and I wish people wouldn't read my silence as agreement.”
A colleague says your proposal is “interesting” and “might need a bit more thought”. You should:
A Read it as a real objection wearing a polite coat, and ask one follow-up: “I want to make sure I read the room right — what would make it stronger?”
B Take it as approval and move the proposal forward.
C Tell them to stop being indirect and just say what they mean.
You want the team to stop treating your thinking pauses as consent. The best way to voice it is:
A “Stop assuming I agree just because I am quiet.”
B “I wish people wouldn't read my silence as agreement — when I go quiet I am weighing something. So I've started saying ‘let me think on that out loud’ to keep it clear.”
C “It is not my fault you misread me.”
Your mentor in Brazil asks what you would change about your first week. The answer that reads as senior is:
A “Nothing, it was fine.”
B “If only I had understood that the culture lives in the small things — the small talk, the first names, the understatement — not in the org chart. I read the policies before I came; I wish I had asked about the unwritten rules.”
C “I would have told them how we do it in Brazil on day one.”

Stage 5: Free Production

Reflection

Record yourself answering the prompt below. Speak for two or three minutes, with no script.

It is your first 1:1 with your Canadian VP, three weeks into the move. She asks how you are settling into the office culture. Name two or three things you wish were different about the way this office works — each as a proper wish, with the verb stepped back in time (past simple for a present you want changed, had + past participle for a regret, would for a behavior in others) — and pair every wish with the adaptation you are already making. Close warmly: you are adjusting, not regretting the move. Use wish and if only correctly throughout, and at least six words from this lesson (rapport, small talk, egalitarian, understatement, etiquette, to read the room, to code-switch, reciprocity).

Survival Card -- Lesson 13

1I wish the meetings got to the point faster, but the small talk is where the rapport is built, so I break the ice first.
2I wish I had asked about the etiquette on day one, because I read the formality of this office wrong.
3I wish people wouldn't read my silence as agreement, so I now say let me think on that out loud.
4If only I had read the room, I would have caught the understatement behind the word interesting.
5The reciprocity here is real: I invest in the small talk first, and the team invests back. I am code-switching, not resisting.
Lesson 14 -- Pre-class

The Coaching 1:1 -- Developing People, Not Managing Them

Running a developmental one-on-one with a talented mentee who is losing confidence and close to quitting: listen before you advise, reframe “I am failing” into “I am on a learning curve”, find the growth edge, and give the guidance that develops instead of the order that shrinks. Key words: developmental feedback, growth edge, coachable moment, active listening, reframing, sounding board, stretch assignment, coaching mindset, boundary setting, career aspirations, to empower, to check in, to play to someone's strengths, to bite the bullet, to throw in the towel. Structure: advanced modals -- would rather / had better / may as well (I would rather we solved the root cause / you had better talk to your manager before the deadline slips / if you are redoing it anyway, you may as well show what you learned).
0%
▼

Stage 1.1: Vocabulary Cards

Vocabulary

Listen to each term and read the example. This is the language of a leader who develops people in a one-on-one — the difference between telling a mentee what to do and coaching them into doing it themselves.

Developmental feedback -- input aimed at helping someone grow, not at correcting a single past error
"She reframed the review as developmental feedback, so Owen heard a path forward instead of a verdict on his past."
Growth edge -- the next skill or capacity a person is just ready to stretch into
"His growth edge was not effort; it was asking for help before a deadline slipped, and she named it gently."
Coachable moment -- a short opening when someone is genuinely open to learning something
"The moment Owen admitted he was struggling was a coachable moment, and she did not waste it with advice he had not asked for."
Active listening -- listening fully to understand the person, not to prepare your reply
"Active listening meant she let the silence sit until Owen said the thing he had almost swallowed."
Reframing -- offering a different, more useful way to see the same situation
"Instead of correcting him, she tried reframing: he was not failing, he was on the steep part of a learning curve."
Sounding board -- a trusted person you think out loud with to test your own ideas
"She offered to be a sounding board, not a judge, so he could think out loud without defending himself."
Stretch assignment -- a task set deliberately beyond someone's comfort zone to build capacity
"The stretch assignment was deliberately a size too big, because that is where he would actually grow."
Coaching mindset -- leading by asking questions instead of handing over answers
"A coaching mindset asks ‘what would it look like if...?’ before it ever offers an answer."
Boundary setting -- making your limits clear, kindly and firmly, so expectations stay realistic
"Boundary setting is not coldness; it is telling someone honestly what you can and cannot take on."
Career aspirations -- where a person genuinely hopes their career will go over time
"Once she understood his real career aspirations, the work she gave him finally pointed somewhere he wanted to go."
To empower -- to give someone the authority and the confidence to act on their own
"The goal of the 1:1 was not to fix Owen but to empower him to solve the next problem without her."
To check in -- to briefly and deliberately ask how someone is really doing
"She had made a habit of checking in before a deadline, not after, so problems surfaced while they were still small."
To play to someone's strengths -- to give someone work that uses what they are already best at
"Rather than force him into spreadsheets, she chose to play to his strengths and let him run the interviews."
To bite the bullet -- to face a hard, unavoidable task instead of putting it off
"Some conversations you can only postpone for so long; eventually you bite the bullet and name the issue."
To throw in the towel -- to give up and stop trying
"He had been ready to throw in the towel, and one honest hour was the difference between quitting and staying."

Stage 1.2: Matching

Practice

Match each term with the correct definition.

Developmental feedback
Growth edge
Coachable moment
Active listening
Reframing
Sounding board
Stretch assignment
Coaching mindset
Boundary setting
Career aspirations
To empower
To check in
To play to someone's strengths
To bite the bullet
To throw in the towel

Stage 1.3: Grammar in Context

GRAMMAR

Read the text and answer the questions.

Owen, a junior coordinator Danielle mentors, had gone quiet for two weeks — deadlines slipping, replies getting shorter. In a voice note the night before their 1:1, he all but said he was ready to throw in the towel. Danielle knew the easy move was to fix it for him. She chose the harder one: a coaching mindset. She opened the meeting not with a verdict but with a question, and then did the thing that is hardest for a fast, capable leader — active listening, letting the silence sit until he named what was really wrong.

What came out was not laziness. Owen was drowning in tasks that did not play to his strengths, and he had been too proud to check in before things slipped. So she tried reframing: “You are not failing — you are on the steep part of the learning curve.” Then she guided, without commanding. “I would rather we solved the root cause together than patch the same problem next month. And honestly, you had better talk to your manager before the next deadline slips, not after — it is far easier now.” When Owen admitted he would have to redo a whole report anyway, she smiled: “If you are redoing it regardless, you may as well use it to show exactly what you have learned.”

This was the coachable moment, and she did not fill it with orders. She offered to be a sounding board, gave him developmental feedback instead of a scorecard, and named his real growth edge: not effort, but asking for help early. She set a stretch assignment just beyond his comfort — running the next round of interviews — because that is where growth actually happens, and it pointed straight at his career aspirations.

The point of the hour was never to rescue Owen. It was to empower him to solve the next problem without her. She modelled boundary setting too — telling him plainly what she could and could not take off his plate — so the help stayed real. He walked in ready to quit and walked out with one honest task and a reason to stay. Sometimes you have to bite the bullet and have the conversation; more often, you just have to ask the right question and then be quiet enough to hear the answer.

1. “I would rather we solved the root cause than patch it again” — what does would rather express here?
A A clear preference between two options — and with a different subject (we) it takes the past form (solved).
B An obligation, exactly the same in meaning as “must”.
C A question asking Owen for permission to continue.
2. “You had better talk to your manager before the deadline slips” — why had better and not just should?
A Because had better is only about the past — it means “it would have been better”.
B Because had better is stronger than should: it is urgent advice that carries a warning — do it, or something bad follows — and it is followed by the bare verb (talk), no to.
C Because had better can only be used with the word please.
3. “If you are redoing it anyway, you may as well use it to show what you learned.” What is the idea behind may as well?
A That the action is forbidden and should be avoided.
B That since there is no better alternative, you might as well do the thing — it costs nothing extra and there is an upside.
C That the speaker is unsure whether the action is even possible.
4. According to the text, what was the real point of Danielle's 1:1 with Owen?
A To take the difficult tasks off his plate and solve the problem for him.
B To deliver a firm scorecard so he knew exactly where he had fallen short.
C To empower him to solve the next problem himself — by listening, reframing, and naming his growth edge instead of giving orders.

Stage 1.4: Grammar Tip -- Advanced Modals: would rather / had better / may as well

GRAMMAR

The modals that shift you from “making a suggestion” to “executive guidance” — warm, but directive. This is how a senior leader advises without commanding, and guides without shrinking the other person.

FormUseExample
would rather
+ bare infinitive		A clear preference (same subject). No to.I would rather grow you than replace you. · NOT “would rather to grow”.
would rather + subject
+ past form		A preference about what someone else does — the verb goes to the past.I would rather you didn't promise that date. · I would rather we solved the root cause.
had better
+ bare infinitive		Strong, urgent advice with a warning: do it, or a bad result follows. Negative: had better not.You had better loop in the team first. · You had better not wait until it slips.
may / might as well
+ bare infinitive		No better option exists, so you might as well do it — low cost, clear upside.If you are redoing it anyway, you may as well show what you learned.
Advisory subjunctive
suggest / recommend / insist + that + base verb		Formal directive language: the verb stays in the base form (no -s, be not is).I suggest that each mentee be given a clear growth edge. · I recommend that he take the interviews.
Why it sounds seniorwould rather owns a preference without an order; had better lands a warning without raising your voice; may as well makes a decision feel easy. Together they are the tone of a leader who is empathetic and precise at the same time — guidance, not commands.

Watch out (the number one slip): all three take the bare infinitive — no to. “You had better to talk” → “You had better talk”. “I would rather to reframe” → “I would rather reframe”.

Watch out (the other slip): with a different subject, would rather takes the past form. “I would rather you don't” → “I would rather you didn't”.

The line that wins the room: “I would rather grow you than replace you — but you had better ask for help before the next deadline, and if you are redoing the report anyway, you may as well use it to show me what changed.”

Stage 1.5: Fill in the Blank

Practice

Complete each sentence with the correct form. Tap Listen to hear the full sentence.

"I would rather this issue now than let it drift into next quarter."
"You had loop in the academic team before you commit to that date."
"If the meeting is happening anyway, we use it to reset expectations."
"I would rather you promise a date we cannot realistically hit."
"You had better not until the deadline slips to ask for help."
"I suggest that each mentee given a clear growth edge for the quarter."

Stage 2: Put the Coaching 1:1 in Order

Order

Put the five moves in the order a leader runs a developmental one-on-one — listen first, empower last.

?Reframe the story: “You are not failing — you are on the steep part of the learning curve.”
?Set a stretch assignment that plays to their strengths, and empower them to run it without you.
?Open with a real question, not a verdict, and use active listening — let the silence sit.
?Name the growth edge and guide, not command: “I would rather we solved the root cause; you had better ask early next time.”
?Catch the coachable moment when they admit the real problem, and stay a sounding board, not a judge.

Stage 3: Pronunciation

Speaking

Listen to each sentence, then record yourself saying it. These are sentences you will actually use in your next mentoring 1:1 — warm, but directive.

I would rather we solved the root cause together than patch the same problem again next month.
You had better talk to your manager before the deadline slips, not after -- it is far easier now.
If you are going to redo the report anyway, you may as well use it to show what you have learned.
Help me understand what got in the way -- I am not here to judge it, I am here to think it through with you.
You are not failing; you are on the steep part of the learning curve, and I would rather grow you than replace you.

Stage 4: Situational Quiz

Quiz

Choose the best answer for each real moment in a developmental one-on-one.

A mentee says, “I am just not good at this. Maybe I should quit.” The most developmental response is:
A “You are right, it is not for everyone — let us talk about an exit.”
B “Help me understand what got in the way. You are not failing — you are on the steep part of the learning curve, and I would rather grow you than replace you.”
C “Just try harder and it will sort itself out.”
You want to give urgent advice that carries a real warning, without shouting. You say:
A “You had better to talk to your manager today.”
B “You had better talk to your manager before the deadline slips — it is far easier now than later.”
C “Maybe, if you feel like it, you could possibly mention it sometime.”
Which sentence uses the advanced modals correctly?
A “I would rather to reframe the problem, and you had better to ask early.”
B “I would rather you don't promise a date, and we may as well to start today.”
C “I would rather you didn't promise a date, and since we are meeting anyway, we may as well start today.”
A mentee is drowning in tasks that do not suit them. The coaching move is to:
A Add more tasks so they toughen up and stop complaining.
B Play to their strengths — give a stretch assignment that uses what they are best at — and empower them to run it.
C Quietly do the tasks yourself so nothing slips this quarter.
Your mentee opens up about being close to giving up. The strongest use of that moment is to:
A Fill the silence with a list of instructions before they change their mind.
B Treat it as a coachable moment — stay a sounding board, name their real growth edge, and offer developmental feedback, not a scorecard.
C Note it for the performance review and move on to the next agenda item.

Stage 5: Free Production

Reflection

Record yourself answering the question below. Speak for two or three minutes, with no script.

Run a two-minute mock coaching 1:1 out loud. Your mentee has gone quiet and is close to throwing in the towel. Open with a real question, reframe their story, name one growth edge, and guide them with the advanced modals — use “I would rather...”, “you had better...” and “you may as well...” at least once each, plus at least six words from this lesson. Tone: warm, but directive.

Survival Card -- Lesson 14

1I would rather we solved the root cause together than patch the same problem again next month.
2You had better talk to your manager before the deadline slips, not after -- it is far easier now.
3If you are going to redo the report anyway, you may as well use it to show what you have learned.
4Help me understand what got in the way -- I am not here to judge it, I am here to think it through with you.
5You are not failing; you are on the steep part of the learning curve, and I would rather grow you than replace you.
Lesson 15 -- Pre-class

Full Simulation -- Danielle Leads the Room (Final Review)

The final review, and the debut it prepares you for: your first high-stakes meeting with the Canadian Maple Bear leadership. This is a preview of the simulation -- no new content, everything reactivated. Reload the executive toolkit (mandate, stakeholder, buy-in, framework, credibility, psychological safety, developmental feedback, actionable insight, groundwork, to move the needle, to read the room, to empower) and the whole grammar map -- future perfect, past perfect, advanced modals, wish / if only, third conditional and cleft sentences -- in the exact sentences you will say when you open the meeting, present your People & Culture plan, and answer the two questions you did not prepare for. A celebration and a test: this is where you sound like the same senior leader in English that you already are in Portuguese.
0%
▼

Stage 1.1: Vocabulary Cards

Vocabulary

Your executive toolkit, reactivated. You have met every one of these before -- listen, read the example, and recall where each one lands in the meeting.

Mandate -- the authority and remit you were given to act on
"Let me frame my mandate: I was brought in to lead the cultural transformation of the Canadian division."
Stakeholder -- anyone with a stake in your work whose support you need
"Every stakeholder in that room has read the Brazil numbers; my job is to turn interest into buy-in."
Buy-in -- genuine agreement and commitment to a plan, not just permission
"My predecessor rolled out the framework before he had the buy-in, so it never gained momentum."
Framework -- a structured set of principles you use to organize and act on a problem
"I am not importing a Brazilian framework; I am importing a diagnostic method and letting your context write the answers."
Credibility -- the trust and standing you build before you spend it on change
"I earn credibility by listening first, and only then do I move the needle."
Psychological safety -- the felt safety to speak up, disagree and admit a mistake without fear
"Success is the day a Canadian leader disagrees with me directly, because the psychological safety is real."
Developmental feedback -- input aimed at someone's growth, not a verdict on their past
"I give developmental feedback that grows a person instead of shrinking them."
Actionable insight -- a conclusion from data that you can actually act on
"I tell a story with the data and tease out the actionable insight without reading too much into it."
Groundwork -- the early, unglamorous preparation that makes later change possible
"I would rather build on the groundwork my predecessor laid than start over."
To move the needle -- to make real, measurable progress on something that matters
"The fastest way to move the needle is to hear the real objection now, not after we launch."
To read the room -- to sense the unspoken mood of a group before you act
"I read 'ambitious' as a polite objection, so I read the room and asked one follow-up."
To empower -- to give someone the authority and confidence to act on their own
"I am not there to rescue the team; I am there to empower them to solve the next problem without me."

Stage 1.2: Matching

Practice

Match each term with the correct definition.

Mandate
Stakeholder
Buy-in
Framework
Credibility
Psychological safety
Developmental feedback
Actionable insight
Groundwork
To move the needle
To read the room
To empower

Stage 1.3: Grammar in Context

GRAMMAR

Read the text and answer the questions. This is a review -- watch every structure you built doing its one job in the room.

It is nine o'clock on a Thursday in Toronto, and for the first time the room Danielle is opening is not in Portuguese. The VP has handed her the meeting, and she begins where a leader begins: with the mandate. “I was brought in to lead the cultural transformation of the Canadian division,” she says, and then, without apologising for the size of it, “by the end of my first year I will have run a full diagnostic and rolled out a new onboarding experience.” The future perfect is doing exactly one job there: projecting quiet confidence about what will already be true. Fifteen weeks ago, that sentence would have come out as a hope. Tonight it comes out as a plan.

A regional director asks the fair, sharp question: how is this different from what her predecessor tried and dropped? She does not trash him. “He had rolled out the framework before he had the buy-in,” she explains — the past perfect sequencing the backstory cleanly — “so it never gained momentum.” Then the cleft, placing the emphasis exactly where she wants it: “What I am changing is the sequence, not the destination.” And the advanced modal that guides without commanding: “I would rather build on the groundwork he laid than start over.” Same destination, a different order of operations; she earns credibility first, and only then does she move the needle.

The academic lead, a doctor, pushes back: a Brazil-designed framework may not transfer here. She concedes the fair part — “I take the point about cultural distance” — and holds her ground with a hedge, not a flat no: “where we may differ is on transfer.” If she had prescribed a Brazilian answer on day one, she would have lost the room; the third conditional lets her own that call without drowning in it. She is not importing conclusions, she says; she is importing a diagnostic method and letting their context read the room for itself.

Then Grant asks the question she could not prepare for: what does success look like for her, personally? She does not reach for the perfect sentence. She reaches for the true one. “Let me think on that out loud,” she says — the hedge that is honesty, not weakness — “I'm not fully sure yet how I'd measure it, but success is the day a leader here disagrees with me directly, because the psychological safety is real.” It is the whole program in one answer: developmental feedback, actionable insight, the confidence to empower a room to push back on her. Grant nods. “That's the answer. Not the polished one — the true one.” The English was only ever the vehicle. The leader was hers all along.

1. Why does “by year one I will have run a full diagnostic” use the future perfect?
A Because the diagnostic already finished last year.
B The future perfect (will have + past participle) projects confidence about what will already be true by a point in time — it turns a hope into a plan.
C Because “year” always requires the future perfect.
2. Why is “He had rolled out the framework before he had the buy-in” in the past perfect?
A The past perfect sequences the earlier action in a backstory: the roll-out happened before the (later) point she is describing, so it steps one tense further back.
B Because the sentence is a question.
C Because “framework” is an uncountable noun.
3. What does the cleft “What I am changing is the sequence, not the destination” achieve?
A It makes the sentence more polite by adding a filler.
B It spotlights the key idea — the sequence — and contrasts it with what is NOT changing, putting the emphasis exactly where she wants it.
C It puts the sentence into the passive voice.
4. When Grant asks the question she didn't prepare for, what does she do — and why is it senior?
A She hedges honestly (“let me think on that out loud… I'm not fully sure yet”) and then commits to the true part. Naming uncertainty and still committing is more senior than a polished but hollow answer.
B She invents a confident-sounding number to fill the silence.
C She refuses to answer and changes the subject.
5. What is the through-line of the whole simulation — the point of the program?
A That perfect grammar is what makes a room follow a leader.
B The English was only ever the vehicle: Danielle is the same senior leader in English that she already was in Portuguese — she saw people clearly and told the truth, and that is what the room follows.
C That she should run the Canadian division exactly as she ran Brazil, unchanged.

Stage 1.4: Grammar Tip -- The Whole Map, One Job Each

GRAMMAR

Nothing new here -- this is the consolidation. Across fifteen lessons you built these structures one at a time. In the room they are not rules to remember; each one simply does a single job. Read the map, then produce one sentence of your own with each structure.

StructureIts one jobExample (your Thursday meeting)
Future perfectProject confidence about what you will have deliveredBy year one I will have run a full diagnostic.
Past perfectSequence a backstory -- what had happened beforeHe had rolled out the framework before he had the buy-in.
Advanced modalsGuide and disagree without commandingI would rather build on it than start over.
Wish / if onlyVoice adaptation, not complaintI wish the objections came sooner, so I read the room instead.
Third conditionalOwn a past call without drowning in itIf I had prescribed, I would have lost the room.
Cleft sentencesPut the emphasis exactly where you want itWhat I am changing is the sequence, not the destination.
HedgingBe honest about uncertainty, then commit: “I'm not fully sure yet -- but here is what I am sure of.”

The only instruction for the room: do not reach for the perfect sentence — reach for the true one. Hedge honestly where you are genuinely unsure, then commit to what you know. The most senior thing in any language is a person who can say “I don't have a complete answer yet, and here is how I would find one.” You already do that in Portuguese; on Thursday you are just going to do it in English.

Stage 1.5: Fill in the Blank

Practice

Complete each sentence with the right structure -- one blank, one structure from the map. Tap Listen to hear the full sentence.

"By the end of my first year, I a full cultural diagnostic."
"He the framework before he had the buy-in."
"I build on the groundwork than start over."
"If I a Brazilian answer on day one, I would have lost the room."
" I am changing is the sequence, not the destination."
"I the objections came sooner, so I read the room instead."

Stage 2: Put the Meeting Opening in Order

Order

Put the five moves in the order that opens the Thursday meeting like a leader, from the mandate to the close.

?Contrast without blame, using a cleft: what I am changing is the sequence, not the destination -- I would rather build on the groundwork than start over.
?Answer the uncomfortable question with hedge-then-commit: let me think on that out loud -- I'm not fully sure yet, but here is what I am sure of.
?Frame the mandate without underselling it: I was brought in to lead the cultural transformation of this division.
?Concede the fair part, then hold your ground: I take the point about cultural distance, and where we may differ is on transfer.
?Project the plan with a future perfect: by the end of my first year I will have run a full diagnostic and rolled out a new onboarding experience.

Stage 3: Pronunciation

Speaking

Listen to each sentence, then record yourself saying it. These five are the ones you will recycle almost word for word in the real meeting.

Let me frame my mandate: I was brought in to lead the cultural transformation of this division, and by year one I will have run a full diagnostic.
What I am changing is the sequence, not the destination. I would rather build on the groundwork than start over.
I take the point about cultural distance, and where we may differ is on transfer. The method is neutral, even if the findings will not be.
I'm reading ambitious as a gentle way of saying you're not yet convinced. Tell me if I have that wrong, because I'd rather hear the real objection now.
Let me think on that out loud. I don't have a complete answer yet, and here is how I would find one.

Stage 4: Situational Quiz

Quiz

Choose the most senior answer for each moment of the Thursday leadership meeting.

Grant opens: “Tell the room what you were brought here to do.” The strongest opening is:
A “Well, I'll just try to help out with the culture stuff a bit, if that's okay.”
B “Let me frame my mandate: I was brought in to lead the cultural transformation of this division, and by year one I will have run a full diagnostic and rolled out a new onboarding experience.”
C “Honestly I'm still figuring out what my job even is.”
A director asks how this differs from what your predecessor dropped. The best answer:
A “My predecessor had no idea what he was doing, honestly.”
B “He laid real groundwork, and I would rather build on it than start over. What I'm changing is the sequence: he rolled out the framework before he had the buy-in, so it never gained momentum.”
C “It's exactly the same plan, just with me running it.”
The academic lead says a Brazil-designed framework may not transfer. You should:
A Concede the fair part and hold your ground with a hedge: “I take the point about cultural distance, and where we may differ is on transfer — I'm importing a method, not conclusions, and letting your context write the answers.”
B Say “No, you're wrong, it will transfer fine.”
C Drop the plan entirely to avoid the conflict.
Grant asks the question you didn't prepare for: “What does success look like for you, personally?” The most senior response:
A Invent a confident metric on the spot so you don't look unsure.
B “Let me think on that out loud. I'm not fully sure yet how I'd measure it, but success is the day a leader here disagrees with me directly, because the psychological safety is real.”
C “I'd rather not answer that one.”
A director you haven't won over says your plan is “ambitious”. You should:
A Read the understatement and surface it: “I'm reading ‘ambitious’ as a gentle way of saying you're not yet convinced — tell me if I have that wrong, because I'd rather hear the real objection now.”
B Say “Thanks!” and take it as a compliment.
C Tell them to stop being indirect and just say what they mean.

Stage 5: Free Production

Reflection

Record yourself answering the prompt below. Speak for two or three minutes, with no script.

This is your Thursday, for real. Open the meeting: frame your mandate (use a future perfect for what you will have delivered by year one), give a sixty-second overview of your People & Culture plan for the Canadian division, and then answer, out loud, the question Grant would ask you: “What does success look like for you, personally, in this role?” Reach for the true sentence, not the perfect one -- hedge honestly where you are unsure, then commit. Use at least three structures from the map (future perfect, advanced modal, cleft, third conditional, wish) and at least five words from the toolkit (mandate, buy-in, groundwork, credibility, psychological safety, to move the needle, to empower).

Survival Card -- Lesson 15

1Let me frame my mandate: I was brought in to lead the cultural transformation of this division, and by year one I will have run a full diagnostic.
2What I am changing is the sequence, not the destination. I would rather build on the groundwork than start over.
3I take the point about cultural distance, and where we may differ is on transfer. The method is neutral, even if the findings will not be.
4I'm reading ambitious as a gentle way of saying you're not yet convinced. Tell me if I have that wrong, because I'd rather hear the real objection now.
5Let me think on that out loud. I don't have a complete answer yet, and here is how I would find one.

Complementary Materials

Content to reinforce your learning outside class. Check it off when you finish.

Lesson 1 — Diagnostic Session -- Who Is Danielle in English?

Documentary
American Factory (Netflix)

A Chinese company takes over an American factory — and two organizational cultures collide in real time. This is literally your job, filmed: cultural artifacts, leadership behavior, and what gets lost when a model is imported with no diagnosis. Connection to Lesson 1: watch how each leader introduces and positions themselves — who sounds senior, who sounds defensive, and why.

Tip: watch with English subtitles only. Write down five organizational-culture terms in English that you would still say in your own language.

Watch ↗
Podcast
HBR IdeaCast — "The Leadership Skills That Make Transformation Stick"

Authentic executive vocabulary about exactly what you do: why culture transformation dies, and what senior leadership has to sustain for it to stick. Connection to Lesson 1: every word of this lesson (organizational culture, alignment, senior leadership, roll out, framework, stakeholder) shows up in native context and at real speed.

Tip: listen once, with no pauses. Then summarize the episode out loud in sixty seconds — record it and listen back. Use at least three words from the lesson.

Listen ↗
TED Talk
Julian Treasure — "How to Speak So That People Want to Listen" (TED)

Ten minutes on the mechanics of positioning: register, pace, timbre, and the seven habits that erode a speaker's authority. It is the “how” half of what this lesson worked on as the “what”. Connection to Lesson 1: you will hear, in native English, the difference between communicating and positioning yourself — which is the reason this programme exists.

Tip: after the video, record your ninety-second executive introduction again — this time paying attention to pace and pauses, not only to the words.

Watch on YouTube ↗

Lesson 2 — The Language of Culture -- Diagnosing and Describing Culture

Documentary
Downfall: The Case Against Boeing (Netflix)

The most expensive case of missing psychological safety ever documented: the engineers saw the problem, they said so, and the company culture taught everyone that speaking up came at a cost. Connection to Lesson 2: this is your cultural diagnosis at scale — disengagement, exit interviews nobody read, and an elephant in the room nobody named until it was too late.

Tip: watch with English subtitles only. Write down THREE third conditional sentences about what leadership should have done (“If they had..., they would have...”).

Watch ↗
Podcast
HBR IdeaCast — “Creating Psychological Safety in the Workplace” (Amy Edmondson)

The Harvard researcher who created the concept explains, in executive English and at native speed, what psychological safety is and what it is not — the exact distinction you will have to defend in front of the Canadian directors. Connection to Lesson 2: every word of this lesson (psychological safety, disengagement, core values, culture fit) shows up in native context.

Tip: listen once, with no pauses. Then summarize the episode out loud in sixty seconds, using at least one should have and one might have. Record it and listen back.

Listen ↗
TEDx Talk
Amy Edmondson — “Building a Psychologically Safe Workplace” (TEDxHGSE)

Eleven minutes with the full structure of a cultural diagnosis: the three leadership behaviors that produce psychological safety, and why “happy” teams can still be silent. Connection to Lesson 2: this is the argument you will use when Dr. Reid says the survey measures mood, not culture.

Tip: write down the hedging phrases she uses to say hard things without accusing anyone. Bring three of them to the next class.

Watch on YouTube ↗

Lesson 3 — Speaking Like a Senior -- Executive Communication in 1:1s and OKRs

Series
Succession — Season 1, Episode 1: “Celebration” (HBO Max)

The whole series is a course in executive register in English: people disagreeing with senior leaders inside the room, without apologizing for disagreeing. Connection to Lesson 3: notice HOW they open the sentence before they take down someone else’s idea — it is exactly the “Building on that, my take on this is different” you rehearsed here.

Tip: watch with English subtitles. Write down THREE push-back sentences — disagreeing without attacking — and one sentence that opens a proposal.

Watch ↗
Podcast
HBR IdeaCast — “Supercharge Your One-on-One Meetings” (Steven Rogelberg)

The researcher who studied thousands of 1:1s explains, in executive English and at native speed, how you prepare, run and close an individual meeting — and why most of them are a status report in disguise. Connection to Lesson 3: this is exactly the meeting you will run with each Canadian leader from August on, with the vocabulary (milestone, deliverable, accountability, buy-in) in native context.

Tip: listen once, with no pauses. Then summarize the episode out loud in sixty seconds, opening with “Let me cut to the chase” and using at least one mixed conditional. Record it and listen back.

Listen ↗
YouTube
John Doerr — “Why the secret to success is setting the right goals” (TED)

Twelve minutes with the man who brought OKRs to Google, in clear executive English. Connection to Lesson 3: it is the source of the whole vocabulary set of this lesson — objective, key result, milestone, accountability — and the argument you will use when the academic directors ask what all of it is for.

Tip: note how he structures a proposal (the number before the story). Bring two observations on HOW he opens and closes his points.

Watch on YouTube ↗

Lesson 4 — Influencing Without Authority -- Presenting Your People Strategy to Canadian Leadership

Series
The Diplomat — Season 1, Episode 1: “The Cinderella Thing” (Netflix)

Kate Wyler walks into rooms full of people who do not report to her — and she still has to get the decision to come out her way. It is influence without authority in its purest form, in the English of closed doors and high pressure. Connection to Lesson 4: notice HOW she builds an argument before she asks for anything — the data first, the ask at the end — and how she names the weakness in her own proposal before the other side can find it.

Tip: watch with English subtitles. Write down THREE lines in which somebody gets a decision without the formal power to demand it, and ONE line that opens a presentation with a piece of data.

Watch ↗
Podcast
HBR — “Building Influence Without Authority” (Coaching Real Leaders, Muriel Wilkins)

A real coaching session, recorded, with a leader who has to get an entire organization to adopt her strategy without a single direct report in the room. This is literally your August first, in executive English and at native speed. Connection to Lesson 4: the whole episode is the argument of this lesson — approval is not adoption, and a plan only moves when somebody in the room decides to carry it.

Tip: listen once, with no pauses. Then summarize the episode out loud in sixty seconds, opening with “What the data shows us is...” and using at least one inversion (“Never have I seen...”). Record it and listen back.

Listen ↗
YouTube
Adam Galinsky — “How to speak up for yourself” (TED, 15 min)

The Columbia professor who studies power and influence explains the mechanics of positioning yourself when you have no formal authority in the room — and why the very same sentence sounds arrogant from one person and legitimate from another. Connection to Lesson 4: it is the theory behind what you practiced today, and the answer to the question that will come up in August: how to disagree with a Canadian director of twenty years without losing the room.

Tip: write down HOW he builds each argument (the data before the opinion). Bring two of his sentences that you would use in an executive presentation, and say each of them out loud in its inverted version.

Watch on YouTube ↗

Lesson 5 — Brazil vs. Canada -- Articulating Cultural Differences with Academic and Executive Teams

Series
The Chair — Season 1, Episode 1 (Netflix)

Ji-Yoon Kim takes over an academic department and finds out that formal authority buys nothing in a room of PhDs with twenty years in the building — people who disagree by email, three days later, never in the meeting. It is your August meeting room, filmed. Connection to Lesson 5: watch HOW the silence works in the department meetings — nobody contradicts anyone on the spot, and the disagreement lands later, in writing. That is power distance and a low-context culture in the same scene.

Tip: watch with English subtitles. Write down TWO lines where someone disagrees without raising their voice, and ONE where the silence in the room is clearly NOT agreement.

Watch ↗
Podcast
HBR IdeaCast — “How Authority and Decision-Making Differ Across Cultures” (Erin Meyer, INSEAD)

Erin Meyer, the author of The Culture Map, explains why hierarchy and decision-making are TWO different things — and why a foreign leader gets it wrong precisely by collapsing them into one. It is the theory behind power distance, in the executive English you will need to use it in. Connection to Lesson 5: it is exactly the argument of your matrix — a room can be egalitarian and still nobody contradicts the director; the silence is not agreement.

Tip: listen once, with no pauses. Then summarize it out loud in sixty seconds, opening with “What surprised me most was...” and using ONE it-cleft (“It was the... that...”) and ONE hedge (“More often than not...”). Record it and listen back.

Listen ↗
YouTube
Erin Meyer — “The Culture Map” (INSEAD)

The author herself presenting the eight scales of the culture map: communication (high-context vs. low-context), feedback, leadership, decision-making, trust, disagreement, time. It is the entire vocabulary of this lesson, spoken by the person who built it. Connection to Lesson 5: her scales are exactly the rows of your Cultural Comparison Matrix — and she shows that Brazil and Canada land on different points of almost all of them.

Tip: watch the first half. Pick THREE scales where Brazil and Canada sit far apart and write ONE sentence about each, with a hedge and a cleft, to bring to Lesson 6.

Watch on YouTube ↗

Lesson 6 — Diagnosing Culture -- Running a Cultural Assessment with the Canadian Team

Film
Moneyball (2011) — Netflix

Billy Beane trades the scouts’ opinion for an instrument of measurement — and then spends the whole film defending that instrument in front of a room that has already decided he is wrong. It is your September meeting, in baseball. Connection to Lesson 6: notice that the resistance is never against the numbers, but against WHO decided to use them. Beane calculates nothing himself: he has it calculated (causative), and he answers for it.

Tip: watch with English subtitles. Write down TWO lines where somebody mistakes a symptom for a root cause, and ONE where Beane defends the instrument without apologizing for the number.

Watch ↗
Podcast
WorkLife with Adam Grant — “How to Trust People You Don’t Like” (TED)

Adam Grant, an organizational psychologist, on how trust is built between people who disagree — astronauts from different countries locked inside a space station. It is the ground under the trust index you will measure in Canada, and the executive English used to talk about it. Connection to Lesson 6: trust is not warmth. It is predictability — which is why the single thing that moves your response rate most is saying, up front, what happens to the findings.

Tip: listen to five or seven minutes with no pauses. Then summarize it out loud in sixty seconds, using ONE causative (“They had the crew trained by...”) and ONE hedge (“The data would suggest that...”). Write down three expressions you had never heard before.

Listen ↗
YouTube
Amy Edmondson — “Building a psychologically safe workplace” (TEDxHGSE, 11 min)

The Harvard researcher who turned a concept into an INSTRUMENT: she explains how she measured hospital teams and found that the better teams reported MORE errors, not fewer. It is a lesson in how a counterintuitive finding is defended. Connection to Lesson 6: her number said the opposite of the obvious, and she did not soften the number — she went after the root cause. It is exactly what Claire asked for in the voice note.

Tip: watch it once, all the way through. Then write THREE sentences about your own diagnostic using her pattern: the finding, the hedge, and the root cause underneath — and bring them to Lesson 7.

Watch on YouTube ↗

Lesson 7 — Navigating Difficult Conversations -- Feedback, Accountability, and Conflict in a Canadian Context

Series
The Last Dance — Episode 7 (Netflix)

The episode where Michael Jordan is confronted with the question of this lesson: did my feedback build that team, or did it wound those people? Teammates describe a behavioral pattern; he answers, he defends himself, and at the end — in the closing scene, with the camera still running — he takes ownership. It is the difference between harshness and constructive feedback, on film. Connection to Lesson 7: watch his blind spot (he does not see what the whole room sees) and defensiveness as INFORMATION: every time he defends himself, he tells you something nobody had asked about.

Tip: watch with English subtitles. Write down THREE lines in which a teammate describes a pattern (not an incident), and ONE in which Jordan drops the defensiveness and starts to take ownership of his own style. Then report the scene in three sentences, one for each reporting verb: what he acknowledged, what he denied, what he agreed to.

Watch ↗
Podcast
WorkLife with Adam Grant — “How to Love Criticism” (TED)

Adam Grant walks into a company where criticizing your boss in public is an obligation, and takes apart why almost everyone confuses hard feedback with cruel feedback. It is the theory behind this lesson: why sugarcoating destroys the message, and why a culture that can take criticism needs psychological safety first. Connection to Lesson 7: this is your room in August — a team that has to hear something difficult from someone who has only just arrived.
