Tip: listen once, with no pauses. Then summarize it out loud in ninety seconds, using THREE reporting verbs with different patterns (“He points out that...” / “He recommends ...ing” / “He encourages leaders to...”). Record it and listen back.

Listen ↗
YouTube
LeeAnn Renninger — “The secret to giving great feedback” (TED, The Way We Work, 5 min)

Five minutes on the four-part formula that holds a difficult 1:1 together: the micro-yes, the specific data point, the impact, and the question that hands the floor back to the other person. It is the backbone of the conversation you are about to run with a resistant coordinator — and the answer to “how do I open without beating around the bush, and without wounding him?”. Connection to Lesson 7: her closing question is the exact moment when defensiveness turns into information.

Tip: watch it twice. On the second viewing, write your own thirty-second opening for the 1:1 with the coordinator, using her formula + address head-on + behavioral pattern. Bring it in writing to Lesson 8.

Watch on YouTube ↗

Lesson 8 — Midpoint Review -- Consolidating HR Leadership Language for Canada

Series
The Diplomat — Season 1, Episode 1 (Netflix)

Kate Wyler spends the whole series doing what you will do in November: walking into a room of senior people, with little time and INCOMPLETE information, and saying what she knows, what she does not know yet, and what she wants them to decide. This is Lesson 8, filmed. Connection to Lesson 8: watch how she separates what is preliminary from what is a conclusion, and how the caveat (“we think, we do not know”) is exactly what makes the room TRUST her — not the other way around.

Tip: watch with English subtitles. Write down THREE moments where somebody delivers information with a caveat (“as far as we know...”, “preliminary reports suggest...”) and ONE where somebody over-promises and pays for it later. Then rewrite the over-promiser's line with a caveat and a participle clause.

Watch ↗
Podcast
HBR IdeaCast (Harvard Business Review) — any episode on leadership and executive presentation

The corporate English your Canadian room speaks, undiluted: twenty to twenty-five minutes, two executives, zero teaching. It is the natural home of hedging — nobody on this podcast says “this will work”; they say “it would appear that”, “the evidence suggests”, “more often than not”. Connection to Lesson 8: it is Warren's exact register, and the exact vocabulary of your midpoint review (takeaway, trade-off, traction, quick win).

Tip: listen to five minutes of an episode and write down THREE hedging expressions the guest uses — and, next to each one, what the sentence would say WITHOUT the hedge. The gap between the two columns is the distance between sounding junior and sounding senior. Bring it to Lesson 9.

Listen ↗
YouTube
Patrick Winston — “How to Speak” (MIT, 60 min — watch the first 15)

The most famous MIT lecture on public speaking, and it opens with exactly the thesis of Lesson 8: start with the promise — what the room will take away — and NEVER with the methodology. It is Mariana Vidal's mistake, explained by an MIT professor fifteen years before you walk into that room. Connection to Lesson 8: his “empowerment promise” is your takeaway in the first sentence, before the first slide.

Tip: watch the first fifteen minutes with English subtitles. Then write YOUR own “empowerment promise” for the November review — ONE sentence, no number, no methodology — and record it out loud five times, until it comes out with no hesitation. It is the first sentence Warren will hear.

Watch on YouTube ↗

Lesson 9 — Diplomatic Pushback -- Disagreeing With People Who Know More Than You

Series
The Diplomat — Season 1, Episode 1 (Netflix)

Kate Wyler spends the whole series doing exactly what you will be doing in August: disagreeing, in English, with people who hold more power, more history and more information than she does — without ever capitulating and without ever fighting. Watch the pattern: she concedes out loud the part where the other person is right (“you are right about that”), and only then holds the part that is hers. Connection to Lesson 9: listen for the concessive structure throughout — While / Granted / That said — and notice that HER point always lands in the main clause, at the end of the sentence, which is the only one the room remembers.

Tip: watch with English subtitles. Write down THREE moments where Kate concedes a point and immediately holds her own. Then rewrite each one for your academic council: same structure, your content (the sample, the focus groups, the six weeks).

Watch ↗
Podcast
Hidden Brain — “Why Conversations Go Wrong” (with Deborah Tannen)

The linguist Deborah Tannen shows how two people stating the SAME facts can walk out of a room with opposite verdicts — and how a reasonable disagreement turns into a confrontation without anyone telling a single lie. It is the theory behind the most important slide of Lesson 9: “While the sample is small, the pattern is consistent” and “While the pattern is consistent, the sample is small” hold the same two facts and deliver contrary judgments. Connection to Lesson 9: what Tannen calls conversational style, grammar calls the main clause.

Tip: listen once, with no pauses. Then summarize it out loud in ninety seconds, using THREE different concessive structures (“While she argues that...” / “Granted... That said...” / “Even if we accept that... it still...”). Record it and listen back: did your concession come BEFORE your thesis, or after it?

Listen ↗
YouTube
Julia Dhar — “How to disagree productively and find common ground” (TED, 12 min)

A three-time world debate champion explains why the point of a well-run disagreement is NOT to win, and how you separate a criticism of the ARGUMENT from a criticism of the PERSON — which is literally the difference between taking issue with his premise and attacking Dr. Prescott. Connection to Lesson 9: she names the move this whole lesson trains, starting from the common ground, and shows why conceding the other side's true point STRENGTHENS your position instead of weakening it.

Tip: watch it twice. On the second pass, write your own thirty-second answer to Dr. Prescott’s line (“that is not a finding, that is a rumor”) using her three moves — concede, counter, propose — and the expressions to concede, to defer to and to stand your ground. Bring it in writing to Lesson 10.

Watch on YouTube ↗

Lesson 10 — People Analytics -- Making the Numbers Talk

Film
Moneyball (2011, Netflix)

Billy Beane rebuilds a baseball team on data that everyone else in the room dismisses as noise — and the whole film is the fight you will have at the Academic Council: a number-driven finding versus a room that trusts its gut. Watch how the analyst wins not by overclaiming, but by being precise about what the data can and cannot say. Connection to Lesson 10: notice every time someone confuses a correlation with a causation, and every time Beane refuses to read too much into a single game — the outlier — and holds the pattern instead.

Tip: watch with English subtitles. Write down THREE moments where a number changes a decision, and rewrite each one as a reporting passive for your own council (“It is estimated that...”, “is thought to be linked to...”).

Watch on Netflix ↗
Podcast
Freakonomics Radio — “How to Be Less Terrible at Predicting the Future”

The show that made a career out of the sentence “correlation is not causation” turns to the researchers who forecast honestly: the ones who state a probability, keep the margin of error visible, and refuse to overclaim. It is the exact register Dr. Prescott is testing you for. Connection to Lesson 10: listen for how the best forecasters attribute their claims to the evidence (“it is estimated that...”, “is thought to...”) instead of asserting them as fact — and how naming what they cannot know is what makes them trusted.

Tip: listen once with no pauses. Then summarize one idea out loud in ninety seconds, using TWO reporting passives and one data comparative (“twice as likely as”, “a threefold increase”). Record it and check: did you claim a cause, or report a link?

Listen ↗
YouTube
Hans Rosling — “The best stats you've ever seen” (TED, 20 min)

The definitive lesson in data storytelling: Rosling takes numbers that everyone assumes they understand and makes them say something true and surprising — without ever overclaiming what a single data point means. This is the skill of Lesson 10 in its purest form: turning a table into a story a room can act on. Connection to Lesson 10: watch how he separates a real signal from noise, teases out the one pattern that matters, and hands the audience a leading indicator rather than a verdict.

Tip: watch it twice. On the second pass, write your own thirty-second “numbers talk” about one finding from your work — state the correlation, name the confounding variable you could not control for, and end with one actionable insight. Bring it to Lesson 11.

Watch on YouTube ↗

Lesson 11 — The Turnaround Story -- Narrating What Had Already Changed

Film
Moneyball (2011) — official trailer & film

This is your board review as a movie. Billy Beane runs a turnaround that everyone else calls luck, and the whole film is an argument about causation versus coincidence — the exact question Marcus Reid asks you tomorrow. Watch how the story is TOLD: the season that had already been lost, the system he had already built before the wins came, the tipping point in the middle. Connection to Lesson 11: the wins at the end mean nothing without the sequence in the middle. That sequence is the past perfect — what had been failing, and what he had built before anything moved.

Tip: watch the trailer here, then find the full film on your streaming service. As you watch, write ONE turnaround sentence about Beane in the past perfect (“By the time they won twenty in a row, he had already...”). Then rewrite it about your own Brazil year.

Watch the trailer ↗
Podcast
Hidden Brain — “The Story of Your Life”

Shankar Vedantam and psychologist Dan McAdams show that the WAY you sequence your own history — not the facts of it — decides whether people hear growth or hear luck. It is the theory behind the most important idea in Lesson 11: the same three true events, told as a flat list or told as a build, deliver opposite verdicts. Connection to Lesson 11: what McAdams calls a redemption narrative, grammar calls the past perfect — the tense that puts the struggle BEFORE the recovery and makes the recovery earned.

Tip: listen once with no pauses. Then narrate your Brazil turnaround out loud in ninety seconds using THREE past-perfect structures (“By the time... had already...” / “had been climbing for...” / “long before the numbers moved, I had...”). Record it and check: did the backdrop come before the result, or after it?

Listen ↗
YouTube
Tim Harford — “Trial, error and the God complex” (TED, 18 min)

The economist Tim Harford explains why real progress almost never looks like a single confident decision, and why the honest version of a success story is a messy sequence of things that had already been tried and had already failed before anything worked. It is the intellectual permission to tell your turnaround as a build rather than a triumph. Connection to Lesson 11: Harford is arguing, in economics, exactly what the past perfect argues in grammar — that the credible story is the one that shows the groundwork and the failures that came before the result.

Tip: watch it twice. On the second pass, write your own two-minute answer to Marcus Reid’s question (“how do you know it was you, and not the market?”) using the past perfect for everything you had built, and the words groundwork, to trace back to and to reverse a trend. Bring it in writing to Lesson 12.

Watch on YouTube ↗

Lesson 12 — The First 90 Days -- Listening Before You Lead

Film
The Intern (2015, Netflix)

Ben, a seventy-year-old “senior intern”, joins a fast company where he has authority over nothing and knowledge of everything — and wins the room by listening long before he offers a single opinion. It is your first ninety days in reverse: no mandate, all credibility, earned one honest conversation at a time. Connection to Lesson 12: watch how he sizes up the culture before trying to move it, and how he leans on being useful rather than being right.

Tip: watch with English subtitles. Note THREE moments where he learns something by asking instead of telling, and rewrite each as reported speech (“She explained that...”, “He admitted that...”) for your own listening tour.

Watch on Netflix ↗
Podcast
HBR IdeaCast — “Taking on a Senior Leadership Role Remotely” (with Michael Watkins)

Michael Watkins, who wrote the book that named the idea, walks through what a new leader should actually do in the entry period — diagnose before deciding, secure early wins that build credibility, and avoid the trap of proving yourself by changing something fast — and does it for exactly your situation: taking the reins of a senior role at a distance, part remote, part in person. It is the strategy of Lesson 12 from the person who defined it. Connection to Lesson 12: listen for how he frames the ramp-up period as learning, not delay, and how the listening tour is the work, not a warm-up to it.

Tip: listen once with no pauses. Then, in ninety seconds, relay two ideas he raised using reported speech and two different reporting verbs (“He argued that...”, “He pointed out that...”). Record it and check your backshift.

Listen ↗
YouTube
Frances Frei — “How to build (and rebuild) trust” (TED, 15 min)

Frei ran the culture turnaround at Uber, and her whole talk is about the one currency a new leader has to earn from zero: trust — the everyday word for credibility. She shows why trying to prove yourself fast destroys it, and why authenticity, logic, and empathy build it back. This is the emotional engine of Lesson 12: you cannot spend a mandate you have not earned. Connection to Lesson 12: watch how she reframes the first weeks as listening and repair, not decisive action — exactly the discipline of the first ninety days.

Tip: watch it twice. On the second pass, write a thirty-second plan for your own first quarter — what you will listen for, whom you will lean on, and the one thing you will deliberately NOT change yet. Bring it to Lesson 13.

Watch on YouTube ↗

Lesson 13 — The Unwritten Rules -- Rapport and Etiquette in the Canadian Office

Series
Ted Lasso — Season 1, official trailer & series (Apple TV+)

This is your first month in Canada, played for comedy. An American coach lands inside a British football club and gets every unwritten rule wrong — he mistakes reserve for coldness, misses the understatement, and only wins the room once he stops resisting the culture and starts reading it. Watch how the newcomers who complain lose the room, and how Ted himself builds rapport through relentless small talk. Connection to Lesson 13: the whole show is the difference between “this place is wrong” and “I wish it worked differently — and here is what I am doing meanwhile”.

Tip: watch the trailer here, then find the full first episode on Apple TV+. As you watch, write TWO sentences with “I wish” from Ted’s point of view — one about the present (“I wish the team trusted me”) and one regret (“I wish I had understood the sarcasm sooner”).

Watch the trailer ↗
Podcast
WorkLife with Adam Grant — “Authenticity is a double-edged sword”

Organizational psychologist Adam Grant examines what happens when “just be yourself” collides with a culture that expects something different — exactly your dilemma in a Canadian office where directness reads as rude and silence reads as consent. He makes the case for adapting how you present without losing who you are: the professional version of code-switching. Connection to Lesson 13: it is the argument behind the whole lesson — you can wish the rules were different and still adapt to them, and that is not fakeness, it is leadership.

Tip: listen once with no pauses. Then record ninety seconds answering: “What is one unwritten rule of the Canadian office you wish you had understood sooner?” Use “if only” + past perfect at least once, and the words understatement, to read the room and reciprocity.

Listen ↗
YouTube
Pellegrino Riccardi — “Cross cultural communication” (TEDxBergen, 20 min)

A funny, precise talk on why every culture runs on invisible rules that feel like “just common sense” to the people inside it — and why a newcomer only sees them by breaking them. Riccardi lived the exact move you are about to make, and he explains directness, small talk, punctuality and formality as CHOICES a culture makes, not right-and-wrong. Connection to Lesson 13: it gives you the calm, non-judgmental frame for the whole lesson — the rules are not wrong, they are just not yours yet, and adapting to them is the work.

Tip: watch it twice. On the second pass, list three unwritten rules of the Canadian office and, for each, write the “wish + own + invite” reframe: name what you wish were different, what you are already doing about it, and how you would invite the other person — then bring it to Lesson 14.

Watch on YouTube ↗

Lesson 14 — The Coaching 1:1 -- Developing People, Not Managing Them

Series
The Bear — Season 1, official trailer & series (FX / Hulu)

A high-pressure kitchen is one long masterclass in the difference between managing people and developing them. Watch Carmy oscillate between barking orders that shrink his team and the rarer, harder moments where he actually coaches — asks a real question, names someone's growth edge, and empowers them to run their own station. The best 1:1 in the whole show is the one where he stops fixing and starts listening. Connection to Lesson 14: every scene is the choice between the command that gets compliance and the guidance that grows a person.

Tip: watch the trailer, then find Season 1 on Disney+. As you watch, note ONE moment where a character coaches instead of commands, and rewrite one of Carmy's orders as guidance — “I would rather...” or “you had better...” + the bare verb.

Watch the trailer ↗
Podcast
Coaching Real Leaders with Muriel Wilkins — HBR Podcast Network

This is the exact conversation you are learning to run, recorded live: an executive coach works through a real leadership dilemma with a real leader, in real time. Listen to how Muriel opens with a question and then stays quiet, how she reframes the person's story instead of solving it, and how she guides without ever handing over an order. It is a whole show built on the coaching mindset. Connection to Lesson 14: it models active listening, reframing, and warm-but-directive guidance — the register you will use in every 1:1 in Canada.

Tip: pick any one episode and listen once with no pauses. Then record ninety seconds answering: “What did the coach do INSTEAD of giving advice?” Use the words active listening, reframing and coachable moment, and one advanced modal.

Listen ↗
YouTube
Julian Treasure — “5 ways to listen better” (TED, 8 min)

The single skill that makes or breaks a coaching 1:1 is the one nobody teaches: listening. In eight minutes Julian Treasure explains why we are losing the ability to listen and gives five practical tools to get it back — including the discipline of staying with someone's silence instead of rushing to fill it. This is the mechanics behind “active listening” from the lesson. Connection to Lesson 14: it is the warm-up drill for the hardest part of any 1:1 — hearing the thing under the thing before you say a word.

Tip: watch it twice. On the second pass, pick two of Treasure's tools and write, in English, how you would use each one in your next mentoring 1:1. Then bring one to Lesson 15 — the full simulation.

Watch on YouTube ↗

Lesson 15 — Full Simulation -- Danielle Leads the Room (Final Review)

Film
The King's Speech — official trailer (2010)

The purest version of your Thursday: a leader who has the authority and the substance, but has to deliver it in a voice he is terrified will fail him in the room. Watch how the work is never about perfect fluency — it is about walking in as the person you already are and letting the words carry the truth. Connection to Lesson 15: the whole film is Ruth's instruction — reach for the true sentence, not the perfect one, and the room follows the leader, not the grammar.

Tip: watch the trailer, then find the full film. As you watch, note one moment where he stops performing and starts meaning what he says — and write two sentences about how you will do the same on Thursday, using a hedge-then-commit ("I'm not fully sure yet, but here is what I am sure of").

Watch the trailer ↗
Podcast
WorkLife with Adam Grant — TED Audio Collective

Organizational psychologist Adam Grant takes you inside how the best leaders actually think, argue, and change their minds — the exact register you have been building: disagreeing without damaging the relationship, holding your ground while staying open, and sounding senior by being honest about what you don't yet know. Connection to Lesson 15: it is a season of masterclasses in the hedge-then-commit move — confident about the thinking, humble about the certainty.

Tip: pick any one episode and listen with no pauses. Then record ninety seconds answering, in your own words: "What does success look like for me, personally, in the Canadian role?" Use one hedge ("I'm not fully sure yet"), then commit to the true part, and land it with a cleft ("What matters most is...").

Listen ↗
YouTube
Julian Treasure — "How to speak so that people want to listen" (TED, 10 min)

A precise, practical talk on the mechanics of a voice people actually follow — register, pace, silence, and speaking from honesty rather than performance. It is the technical companion to everything you did tonight: not what you say, but how you land it in a room that is deciding whether to trust you. Connection to Lesson 15: it turns "sound like a leader" from an instinct into a set of choices you can make on Thursday morning.

Tip: watch it twice. On the second pass, pick two techniques (pace and silence are the easiest to steal) and rehearse your meeting opening — "Let me frame my mandate..." — applying them out loud until the delivery matches the content.

Watch on YouTube ↗
